-  [ ]  Plan connection work as critical path: module assembly (1–2 weeks) is fast; connections and sealing (2–4 weeks) are where delays happen. Ensure experienced connector crews are scheduled weeks in advance
-  [ ]  Implement just-in-time module delivery if site has constrained staging area: sequence deliveries by floor and position rather than bulk delivery

**Tariff & Trade Risk (Months 6–18):**

-  [ ]  Model tariff scenarios if sourcing modules from China: baseline 7.5% tariff (Section 301) vs. worst case 25% (if escalation occurs). On 160 modules at $40K each, 25% tariff = $160K cost swing
-  [ ]  Establish customs broker relationship early; confirm HS code classification (9406: prefabricated buildings) and applicable tariffs before committing to import schedule

:::

\ornsep

## Overview

Modular construction manufactures building components in a **controlled factory environment**, then ships and assembles them on-site. For hotel and multifamily projects with repetitive unit layouts, modular can deliver **25–50% schedule savings** with improved quality control and reduced site disruption.

\ornsep

## Modular Typology

| Type | Description | Schedule Savings |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Volumetric** | Complete 3D rooms; shipped and stacked | 25–50% faster |
| **Panelized** | 2D panels assembled on-site | 15–25% faster |
| **Hybrid** | Modular rooms on conventional podium | 20–40% faster |
| **Bathroom Pods** | Pre-assembled baths in conventional frame | Reduces MEP 30–40% |

### Selecting the Right Approach

- **Volumetric**: Best for hotels and student housing with highly repetitive rooms
- **Panelized**: Best for residential with varied floor plans
- **Hybrid**: Most common — concrete podium (parking, lobby, amenities) with modular rooms above
- **Bathroom Pods**: Lowest risk entry point; works within conventional construction

\ornsep

## Structural Systems

| System | Height Limit | Seismic Performance | Best For |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **CFS Frame** | 5–8 stories | Good with bracing | Mid-rise; most cost-effective |
| **Hot-Rolled Steel** | No limit | Excellent | High-rise modular |
| **Concrete Podium + Modular** | 5–6 over 1–4 concrete | Concrete absorbs lateral | Most common hybrid |
| **CLT / Mass Timber** | Up to 18 stories | Emerging | Sustainability premium |

### California Seismic Considerations

Modular in California's high-seismic zones requires **dual-performance engineering**:
- Modules must be **stiff enough** to protect interiors during shipping
- Modules must be **flexible enough** for seismic loads when stacked
- Connection design is the critical engineering challenge
- Concrete shear cores and buckling-restrained brace (BRB) frames are proven solutions

\ornsep

## Transport Constraints

| Constraint | California Standard |
| --- | --- |
| **Road width** | 14 ft standard, 16 ft with wide-load permit |
| **Road height** | 14 ft maximum |
| **Length** | 60–75 ft with permit |
| **Weight** | ~40,000–45,000 lbs per module (road) |
| **Ocean container** | 44,000 lbs maximum |

### Module Sizing Examples

| Brand/System | Module Dimensions | Configuration |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **citizenM** | 48 ft x 8 ft x 9 ft | 2 rooms + corridor section |
| **Marriott** | 12–14 ft wide x 30–60 ft long | 1 room per module |

> Transport constraints drive module design. Design for the most restrictive segment of the delivery route, including bridge heights, turning radii, and site access.

\ornsep

## Factory vs. Site Work Split

| Work Category | Factory (%) | Site (%) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Structure** | 80–90% | 10–20% |
| **MEP rough-in** | 85–95% | 5–15% |
| **Interior finishes** | 90–100% | 0–10% |
| **Facade** | 50–80% | 20–50% |
| **Site work/foundations** | 0% | 100% |
| **Connections/sealing** | 0% | 100% |

\ornsep

## Quality Control Advantages

| Factor | Conventional | Modular |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Weather exposure** | Full duration | Foundation only |
| **Inspection access** | Scaffolding required | Factory floor level |
| **Material storage** | Open-air staging | Climate-controlled warehouse |
| **Waste generation** | Baseline | 30–40% less |
| **Punch list items** | Standard | 50–70% fewer |
| **Change orders** | 3–5% of contract | Less than 1% |

\ornsep

## Permitting Considerations

### State vs. Local Jurisdiction

- Modular units built in-state: inspected by **HCD** (Housing and Community Development) or **DSA** (Division of the State Architect)
- Modular units from out-of-state: must carry **insignia of approval** from originating state or third-party inspection agency
- Site work (foundation, connections, utilities): inspected by **local building department**
- Dual jurisdiction creates coordination challenges — engage both early

### California-Specific Requirements

- CBC (California Building Code) seismic provisions apply regardless of manufacturing location
- Fire-rated assemblies must be tested and listed per CBC
- Energy code (Title 24) compliance documentation required from factory
- Accessibility (CBC Chapter 11B) applies to assembled building

\ornsep

## Bay Area Case Studies & Lessons Learned

### Case 1: citizenM Menlo Park — 160+ Modules in 2 Months

The citizenM Menlo Park hotel assembled **160+ volumetric modules in approximately 2 months** — roughly 4 modules per day at peak. The modules were manufactured by CIMC in China, shipped via ocean freight to the Port of Oakland, trucked to the Menlo Park site, and crane-set onto a concrete podium.

Key success factors: (1) flat, accessible site with ample staging area on the Meta campus, (2) dedicated tower crane with sufficient reach, (3) just-in-time module delivery sequenced by floor and position, (4) experienced crane operators and connector crews, (5) pre-installed MEP within each module requiring only riser connections on-site.

**Lesson Learned**: Module assembly speed is achievable in California, but requires meticulous logistics planning. The Menlo Park site's advantages (large staging area, controlled campus access, no adjacent residential neighbors) are atypical. For constrained urban sites, expect 2 modules per day rather than 4, and budget for off-site staging if the site cannot accommodate module storage.

\ornsep

### Case 2: Bay Area Seismic — The Connection Engineering Challenge

A mid-rise modular project in San Jose encountered issues during structural peer review when the independent reviewer questioned whether the module-to-module connections had been adequately tested for the site-specific seismic demand. The original connection design (bolted steel brackets) had been tested for Seismic Design Category D, but the site was classified as SDC E due to soft soil conditions.

The redesign — from bolted brackets to Hollo-Bolt countersunk connections with supplemental BRB frames — added $800K to the structural budget and delayed factory production by 3 months while new connection details were engineered and tested.

**Lesson Learned**: In the Bay Area, do not finalize connection design before receiving the geotechnical report and site-specific seismic demand parameters. Seismic Design Category varies significantly by soil condition — a site 2 miles away may have a different SDC. Engage the structural engineer and peer reviewer **before** ordering modules, not after.

\ornsep

### Case 3: Port of Oakland — Modular Import Logistics

Developers importing modules from China to the Bay Area route through the **Port of Oakland**. Key logistics considerations:

- **Customs clearance**: Modules are classified as prefabricated buildings (HS code 9406), subject to applicable tariffs
- **Port drayage**: Heavy haul from Port of Oakland to Peninsula sites requires oversize load permits (Caltrans + local jurisdictions)
- **Route survey**: US-101 has height restrictions at several overpasses; I-280 may be preferred for Peninsula destinations
- **Night hauling**: Most Bay Area jurisdictions require oversize loads to move between 9 PM and 6 AM
- **Staging**: If the site cannot receive modules immediately, temporary storage yards in the East Bay (Hayward, Fremont) cost $0.50–$1.00/SF/month

**Lesson Learned**: Ocean shipping from China to Oakland is cost-effective (~$4K–$6K per container-sized module), but the "last mile" from port to site is where logistics complexity lives. Survey the route, secure all permits, and have contingency staging before modules arrive. A module sitting at the port incurs $300–$500/day in demurrage.

\ornsep

## Frontier Developer Profiles

### Aro Homes — Volumetric Prefab by Olson Kundig

**Aro Homes** (aro.homes) is a Bay Area startup that has partnered with world-renowned architecture firm **Olson Kundig** to deliver precision-engineered, carbon-negative volumetric prefab homes at scale.

| Metric | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **HQ** | Bay Area, California |
| **Factory** | Sacramento, CA |
| **Prototype** | 3,000 SF, 4BR — Mountain View, CA |
| **First Sale Price** | $4.2M |
| **Construction Timeline** | 90 days on-site assembly vs. 18 months conventional |
| **Energy Performance** | Net-zero capable; 50% more efficient than conventional; 11.2 tons CO2 saved/year |
| **Construction Method** | Volumetric modules manufactured in Sacramento factory, trucked to site |

**What Makes Aro Different**: Unlike most prefab companies that compromise on design for efficiency, Aro inverts the model — world-class architecture first (Olson Kundig), then engineered for factory production. The result is a home that looks and feels custom but delivers with prefab speed and precision. Their hybrid off-site/on-site strategy compresses the delivery timeline from 18 months to approximately 90 days.

**Relevance to Developers**: Aro demonstrates that volumetric prefab can work at the **luxury price point** ($1,400/SF) in Silicon Valley. This disproves the assumption that modular = budget. For developers targeting high-end infill lots, Aro's model shows that factory precision + premium design can command premium pricing while delivering faster.

> **Deep Dive**: For the full McKinsey-style case study on Aro — including their 90-day delivery model, Olson Kundig partnership, process parallelization strategy, and the competitive analysis against conventional luxury builders — see **Appendix F, §F.2**.

\ornsep

### Livio Building Systems — CFS Panelized + AI Factory

**Livio Building Systems** (golivio.com) is a BuildTech company headquartered in **Los Altos, CA** that has developed a proprietary Multi-Trade Building System using cold-formed steel (CFS) panelized construction.

| Metric | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **HQ** | Los Altos, California |
| **Bay Area Team** | ~30 people |
| **Manufacturing Team** | 200+ in Pune, India |
| **Speed Claim** | 5-story building erected in 8.5 hours |
| **Labor Reduction** | 75% reduction in on-site labor hours |
| **Cost Savings** | ~50% savings in labor cost |
| **Margin Profile** | 75%+ gross margins on panelized steel framing products |
| **Founded** | 2017 |

**What Makes Livio Different**: Livio has pivoted from a service-oriented construction company to a **product-based business** — selling engineered CFS panel systems rather than general contracting services. Their panels are designed and engineered using AI-driven tools, manufactured with precision in India, and shipped to California sites where they can be assembled with minimal skilled labor. The "HomeKit" product allows homeowners to work with their own architect while Livio engineers, permits, and delivers the structural system.

**Relevance to Developers**: Livio's model addresses the two biggest constraints in California construction: **labor scarcity and labor cost**. By moving 75% of labor hours off-site and into a factory (with significantly lower labor costs in India), the system dramatically changes project economics. For developers building ADUs, small multifamily, or hotel projects with CFS framing, Livio represents an emerging supply chain option that bypasses domestic labor bottlenecks.

> **Deep Dive**: For the full case study on Livio — including their pivot from service to product business, 75%+ gross margins on panelized systems, AI-driven engineering platform, and the strategic implications of offshore manufacturing — see **Appendix F, §F.3**.

\ornsep

## Practitioner Checklist

-  [ ]  Select modular typology based on project type and repetition level
-  [ ]  Evaluate structural system for target height and seismic zone
-  [ ]  Survey transport route for clearance constraints
-  [ ]  Confirm factory capacity and production timeline
-  [ ]  Coordinate dual-jurisdiction permitting (HCD/DSA + local)
-  [ ]  Design connections for California seismic requirements
-  [ ]  Plan crane logistics for module setting (access, capacity, duration)
-  [ ]  Budget for module storage if site is not ready for immediate setting

\ornsep

## Financial Decision Gateway

Modular construction directly impacts the pro forma through schedule compression and the Death Point calculation:

| Modular Variable | Conventional | Modular | App G Connection |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Schedule** | 18–21 months (120-key hotel) | 12–15 months | Level 2: Death Point (§2D) pushed out 3–6 months |
| **Carrying cost savings** | Baseline | $200K–$400K less interest | Level 2: direct bottom-line improvement |
| **Change orders** | 3–5% of contract | Less than 1% | Level 3: Stress Test — cost variable narrows |
| **Cost savings** | Baseline | 10–20% reduction | Level 2: Sources & Uses — construction cost |
| **Transport/logistics** | N/A | $4K–$6K per module (ocean) + last-mile | Level 2: new line item not in conventional pro forma |
| **Tariff risk (Section 301)** | N/A | 7.5–25% on China-sourced modules | Level 3: Kill Criteria #11 — model worst-case scenario |

> **Death Point Insight** (Appendix G, §2D): The single most powerful financial benefit of modular is **Death Point compression**. On a 120-key hotel with $120K/month carrying cost, 4 months of schedule savings = $480K less holding cost — often the difference between a project that survives and one that dies. Run the Death Point calculation for both modular and conventional scenarios.

> **Frontier Builder Lesson**: Appendix F's five case studies (§F.1–F.5) map the full spectrum from capital-light models (TJH) to fully vertically integrated manufacturing (Veev, Katerra). The central lesson: **process parallelization beats vertical integration**. Choose the modular approach that matches your capital structure, not the one that requires the most capital. See the two post-mortems (Veev §F.4, Katerra §F.5) for critical lessons on when vertical integration destroys value.

\ornsep

## Key Takeaway

> Modular construction delivers its greatest value in projects with **high unit repetition** (hotels, student housing, multifamily). The hybrid approach — concrete podium with modular rooms above — is the most proven model in California's seismic environment. Transport logistics and connection engineering are the two areas where projects most commonly underperform.

*Next: Chapter 11 applies these modular fundamentals to Marriott's pre-approved prototypes — the most established branded modular program in North America, with select-service brands purpose-built for modular delivery. -> Chapter 11: Marriott Modular Program*


# Marriott Modular Program

**Statutory Reference**: Health & Safety Code §19960+ (Factory-Built Housing); California Building Code Title 24; 16 CFR Part 436 (FTC Franchise Rule/FDD)

\ornsep

## Executive Summary, Key Takeaways, and Action Checklist

\ornsep

::: execsummary

Marriott International has been the **most aggressive major hotel brand in embracing modular construction**, offering pre-approved volumetric and panelized prototypes, vetted supplier networks, and key money incentives (up to $20K per key in supply-constrained markets) for developers combining CFS expertise with Asian supply chain connections. This chapter decodes the Marriott modular ecosystem: six pre-approved brands optimized for modular delivery (Fairfield Inn: 80–120 keys, highly standardized; Courtyard: 120–180 keys, select-service anchor; SpringHill Suites: 100–150 keys, larger rooms; TownePlace Suites: 90–130 keys, extended-stay; AC Hotels: 150–200 keys, compact lifestyle; Moxy: 150–250 keys, smallest rooms), three approved suppliers (Guerdon Modular: Boise ID, CFS and steel; Champion Commercial Structures: Detroit MI; Oldcastle Surepods: bathroom pods only). Three California case studies validate the model: Fairfield Folsom (97 keys, 4-story CFS, 14-week factory timeline, 3-week module setting, 6-month schedule savings vs. conventional, 3% cost within conventional bid); Courtyard Hawthorne (354 keys, 6-story CFS, largest Marriott modular in US, proved scale viability); Peninsula market gap (no Marriott modular hotel built Peninsula to date—first-mover advantage for franchise-friendly jurisdiction like Redwood City or Sunnyvale). Financial impact: 120-key hotel gains $600K–$900K additional revenue from 3-month earlier opening, $200K–$400K reduced loan interest, $800K–$1.3M total schedule value. **Core principle**: Marriott's modular program is the lowest-risk path to modular hotel development in California. Pre-approved prototypes, established suppliers, and financial incentives remove technology risk. The competitive advantage lies in CFS expertise, Asian supply chain relationships, and Bay Area seismic proficiency.

:::

\ornsep

::: keytakeaways

- **Six Marriott brands pre-approved for modular with ready-to-deploy prototypes**: Fairfield Inn (most standardized, highest factory efficiency), Courtyard (largest select-service brand, proven modular track record), AC Hotels (compact, lifestyle-oriented), Moxy (smallest rooms, best modular fit). These brands have modular-specific construction documents already engineered. You're not designing from scratch; you're adapting a proven prototype to your site and local code.

- **Fairfield Folsom case study (97 keys, 4 stories, CFS framing) is California's modular template: 14-week factory, 3-week module setting, 6-month schedule savings, 3% cost within conventional bid**: This is the closest reference project to Bay Area mid-rise modular. Key lesson: CFS is cost-effective for SDC D sites like Folsom, but Bay Area SDC D–E sites may require upgraded connections (countersunk Hollo-Bolts, BRB frames), adding 5–10% cost premium. Use Folsom as baseline; budget 5–10% seismic uplift for Peninsula sites.

- **Guerdon Modular (Boise ID) is closest approved Marriott supplier to California; capacity is limited; 6–9 month lead times typical**: If planning 150+ key Marriott modular hotel, engage Guerdon early—Bay Area projects compete with western US projects for factory slots. For projects >200 keys, consider splitting production between Guerdon and secondary supplier (Champion Detroit), or sourcing CFS components from Asian fabricators to build modules domestically.

- **Key money up to $20K per key in supply-constrained markets reduces equity requirement significantly**: On 120-key hotel, $10K/key key money = $1.2M forgivable loan reducing equity. Marriott most aggressive with key money for AC Hotels and Moxy in Peninsula markets where they want lifestyle brand presence. Negotiate key money at franchise LOI stage; availability varies 10–20x by brand, location, and competitive dynamics.

- **Courtyard Hawthorne (354 keys, 6 stories, largest Marriott modular in US) proved scale viability but revealed capacity constraints**: Hawthorne required precise module sequencing, dedicated 6-story staging area, and additional steel bracing at lower floors to handle lateral loads from upper modules. This is the upper bound of CFS modular scalability. Lesson: for 200+ key projects, split production or explore hot-rolled steel alternatives.

- **Modular schedule savings ($600K–$900K additional revenue from 3-month opening acceleration + $200K–$400K reduced loan interest = $800K–$1.3M total value) is the primary financial driver of Marriott modular ROI**: This is NOT about construction cost savings (modular within 3% of conventional cost). It's about Death Point compression. Model this explicitly in Appendix G Level 2: pro forma with modular timeline vs. conventional timeline; the revenue and interest savings drive the decision, not materials or labor.

- **Peninsula market gap: no Marriott modular hotel built to date (citizenM Menlo Park used their own volumetric system, not Marriott)—first-mover advantage for franchise-friendly jurisdiction**: Being first carries risk but creates reference project that unlocks future deals. Position pilot in franchise-permissive municipality (Redwood City, San Mateo, Sunnyvale) rather than restrictive (Palo Alto). Success on Peninsula modular project generates $5K–$10K/key key money incentive for next 2–3 Peninsula projects.

- **CFS-based modular requires Marriott technical services inspections at key milestones: factory progress, module delivery, on-site connections, pre-opening systems testing**: Marriott's pre-opening protocol is stricter than conventional hotels. Plan 4–6 week pre-opening period for brand training, operating systems installation (PMS/Bonvoy integration), and brand sign-off. Shortcutting brand protocols delays opening.

:::

\ornsep

::: checklist

**Brand Selection & Market Analysis (Months 1–3):**

-  [ ]  Analyze Peninsula market demand by brand tier (is Courtyard already saturated? Is there white space for AC Hotel or Moxy?)—use Marriott's market analysis tools or third-party STR data
-  [ ]  Identify target brand (Fairfield, Courtyard, AC, Moxy) based on market positioning and modular fit—Moxy and AC highest modular priority; Fairfield most standardized
-  [ ]  Assess key money availability for target brand and market: Marriott most aggressive for AC and Moxy in Peninsula; key money for Courtyard typically lower. Negotiate at LOI stage.

**Franchise Application & LOI (Months 3–6):**

-  [ ]  Schedule meeting with Marriott development team; present modular-specific pitch with reference to Fairfield Folsom (97 keys) and Courtyard Hawthorne (354 keys) case studies
-  [ ]  Request Marriott pre-approved modular prototype documentation (schematic design, typical floor plan, MEP riser details, structural criteria)—this forms the design development baseline
-  [ ]  Negotiate franchise LOI with **minimum 36-month term** and key money incentive—typical structure: forgivable loan amortized over franchise term. Validate LOI expiration is 90+ days *after* entitlement completion.
-  [ ]  Confirm Marriott's modular supplier approval (Guerdon, Champion); if using alternative supplier, obtain Marriott technical approval *before* design development

**Structural & Seismic Engineering (Months 4–8):**

-  [ ]  Obtain geotechnical report and site-specific Seismic Design Category—if SDC E, budget upgrade from CFS to hot-rolled steel or concrete podium system (5–10% cost premium)
-  [ ]  Engage structural engineer experienced with modular construction; review Fairfield Folsom structural system (CFS frame, steel strap bracing, light-gauge studs 16", 16" OC) as baseline
-  [ ]  For Bay Area sites: confirm Hollo-Bolt countersunk connections (not standard bolted brackets) if SDC E—connection engineering must be complete *before* ordering modules
-  [ ]  Conduct peer review of connection design; ensure dual-performance engineering (stiff for shipping, flexible for seismic). Budget $100K–$300K for seismic-upgraded connections vs. Folsom baseline.

**Permitting & Technical Coordination (Months 6–12):**

-  [ ]  Coordinate with Marriott technical services during design development—brand approves prototype adaptation, MEP specifications, FF&E standards before permit submission
-  [ ]  Engage both HCD (state modular inspection) and local building department simultaneously—establish dual-jurisdiction coordination protocol with single points of contact
-  [ ]  Confirm local municipality has experience permitting modular construction; if not, educate planning/building departments early using Folsom and Hawthorne case studies
-  [ ]  Secure building permit; submit dual applications (local + HCD/DSA) for factory-built modules; timeline typically 4–12 weeks depending on local jurisdiction

**Modular Supplier & Production (Months 8–15):**

-  [ ]  Engage Guerdon Modular early if capacity is available; confirm 6–9 month lead time and factory production schedule aligned with site preparation
-  [ ]  If Guerdon capacity unavailable, negotiate Champion Commercial Structures (Detroit) or alternative supplier with Marriott technical approval
-  [ ]  Establish design-builder relationship: supplier becomes partner in design development, not post-design commodity. Supplier input on constructability, logistics, quality control is critical.
-  [ ]  Order CFS components and long-lead items (windows, doors, FF&E) at contract signing; production typically 12–16 weeks from order to module ready for shipment

**Logistics & Site Preparation (Months 12–18):**

-  [ ]  Plan module import from factory: if Guerdon (Boise), 2–3 week trucking to Peninsula. If Champion (Detroit), 4–5 weeks. If Asian CFS sourced, 6–8 weeks via Port of Oakland
-  [ ]  Ensure site foundation and utilities ready 30–45 days before first module arrival—staged foundation completion aligns with factory production ramp
-  [ ]  Arrange tower crane: dedicated crane for 3–4 week assembly period. Typical hotels set 2–4 modules/day at peak rate. Budget crane time and crane crew: $50K–$150K for assembly period
-  [ ]  Secure module staging area on-site or identify temporary storage yards (East Bay: $0.50–$1.00 SF/month); modules cannot stage >45 days without carrying cost impact

**Pre-Opening & Brand Integration (Months 18–24):**

-  [ ]  Implement Marriott Bonvoy PMS integration and loyalty program; this must be operational before opening to capture day-one reservations
-  [ ]  Schedule Marriott technical services inspections at factory (pre-shipment), on-site (post-assembly), and pre-opening (final approval). Plan 4–6 week pre-opening period for brand training
-  [ ]  Conduct staff training: front desk, housekeeping, revenue management, Bonvoy operations. Marriott training coordinator provides curriculum; typically 4–8 weeks pre-opening
-  [ ]  Coordinate FF&E delivery and installation: brand-specified furniture, fixtures, art, signage, linens must be ordered 4–6 months before opening; non-compliance delays opening

**Financial Modeling & Risk Management:**

-  [ ]  Model Death Point (Appendix G §2D) with modular timeline: 12–15 months construction (vs. 18–21 months conventional) at $120K/month carrying cost = $480K–$720K value from schedule compression
-  [ ]  Calculate early revenue: 3-month opening acceleration at $150–$200 RevPAR on 120 keys = $600K–$900K additional gross room revenue. This is the primary modular ROI driver.
-  [ ]  Evaluate key money credit in pro forma (Appendix G §2A, Sources section): $10K–$20K/key reduces equity requirement dollar-for-dollar. On $1.2M key money, leverage improves by 20%+ depending on project capital structure.
-  [ ]  Stress test tariff scenarios if sourcing CFS from China: baseline 7.5% tariff vs. worst-case 25%; on $40K/module cost, 25% tariff = $160K+ swing on 160 modules. Model sensitivity in Appendix G §3.

**Competitive Positioning & Exit:**

-  [ ]  Position as "first Marriott modular hotel on Peninsula"—this creates marketing advantage and reference project credibility for next 2–3 Peninsula modular deals
-  [ ]  Plan long-term: franchise term typically 15–25 years; evaluate transfer provisions for exit strategy. Modular construction may increase resale value (newer building stock, lower maintenance) or decrease (perceived as "prefab" by some buyers).
-  [ ]  Benchmark against conventional Marriott on Peninsula: schedule advantage, cost within 3%, schedule value $800K–$1.3M, revenue uplift $600K–$900K. Use these metrics in franchise application and investor communications.

:::

\ornsep

## Overview

Marriott International has been the **most aggressive major hotel brand** in embracing modular construction. They offer pre-approved prototypes, approved suppliers, and key money incentives for qualifying modular projects. For developers with CFS expertise and Asian supply chain connections, Marriott modular represents a differentiated market opportunity.

\ornsep

## Pre-Approved Brands for Modular

| Brand | Typical Keys | Modular Fit | Prototype Available |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Fairfield Inn** | 80–120 | Excellent — highly standardized | Yes |
| **Courtyard** | 120–180 | Excellent — select-service | Yes |
| **SpringHill Suites** | 100–150 | Good — larger rooms | Yes |
| **TownePlace Suites** | 90–130 | Good — extended stay | Yes |
| **AC Hotels** | 150–200 | Excellent — compact lifestyle | Yes |
| **Moxy Hotels** | 150–250 | Excellent — smallest rooms | Yes |

### Best Candidates for Modular

- **Moxy** and **AC Hotels**: Compact, lifestyle-oriented rooms with high repetition
- **Fairfield Inn**: Most standardized; highest factory efficiency
- **Courtyard**: Largest select-service brand; proven modular track record

\ornsep

## Performance Metrics: Modular vs. Conventional

| Metric | Conventional | Modular | Delta |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Schedule** (120-key) | 18–21 months | 12–15 months | -3 to -6 months |
| **Site Labor** | Baseline | 30–50% fewer workers | Safety improvement |
| **Change Orders** | 3–5% of contract | Less than 1% | Cost predictability |
| **Punch List** | Standard | 50–70% fewer | Faster occupancy |
| **Waste** | Baseline | 30–40% less | Sustainability |

### Financial Impact of Schedule Savings

For a 120-key select-service hotel:
- **3-month earlier opening** = ~$600K–$900K additional revenue (at $150–$200 RevPAR)
- **Reduced construction loan interest** = $200K–$400K savings
- **Earlier stabilization** = improved refinancing terms
- **Total benefit**: $800K–$1.3M in value creation from schedule acceleration alone

\ornsep

| Supplier | Location | Specialization |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Guerdon Modular Buildings** | Boise, ID | Full volumetric modules; CFS and steel |
| **Champion Commercial Structures** | Detroit, MI | Large-scale modular production |
| **Oldcastle Surepods** | Orange County, FL | Bathroom pods (not full modules) |

### Key Money Incentives

Marriott offers key money incentives for qualifying modular projects in target markets. Key money is structured as a forgivable loan amortized over the franchise term. Incentives are strongest for:
- Markets where Marriott wants to establish or grow presence
- Projects that demonstrate modular cost/schedule advantages
- Developers with proven track records

\ornsep

## CFS + Marriott Strategy

### Value Proposition

The combination of:
- **CFS structural expertise**
- **Chinese steel fabrication supply chain**
- **Marriott's modular program and prototypes**

...creates a differentiated position in the market.

### Execution Path

1. **Select target brand** (Fairfield, Courtyard, or Moxy based on market)
2. **Engage Marriott development team** with modular-specific pitch
3. **Secure franchise approval** with modular construction plan
4. **Source CFS components** from established Asian fabricators
5. **Partner with or become** the modular manufacturer
6. **Deliver pilot project** to prove the model

\ornsep

## Construction Document Workflow

| Phase | Marriott Role | Developer Role |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Schematic Design** | Provide prototype package | Adapt to site and local code |
| **Design Development** | Review for brand compliance | Coordinate with modular manufacturer |
| **Construction Documents** | Approve final documents | Submit for building permit |
| **Factory Production** | Technical services inspections | Quality oversight, logistics planning |
| **Site Assembly** | Milestone inspections | General contractor management |
| **Pre-Opening** | Operating systems, training | FF&E installation, staffing |

\ornsep

## Bay Area Case Studies & Lessons Learned

### Case 1: Fairfield Inn Folsom — California's CFS Modular Template

The **Fairfield Inn Folsom** (97 keys, 4 stories) is the most relevant California Marriott modular case study. Built using CFS-framed modules manufactured by Guerdon Modular in Boise, Idaho, it sits on a conventional concrete podium with surface parking.

Key metrics: (1) modules fabricated in 14 weeks, (2) site work (foundation, utilities) proceeded in parallel with factory production, (3) module setting completed in 3 weeks, (4) total construction schedule: 14 months vs. 20 months conventional = 6-month savings, (5) construction cost was within 3% of conventional bid.

The project's CFS structural system used light-gauge steel studs (16-gauge, 16" OC) with steel strap bracing for lateral loads. The Folsom site is Seismic Design Category D — lower than Bay Area sites, which may be SDC D or E.

**Lesson Learned**: Fairfield Folsom validates CFS modular for California, but with a caveat: the seismic demand in Folsom (SDC D) is lower than most Bay Area sites (SDC D–E). Adapting this template for Peninsula sites requires upgraded connections and potentially heavier gauge studs. The cost premium for Bay Area seismic compliance may be 5–10% above the Folsom baseline.

\ornsep

### Case 2: Courtyard by Marriott Hawthorne — Scale Proof Point

The **Courtyard Hawthorne** (354 keys, 6 stories) near LAX is the largest Marriott modular hotel completed in the US. Also built by Guerdon Modular, this project demonstrated that modular scales beyond boutique-sized projects.

Key lessons for Bay Area developers: (1) 354 modules required precise sequencing and dedicated staging area, (2) the 6-story height pushed CFS capacity limits — additional steel bracing was required at lower floors, (3) Guerdon's Boise factory is the closest approved Marriott modular supplier to California, but production capacity is limited, (4) lead times of 6–9 months from order to delivery should be budgeted.

**Lesson Learned**: If you're planning a 150+ key Marriott modular hotel in the Bay Area, engage Guerdon early — their capacity is limited, and Bay Area projects compete with projects across the western US for factory slots. For projects over 200 keys, consider splitting production between Guerdon and a secondary supplier, or exploring Asian-sourced CFS modules.

\ornsep

### Case 3: Peninsula Market Gap — Modular Opportunity

The San Mateo County / Santa Clara County hotel market has limited modular supply. Most recent hotel construction on the Peninsula has been conventional — including the Hotel Indigo San Carlos (188 keys, conventional construction) and multiple Marriott-branded renovations. No Marriott modular hotel has been built on the Peninsula to date.

This represents a first-mover opportunity: a developer who can deliver a Marriott modular hotel on the Peninsula — demonstrating Bay Area seismic compliance, local permitting coordination, and schedule/cost advantages — would have a compelling pitch for subsequent franchise approvals in the region.

**Lesson Learned**: The Bay Area Peninsula is underserved for modular hotel development. While citizenM has proven modular works here (Menlo Park), no major franchise brand has built modular on the Peninsula. Being first carries risk but also creates a reference project that unlocks future deals. Position your pilot project in a franchise-friendly jurisdiction (Redwood City, San Mateo, or Sunnyvale) rather than a restrictive one (Palo Alto).

\ornsep

The AI-Agile framework (§F.10) shows how modular's factory-controlled environment is uniquely suited to AI-powered quality control (OpenSpace) and schedule optimization (Alice Technologies, Ch 15 §15.10).

\ornsep

## Key Takeaway

> Marriott's modular program offers **pre-approved prototypes, established suppliers, and financial incentives** that significantly reduce development risk. The select-service brands (Fairfield, Courtyard, Moxy, AC) are purpose-built for modular delivery. Combined with CFS expertise and Asian supply chain access, this creates a compelling competitive advantage.

*Next: Chapter 12 examines citizenM's fully integrated volumetric system — the most proven modular hotel model globally, with two California projects demonstrating seismic compliance and rapid assembly. -> Chapter 12: citizenM Volumetric System*


# citizenM Volumetric System

**Statutory Reference**: Health & Safety Code §19960+ (Factory-Built Housing); California Building Code Title 24; 19 USC §1304 (Country of Origin Marking)

\ornsep

## Executive Summary, Key Takeaways, and Action Checklist

\ornsep

::: execsummary

The citizenM hotel system represents the most proven volumetric modular construction model globally, with direct California precedent at scale. This chapter decodes the complete technical and commercial framework: module specifications (150–160 SF rooms, 40,000 lbs per module), the CIMC (China) manufacturing pipeline optimized for West Coast delivery (3–4 week Pacific crossing), and the seismic engineering solutions (reinforced concrete cores + BRB frames) that satisfy California Building Code requirements. The citizenM Menlo Park project—160+ modules assembled in 2 months on Meta's campus—provides the definitive Bay Area reference, proving volumetric viability in Seismic Design Category D/E. You'll master the dual-performance structural challenge: modules must be stiff enough to survive factory-installed interiors during ocean transit, yet flexible enough at connection points to accommodate seismic drift. For CFAs and hotel developers, this translates to 10–12 month construction schedules versus 28–30 months conventional, zero franchise fees (vs. 6–10% revenue drag), and 0.3 FTE/key staffing (vs. 0.5–0.8 FTE conventional). The tariff risk on CIMC modules (currently 7.5–25% Section 301) is the primary variable to stress-test in financial models, but the Menlo Park project demonstrates the model's resilience. **Core principle**: volumetric modular is no longer experimental for California—it's proven, scalable, and operationally superior to conventional hospitality construction.

:::

\ornsep

::: keytakeaways

- **citizenM Menlo Park is your California benchmark**: 240 keys, 5 stories modular on concrete podium, 160+ CIMC-manufactured modules assembled in 2 months. Study this project's design, logistics, seismic engineering, and assembly sequence—it answers definitively whether modular works in California's seismic environment.

- **CIMC (China) manufacturing is the optimal pipeline for West Coast projects**: 3–4 week Pacific crossing to Oakland Port, 15–25% lower module cost than Polish sourcing, and proven California track record (DTLA, Menlo Park). Budget tariff risk at both current (7.5%) and worst-case (25%) scenarios—delta could be $1M–$2M on 160-module project.

- **Module specifications deliver maximum key density**: 150–160 SF rooms (vs. 300+ SF conventional), 48 ft x 8 ft x 9 ft modules stack 5–9 floors above podium. Revenue per SF is 60–80% higher than conventional hotels at same ADR—a structural advantage in low-yield markets.

- **Seismic performance requires specialized engineering**: Reinforced concrete shear core + BRB frames handle lateral loads; Hollo-Bolt countersunk connections accommodate controlled slip under seismic motion. Seek structural engineers with specific modular-seismic experience—this is not standard EOR skill set. John A. Martin & Associates' DTLA solutions are transferable to Bay Area projects.

- **Assembly rate of 1 floor/week is achievable at scale**: Menlo Park demonstrated 4 floors/week at peak. Concurrent podium construction means modules begin setting while concrete work continues. Learning curve flattens after 2–3 floors—plan accordingly in schedule and crew sizing.

- **Concrete podium timing is the critical path driver**: 6–9 months podium construction before modules begin setting. Optimize podium schedule to minimize gap between completion and module arrival. Just-in-time delivery of modules (staggered arrivals) reduces on-site storage footprint and carrying costs.

- **Technology-forward operations reduce staffing and increase NOI**: Self-check-in kiosks + app-controlled rooms reduce operating labor. 0.3 FTE/key (citizenM standard) vs. 0.5–0.8 FTE (conventional full-service) creates 40–60% labor cost advantage. This advantage persists over project life and stacks with no-franchise-fee model.

- **Pro forma integration: Model citizenM at 200–300 ADR with 80%+ occupancy**; Layer in $200–$300 ADR (brand positioning), 0.3 FTE/key staffing, zero franchise fees, and tariff scenarios. Stress-test at both current and worst-case tariff levels to understand downside risk tolerance.

:::

\ornsep

::: checklist

**Site Selection & Acquisition:**

-  [ ]  Confirm parcel can accommodate concrete podium (2–4 stories) + 5–9 modular floors (min. 75–100 ft building height)
-  [ ]  Verify zoning allows hotel use and confirms height/setback envelope for planned floor count
-  [ ]  Evaluate neighborhood ADR and demand elasticity at citizenM's brand positioning (value-oriented, tech-forward)
-  [ ]  Check site for ground improvement requirements (liquefaction, expansive soil) that affect podium cost
-  [ ]  Confirm access for ocean freight delivery to nearby port (Oakland for CIMC modules)

**Design & Structural Engineering:**

-  [ ]  Engage structural engineer with prior modular seismic project experience (CIMC modules preferred)
-  [ ]  Design concrete core for primary lateral resistance; BRB frames for supplemental capacity
-  [ ]  Specify Hollo-Bolt countersunk connection details for module-to-module and module-to-podium
-  [ ]  Model dual-performance structural requirements: shipping loads + seismic in-service loads
-  [ ]  Include third-party special inspection protocol for all module connections and core construction

**Pro Forma & Financial Modeling:**

-  [ ]  Model ADR at $200–$300 range; occupancy at 78–82% (low-volatility brand positioning)
-  [ ]  Budget construction cost at $150K–$180K/key (vs. $180K–$220K conventional); confirm CIMC module cost estimates
-  [ ]  Stress-test tariff impact: run scenarios at 7.5%, 15%, and 25% tariff rates on total CIMC module cost
-  [ ]  Calculate carrying cost advantage: 10–12 month construction vs. 28–30 months conventional (carrying cost delta ~$200K–$400K)
-  [ ]  Model staffing at 0.3 FTE/key; compare NOI advantage vs. branded conventional hotel (typically 6–10% revenue improvement)

**Franchise & Brand Development:**

-  [ ]  Secure citizenM franchise LOI; confirm room design compliance with module specifications (150–160 SF max)
-  [ ]  Obtain brand building program and FF&E specifications from citizenM (may differ from typical hotel standards)
-  [ ]  Confirm technology platform (self-check-in, room controls) and IT infrastructure requirements
-  [ ]  Validate revenue share structure: citizenM typically charges licensing fee vs. franchise fee (confirm exact terms)

**Logistics & Manufacturing Coordination:**

-  [ ]  Issue RFQ to CIMC for module fabrication; request quotes at baseline and worst-case tariff levels
-  [ ]  Plan module delivery schedule: stagger arrivals to match crane capacity and on-site storage limits
-  [ ]  Coordinate with Port of Oakland: confirm ship schedule, dwell time, and trucking to site
-  [ ]  Contract heavy haul trucking for oversize load (modules are 48 ft wide); plan night hauling if urban area
-  [ ]  Arrange on-site staging yard if modules cannot be set immediately upon arrival

**Permitting & Approvals:**

-  [ ]  Confirm California Building Code seismic compliance pathway; may require peer review of connection details
-  [ ]  Obtain soils engineer recommendation for foundation and podium design
-  [ ]  File building permit with modular-specific structural documentation (connection details, special inspection plan)
-  [ ]  Schedule pre-construction meetings with building department and special inspector regarding module acceptance

:::

\ornsep

## Overview

citizenM has pioneered the most **fully integrated volumetric modular hotel system** in the world. Their model — compact luxury rooms manufactured in China or Poland, shipped globally, and stacked on concrete podiums — represents the leading edge of modular hospitality construction. Their California projects provide directly relevant case studies.

\ornsep

## Technical Specifications

| Spec | citizenM Standard |
| --- | --- |
| **Room Size** | 150–160 SF (king bed) |
| **Module Dimensions** | 48 ft x 8 ft x 9 ft (2 rooms + corridor) |
| **Module Weight** | ~40,000 lbs per module |
| **Structure** | Steel frame on concrete podium |
| **Manufacturer** | Polcom (Poland); CIMC (China) |
| **Podium** | 2–4 story concrete (lobby, F&B, amenities) |
| **Module Stack** | 5–9 modular floors above podium |
| **Connections** | Hollo-Bolt countersunk; module-to-module bearing |
| **Lateral System** | Concrete shear core + BRB frames |
| **Facade** | Factory-applied panels per module |
| **MEP** | Pre-installed; central riser shaft per floor |
| **Assembly Rate** | 1 floor/week after learning curve |

\ornsep

| Project | Keys | Stories | Modules | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **citizenM Bowery, NYC** | 300 | 21 | 210+ | Polcom (Poland) |
| **citizenM DTLA** | 315 | 11 | 225+ | CIMC (China) |
| **citizenM Seattle** | 264 | 7 | 228 | Polcom (Poland) |
| **citizenM Menlo Park** | 240 | 5 | 160+ | CIMC (China) |

### Key Observations

- **Bowery NYC** (21 stories): Tallest volumetric modular hotel; proved high-rise viability
- **DTLA** (11 stories): China-manufactured modules shipped via ocean freight; California seismic compliance
- **Seattle** (7 stories): Polish manufacturing; standard mid-rise format
- **Menlo Park** (5 stories): On Meta campus; **160+ modules assembled in just 2 months**; closest reference project for Bay Area developers

\ornsep

## California Seismic Engineering

### The Dual-Performance Challenge

Modules must satisfy two conflicting structural requirements:

1. **During shipping**: Stiff enough to protect factory-installed interiors, finishes, and MEP during ocean transit and road transport
2. **When stacked**: Flexible enough to accommodate seismic lateral loads without damage to connections or interior finishes

### Proven Solutions (citizenM DTLA)

- **Reinforced concrete core** provides primary lateral force resistance
- **Buckling-restrained brace (BRB) frames** supplement the core
- **Hollo-Bolt connections** transfer gravity and lateral loads between modules
- **Module-to-module bearing** distributes vertical loads
- **Seismic isolation** between podium and module stack (where applicable)

### Engineering Coordination

- Structural engineer must design for both transport and in-service conditions
- Connection details require specialized testing and peer review
- California building code (CBC) seismic provisions apply regardless of manufacturing location
- Third-party special inspection required for all structural connections

\ornsep

## Manufacturing and Supply Chain

### Poland (Polcom)

- Established modular manufacturer with long citizenM relationship
- European labor standards and quality control
- Ocean shipping to US East Coast: 2–3 weeks
- Best for East Coast and Midwest US projects

### China (CIMC)

- Largest container manufacturer in the world; modular is adjacent capability
- Significant cost advantage in labor and steel
- Ocean shipping to US West Coast: 3–4 weeks
- **Best for California and Pacific projects**
- citizenM DTLA and Menlo Park both sourced from CIMC

### Supply Chain Comparison

| Factor | Poland (Polcom) | China (CIMC) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Labor Cost** | Moderate | Low |
| **Steel Cost** | EU market | Lower (domestic supply) |
| **Shipping to CA** | 6–8 weeks (via Panama) | 3–4 weeks (Pacific) |
| **Quality** | Established | Proven on citizenM projects |
| **Lead Time** | 12–16 weeks production | 10–14 weeks production |
| **Tariff Risk** | Lower | Higher (Section 301) |

\ornsep

## Site Assembly Process

### Typical Sequence

1. **Podium construction**: Conventional concrete; 6–9 months
2. **Module delivery staging**: Off-site yard or just-in-time delivery
3. **Crane setting**: Mobile or tower crane; 1 floor per week target
4. **Module connections**: Structural, MEP, fire-rated
5. **Corridor and common area completion**: Between module rows
6. **Facade sealing**: Weather-tight envelope
7. **MEP tie-ins**: Connect module risers to central plant
8. **Testing and commissioning**: Per brand standards

### Assembly Rate

- **Learning curve**: First 2–3 floors slower; team develops rhythm
- **Steady state**: 1 floor per week is achievable target
- **Menlo Park**: 160+ modules in ~2 months = approximately 4 floors per week at peak

\ornsep

## citizenM Model Relevance

### Why citizenM Matters for California Developers

- **Proven in California**: DTLA and Menlo Park projects demonstrate seismic compliance
- **CIMC manufacturing**: Chinese supply chain established for West Coast delivery
- **Compact rooms**: 150–160 SF maximizes keys per floor plate
- **Hybrid podium**: Concrete podium handles parking, lobby, and amenities
- **Rapid assembly**: 2-month module setting demonstrated at Menlo Park
- **Technology-forward**: Self-check-in, app-controlled rooms reduce operating costs

\ornsep

## Bay Area Case Studies & Lessons Learned

### Case 1: citizenM Menlo Park — The Definitive Bay Area Reference

The citizenM Menlo Park hotel (240 keys, 5 stories modular on concrete podium) on Meta's Willow Village campus is the **single most important modular hotel reference project** for Bay Area developers. Key data points:

- **160+ modules** manufactured by CIMC in Shenzhen, China
- Shipped via ocean freight to **Port of Oakland** (~3-week transit)
- Trucked to Menlo Park via US-101 (oversize load, night hauling)
- All modules set in approximately **2 months** (peak rate: ~4/day)
- Concrete podium: 2 stories (lobby, restaurant, meeting rooms, back-of-house)
- Seismic system: reinforced concrete shear core + BRB frames
- Module connections: Hollo-Bolt countersunk, module-to-module bearing
- Total construction schedule: approximately 18 months (vs. 28–30 months conventional estimate)

The project proved that volumetric modular works in California's Seismic Design Category D/E environment, with Chinese-manufactured modules meeting CBC requirements.

**Lesson Learned**: citizenM Menlo Park is your benchmark. When pitching modular to investors, lenders, or franchise brands in the Bay Area, this is the proof point. Study every aspect: the CIMC manufacturing process, the shipping logistics, the connection details, the seismic engineering, and the assembly sequence. This project answers the question "Can modular work in California?" definitively.

\ornsep

### Case 2: citizenM DTLA — High-Seismic Volumetric at Scale

The citizenM DTLA (315 keys, 11 stories, CIMC-manufactured) provided the engineering template that Menlo Park adapted. The DTLA project's reinforced concrete core with BRB frames handled the full seismic lateral demand, while modules transferred gravity loads through bearing connections.

Key engineering detail: modules were designed with **dual-performance steel frames** — stiff enough to protect factory-installed finishes during the 3-week Pacific Ocean crossing, yet flexible enough at the connection points to accommodate seismic drift when stacked. This dual requirement drove the use of countersunk Hollo-Bolt connections that allow controlled slip under seismic loading.

**Lesson Learned**: The DTLA-to-Menlo Park engineering progression shows that citizenM's seismic solutions are transferable within California. The structural engineer who worked on DTLA (John A. Martin & Associates) developed connection protocols that were directly adapted for Menlo Park. When planning your modular project, seek structural engineers with specific modular-seismic experience — this is a specialized skill set that most structural firms do not have.

\ornsep

### Case 3: Supply Chain Comparison — CIMC (China) for Bay Area Projects

For Bay Area projects, CIMC offers significant advantages over Polcom (Poland):

| Factor | CIMC (China) | Polcom (Poland) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Ocean transit to Oakland** | 3–4 weeks (Pacific) | 6–8 weeks (Atlantic + Panama Canal) |
| **Module cost** | 15–25% lower | Baseline |
| **Steel cost** | Domestic Chinese supply | EU market pricing |
| **Production capacity** | Very large (container manufacturing adjacent) | Medium |
| **Quality** | Proven on citizenM DTLA + Menlo Park | Proven on citizenM Bowery + Seattle |
| **Tariff risk** | Section 301 tariffs may apply (currently 7.5–25%) | Lower tariff risk |
| **Communication** | Mandarin/English; aligns with SVDen team | English/Polish |

CIMC's Shenzhen facility has produced modules for two California citizenM projects and has demonstrated compliance with CBC seismic requirements. The primary risk is **tariff volatility** under Section 301.

**Lesson Learned**: For Bay Area projects, CIMC is the preferred manufacturer based on transit time, cost, and proven California track record. However, budget for tariff risk — model your pro forma at both current tariff levels and a worst-case 25% tariff scenario. The tariff delta on a 160-module project could be $1M–$2M.

\ornsep

## Practitioner Checklist

-  [ ]  Study citizenM Menlo Park as primary California reference project
-  [ ]  Evaluate CIMC as module manufacturer for West Coast projects
-  [ ]  Engage structural engineer experienced in modular seismic design
-  [ ]  Design for dual-performance: shipping stiffness + seismic flexibility
-  [ ]  Plan ocean shipping logistics: port to site, storage, just-in-time delivery
-  [ ]  Assess tariff risk for China-sourced modules (Section 301)
-  [ ]  Budget for concrete podium construction concurrent with module fabrication
-  [ ]  Plan crane logistics: tower vs. mobile, duration, staging area

\ornsep

## Financial Decision Gateway

citizenM's vertically integrated model represents the upper bound of modular hotel financial efficiency:

| citizenM Variable | citizenM Model | Traditional Hotel | App G Connection |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Construction schedule** | ~18 months total | 28–30 months | Level 2: Death Point (§2D) pushed 10–12 months |
| **No franchise fees** | 0% of revenue | 6–10% of gross revenue | Level 2: NOI improves 6–10% |
| **Lean staffing** | 0.3 FTE/key | 0.5–0.8 FTE/key | Level 2: 40–60% labor cost reduction |
| **Compact rooms (150 SF)** | Maximized keys/floor | Standard (300+ SF) | Level 2: Revenue per SF significantly higher |
| **CIMC manufacturing** | 15–25% lower module cost | EU/US sourcing | Level 2: hard cost reduction |
| **Tariff risk (Section 301)** | 7.5–25% on CIMC modules | N/A | Level 3: Stress Test variable |

> **Pro Forma Integration**: When underwriting a citizenM-style project, apply Appendix G's 3-level framework with these citizenM-specific inputs: (1) Napkin Test — use $200–$300 ADR at 80%+ occupancy; (2) Development Pro Forma — model 0.3 FTE/key staffing; (3) Stress Test — model tariff scenarios at 7.5% and 25% on CIMC modules, and test a 3-month shipping delay scenario.

> **Frontier Builder Connection**: citizenM is profiled in **Appendix F §F.6**, positioned in the "High Technology / Owner-Operator" quadrant of the Survival Curve matrix (§F.8). The key insight: citizenM's vertical integration (own-operate, no franchise) avoids the franchise fee drag — but requires the developer to also be the operator, a fundamentally different business than development-for-sale. Compare with Marriott's franchise-backed modular model (Ch 11).

\ornsep

## Key Takeaway

> citizenM's volumetric system is the **most fully proven modular hotel model globally**, with two California projects demonstrating seismic compliance and rapid assembly. The CIMC (China) manufacturing pipeline is directly relevant for West Coast developers. The Menlo Park project — 160+ modules assembled in 2 months — sets the benchmark for California modular hotel delivery.

*Next: Chapter 13 shifts to the due diligence trifecta — title, survey, and geotechnical investigations — that must confirm your site can physically and legally support the project you've designed. -> Chapter 13: Title, Survey & Geotechnical*


\part{Technical Due Diligence}

# Title, Survey & Geotechnical Engineering

**Statutory Reference**: Insurance Code §12340+ (Title Insurance); Public Resources Code §2690+ (Seismic Hazards Mapping Act); California Building Code §1613 (Earthquake Loads)

\ornsep

## Executive Summary, Key Takeaways, and Action Checklist

\ornsep

::: execsummary

Title, survey, and geotechnical investigations form the deal-evaluation trifecta that separates competent developers from those blindsided by hidden liabilities. This chapter teaches you to read and interpret each discipline critically, catching site constraints before capital is committed. Title reports reveal encumbrances and CC&Rs that may fundamentally constrain development potential; surveys physically map those constraints onto your site plan; geotechnical reports quantify subsurface conditions and foundation costs. The Bay Area presents specific challenges: alluvial soils in Santa Clara Valley with liquefaction hazards requiring $8K–$15K/unit for ground improvement and enhanced foundations; high water tables near creeks triggering $200K–$500K in dewatering and waterproofing; expansive clays in South Bay requiring post-tensioned slabs and $6K–$15K/unit foundation premiums. Real case studies—Menlo Park's $1.4M liquefaction cost shock, Palo Alto's 14-month easement negotiation, San Jose's expansive clay surprises—demonstrate that skipping due diligence costs exponentially more than the $3K–$6K Phase I ESA. ALTA surveys with appropriate Table A items catch easement conflicts that title reports miss. Geotechnical investigations revealing liquefaction risk, high water tables, or expansive soils should directly drive design decisions (elevated podiums, ground improvement, alternative foundations), not be discovered during plan check. **Core principle**: treat due diligence findings as design inputs, not obstacles to navigate around.

:::

\ornsep

::: keytakeaways

- **Read title reports and surveys together, never separately**: Title reports describe easements by document reference; ALTA surveys show where they actually sit on the ground. Palo Alto case study: 10-ft PG&E easement in title became 15-ft diagonal easement on survey, cutting buildable envelope 18%. Always cross-reference with Table A Items 6, 11, 17 before closing.

- **Bay Area liquefaction risk is non-negotiable in Menlo Park and San Jose**: CGS Seismic Hazard Zone maps identify mapped liquefaction zones in Santa Clara Valley alluvial soils. Budget $8K–$15K/unit for ground improvement (stone columns, jet grouting) and enhanced foundations (mat slabs, lateral spreading design). Menlo Park case: $1.4M additional cost (~$12K/unit) discovered post-closing due to skipped GIS screening.

- **Geotechnical bearing capacity directly controls foundation economics**: Allowable bearing pressure of 1,500+ psf allows spread footings; less than 1,500 psf or highly variable conditions trigger deep foundations (piles/piers), adding $3K–$8K per unit. Liquefaction factor of safety (FOS) less than 1.2 requires mitigation. Settle these issues in geotech phase, not at foundation design.

- **High water table demands waterproofing and dewatering planning**: Santa Clara Valley near creeks and wetlands may have seasonal high water tables within 10 feet of grade. Budget $3K–$8K/unit for waterproofing; $100K–$300K for temporary dewatering systems if basements required. ICF basement projects especially vulnerable—plan dewatering permits 6 months in advance.

- **Expansive clays require post-tensioned slabs and overexcavation**: South Bay (Willow Glen, etc.) frequently has CH-classification expansive clays (PI > 40). Budget post-tensioned slab foundations ($4K–$6K/unit premium) plus 18-inch overexcavation with imported non-expansive backfill (adds $5K–$10K/unit site work). San Jose case: $6K/unit foundation cost + 25% grading cost increase from expansive soil.

- **Phase I ESA is mandatory before closing; Phase II cost is controllable after REC identification**: Phase I costs $3K–$6K and is non-negotiable—a $5K Phase I can save $5M remediation liability. If Phase I identifies RECs, budget Phase II at $15K–$100K for soil/groundwater sampling and testing. Check DTSC EnviroStor and SWRCB GeoTracker for adjacent contamination within 1,000 ft (not just on-site).

- **CREC (Controlled REC) or HREC (Historical REC) with engineering controls may still allow development**: Former gas station with institutional controls (deed notice, engineering assessment) may proceed; requires documenting that controls remain effective and factoring ongoing monitoring into operating budget. Distinguish from new REC requiring Phase II investigation and possible remediation.

- **Geotechnical findings drive site plan design decisions**: Liquefaction + flood zone in Menlo Park resulted in elevated podium design—a direct design consequence of geotechnical + survey findings. Don't treat geotech as a constraint to overcome; treat it as a design parameter that shapes your building footprint, foundation type, and cost structure.

:::

\ornsep

::: checklist

**Pre-Acquisition Due Diligence:**

-  [ ]  Run GIS site analysis (§15.5–15.6 protocol): Check for liquefaction zones, flood zones, fault zones, contamination
-  [ ]  If in Bay Area liquefaction zone or near creek: budget $8K–$15K/unit for ground improvement + enhanced foundations
-  [ ]  Check CGS Seismic Hazard Zone maps (Alquist-Priolo, liquefaction, landslide) before making offer
-  [ ]  Review DTSC EnviroStor and SWRCB GeoTracker for contamination within 1,000 ft radius
-  [ ]  For South Bay sites: research soil types (alluvial, clay, fill) and anticipate potential expansive soil conditions

**Title Analysis:**

-  [ ]  Request and review preliminary title report; categorize every Schedule B exception
-  [ ]  Flag CC&Rs that restrict density, height, use type, or commercial operations
-  [ ]  Identify all easements (utility, access, drainage, conservation); note their location on property
-  [ ]  For hotels: verify no restrictions on commercial use, 24/7 operations, or alcohol service
-  [ ]  Confirm all liens are disclosed and plan to clear or subordinate before close (request payoff amounts)

**Survey Ordering & Verification:**

-  [ ]  Order ALTA/NSPS survey with Table A Items: 1 (monuments), 3 (flood zone), 4 (area), 6 (setback lines), 8 (substantial features), 11 (utilities), 13 (adjoining owners), 16 (access), 17 (off-site easements), 19 (topo)
-  [ ]  Require surveyor to plot all title exceptions onto the survey map
-  [ ]  Cross-reference survey with assessor records: verify parcel boundaries match and lot size matches title
-  [ ]  Verify setback lines and buildable envelope against zoning requirements
-  [ ]  Confirm utility easement locations and clearances to planned building footprint

**Geotechnical Investigation:**

-  [ ]  Engage geotechnical engineer before finalizing pro forma assumptions
-  [ ]  Require soil profile analysis to 8 ft minimum depth (boring or test pits); 12 ft if deep foundations likely
-  [ ]  For Bay Area sites: specifically request liquefaction evaluation with factor of safety calculations
-  [ ]  Assess groundwater depth and seasonal variation; request monitoring well data if available
-  [ ]  For sites with basements or underbuilding: request waterproofing and dewatering recommendations
-  [ ]  Request bearing capacity and foundation type recommendations (spread footings vs. deep foundations)
-  [ ]  For modular projects: confirm bearing capacity supports concentrated loads at module bearing points

**Phase I ESA and Environmental Screening:**

-  [ ]  Complete Phase I ESA before closing (cost: $3K–$6K); non-negotiable
-  [ ]  Review Phase I findings for RECs, CRECs, and HRECs; understand remediation timeline for each
-  [ ]  If REC identified: budget Phase II ESA at $15K–$100K depending on scope
-  [ ]  Check for vapor intrusion risk (especially near VOC contamination); budget mitigation ($5K–$50K) if flagged
-  [ ]  For former commercial/industrial sites: budget asbestos and lead paint surveys before demolition

**Cost Impact Assessment:**

-  [ ]  Liquefaction or high water table identified: add $8K–$15K/unit to pro forma hard costs
-  [ ]  Expansive clay: add $5K–$10K/unit for enhanced foundations + grading impact
-  [ ]  REC with Phase II needed: add $15K–$100K soft costs + potential remediation (verify with engineer)
-  [ ]  Easement conflicts: model redline costs or negotiation timeline (could delay approval 6–18 months)
-  [ ]  Flood zone or seismic constraints: incorporate into site plan and verify design accommodates

**Title Clearance & Closing Coordination:**

-  [ ]  Ensure all liens are cleared or subordinated before closing
-  [ ]  Obtain title insurance commitment; verify exceptions are acceptable
-  [ ]  For easements that may impact development: consider easement relocation or recordation agreement
-  [ ]  Confirm access is legally recorded and not subject to termination by property owner
-  [ ]  Record all relevant survey information and geotechnical limitations in project file

:::

\ornsep

## Overview

Technical due diligence separates experienced developers from novices. The ability to fluently read title reports, surveys, and geotechnical reports — and know what to look for — directly impacts your ability to accurately underwrite a deal and avoid costly surprises.

\ornsep

## Part A: Title Report Analysis

### Preliminary Title Report

The preliminary title report is your **first look at what encumbers a property**. Every item in Schedule B (exceptions from coverage) must be reviewed and categorized.

| Exception Category | Typical Items | Risk Level | Action Required |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **CC&Rs** | Use restrictions, architectural controls, HOA requirements | Medium–High | Review for conflicts with proposed development |
| **Easements** | Utility, access, drainage, view, conservation | High | Plot on site plan; check for building envelope conflicts |
| **Liens** | Tax liens, mechanic's liens, judgment liens | Critical | Must be cleared or subordinated before close |
| **Deed Restrictions** | Building height limits, use restrictions, setback requirements | High | May require quiet title action or modification |
| **Mineral Rights** | Subsurface rights reservations | Low–Medium | Evaluate impact on foundation and underground work |

### Red Flags in Title Reports

- Unrecorded easements discovered during survey
- Access easements that don't align with planned site circulation
- CC&Rs that restrict density, height, or use type
- Outstanding liens from prior owners
- For hotels: restrictions on commercial use, alcohol sales, or 24/7 operations

\ornsep

## Part B: ALTA/NSPS Land Title Survey

### The ALTA Survey

The ALTA survey is the **gold standard** for development sites. It combines a boundary survey, topographic information, and title exception mapping into a single document.

### Essential Table A Optional Items

For development projects, specify these Table A items:

| Item | Content | Why It Matters |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Item 1** | Monuments | Confirms boundary markers exist |
| **Item 2** | Address and parcel ID | Cross-reference with assessor records |
| **Item 3** | Flood zone | FEMA determination; insurance requirements |
| **Item 4** | Gross land area | Confirms lot size for density calculations |
| **Item 6** | Setback/building lines | Defines buildable envelope |
| **Item 8** | Substantial features | Trees, walls, structures near boundaries |
| **Item 11** | Utilities | Location of all utility connections and services |
| **Item 13** | Names of adjoining owners | Identify neighbors for outreach |
| **Item 16** | Evidence of access | Confirms legal and physical access |
| **Item 17** | Off-site easements | Easements benefiting the property |
| **Item 19** | Topo contours | Grading design basis; drainage analysis |

\ornsep

## Part C: Geotechnical Engineering

### Reading a Geotechnical Report

A geotechnical report translates subsurface conditions into engineering recommendations that directly impact your **foundation design, construction method, and project cost**.

| Section | What to Look For | Red Flags |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Soil Profile** | USCS classification, depth to bedrock, consistency | Expansive clays (CH), organic soils (OH/OL), fill soils |
| **Groundwater** | Seasonal high water table depth | Water table within 10 ft of foundation; perched water |
| **Bearing Capacity** | Allowable bearing pressure (psf) | Less than 1,500 psf; significant variation across site |
| **Liquefaction** | Factor of safety, settlement estimates | FOS less than 1.2; settlement greater than 1 inch; lateral spreading |
| **Seismic Design** | Site class, spectral acceleration values | Site Class E or F; near-fault effects |
| **Foundation Recommendations** | Type, depth, reinforcement, drainage | Deep foundations required (piles/piers); extensive soil improvement |
| **Slope Stability** | Factor of safety for cut/fill slopes | FOS less than 1.5 for static; less than 1.0 for seismic |

### Hotel-Specific Geotechnical Concerns

| Concern | Why It Matters for Hotels |
| --- | --- |
| **Larger footprint** | More soil variability across site |
| **Underground parking** | Dewatering, lateral earth pressure, waterproofing |
| **Modular stacking loads** | Higher point loads at module bearing points |
| **Full-block sites** | May need ground improvement program |
| **Module connections** | Must transfer seismic loads through connections |

\ornsep

### Bay Area Geotechnical Considerations

The San Francisco Bay Area presents specific challenges:

- **Alluvial soils** in the Santa Clara Valley are susceptible to liquefaction
- **High water tables** near creeks and former marshlands
- **Mapped liquefaction hazard zones** under the Seismic Hazards Mapping Act
- Portions of **Menlo Park and San Jose** have documented liquefaction risk

### Foundation Implications

For projects with basements (e.g., ICF basement construction):
- **Waterproofing requirements** based on groundwater depth
- **Dewatering during construction** — permits and temporary systems
- **Lateral earth pressure** design for basement walls
- **Hydrostatic uplift** potential on mat foundations

\ornsep

## Bay Area Case Studies & Lessons Learned

### Case 1: Menlo Park — Liquefaction Risk on Former Marshland

A developer acquired a 1.5-acre parcel near Bayfront Expressway in Menlo Park for a 120-unit multifamily project. The preliminary title report was clean, and the ALTA survey showed no unusual conditions. However, the geotechnical investigation revealed the site was on **former marshland with a seasonal high water table at 4 feet below grade** and a liquefaction factor of safety below 1.0 for the design earthquake.

The geotechnical engineer recommended: (1) ground improvement using stone columns ($800K), (2) a mat foundation instead of spread footings ($400K premium), (3) a structural slab designed for lateral spreading forces ($200K premium). Total geotechnical-driven cost increase: **$1.4M** — approximately $12K per unit that was not in the original underwriting.

**Lesson Learned**: In Menlo Park and other Bay-adjacent areas, always check the CGS Seismic Hazard Zone maps for liquefaction before putting a site under contract. If the site is in a mapped liquefaction zone, budget $8K–$15K per unit for ground improvement and enhanced foundations. This cost should be reflected in your offer price, not discovered after closing.

\ornsep

### Case 2: Palo Alto — Utility Easement Kills Building Envelope

A developer purchased a 12,000 SF lot on University Avenue in Palo Alto for a mixed-use project. The preliminary title report showed a 10-ft PG&E gas easement along the rear property line — seemingly routine. However, when the ALTA survey plotted the easement, it was discovered that the easement was actually a **15-ft easement** (the title report referenced the wrong recorded document) and it ran diagonally, not parallel to the rear lot line. The easement cut into the buildable envelope, reducing the building footprint by 18% and making the planned underground parking layout infeasible.

The developer spent 14 months negotiating an easement relocation with PG&E, ultimately paying $180K for PG&E to relocate the gas main and rerecord the easement.

**Lesson Learned**: Never trust the title report alone — always cross-reference with the ALTA survey. Title reports describe easements by document reference; surveys show where they actually are on the ground. Order your ALTA survey with Table A Items 6 (setback lines), 11 (utilities), and 17 (off-site easements) to catch these conflicts before closing.

\ornsep

### Case 3: San Jose — Expansive Clay Surprises

A residential developer in the Willow Glen area of San Jose encountered **highly expansive clays (CH classification, PI > 40)** at shallow depth across the entire site. The geotechnical report recommended post-tensioned slab foundations with a minimum 30-inch moisture barrier on all sides — adding $6K per unit to the foundation cost.

More critically, the expansive soil conditions required deeper utility trenches with imported non-expansive backfill, increasing site work costs by 25%. The grading plan had to be revised to accommodate 18 inches of overexcavation and replacement across the pad area.

**Lesson Learned**: Santa Clara Valley soils are notoriously variable. Even within a single neighborhood, soil conditions can range from well-graded gravel to highly expansive clay. Always order a geotechnical investigation before finalizing your pro forma — foundation costs in the South Bay can vary by $5K–$15K per unit based on soil conditions alone.

\ornsep

## Practitioner Checklist

### Title
-  [ ]  Review every Schedule B exception in the preliminary title report
-  [ ]  Plot all easements on the site plan
-  [ ]  Identify and clear all liens before closing
-  [ ]  Check CC&Rs for development restrictions
-  [ ]  For hotels: verify no restrictions on commercial use or alcohol

### Survey
-  [ ]  Order ALTA survey with appropriate Table A items
-  [ ]  Cross-reference survey with title exceptions
-  [ ]  Verify lot size matches assessor records
-  [ ]  Confirm setback lines and buildable envelope
-  [ ]  Check flood zone determination

### Geotechnical
-  [ ]  Review soil profile for expansive clays, fill, or organic soils
-  [ ]  Check groundwater depth and seasonal variation
-  [ ]  Evaluate bearing capacity for proposed structure type
-  [ ]  Assess liquefaction risk (especially in Bay Area)
-  [ ]  Review foundation recommendations and cost implications
-  [ ]  For modular: confirm bearing capacity supports stacking loads
-  [ ]  For basements: evaluate waterproofing and dewatering requirements

\ornsep

## Financial Decision Gateway

Due diligence findings feed directly into the pro forma financial decision framework:

| Finding | Cost Impact | App G Connection |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Easement reduces buildable area** | Fewer units -> revenue reduction | Level 1: Napkin Test may fail if unit count drops |
| **Liquefaction zone** | $8K–$15K/unit for ground improvement | Level 2: add to Sources — site work line |
| **Expansive clay** | $5K–$15K/unit for enhanced foundations | Level 2: add to Sources — foundation line |
| **High water table** | Dewatering + waterproofing = $200K–$500K | Level 3: Stress Test — geotech contingency |
| **Title defect / lien** | Variable — may be deal-killer | Level 3: Kill Criteria #2 (title not insurable) |

> **Pre-Design Connection**: This chapter's due diligence findings should be completed as part of the Pre-Design Site Constraint Analysis (Appendix E). Sections 1 (Title & Easement) and 4 (Physical Site Constraints) of Appendix E provide the structured template for documenting these findings before authorizing design work. The Peninsula case study in Appendix E — where a $800K+ loss resulted from skipping this analysis — is the cautionary tale that makes this chapter's content essential, not optional.

> **AI Tool Connection**: GIS site screening (Ch 15, §15.5–15.6) can identify liquefaction zones, flood zones, and contamination risks in the 3-minute screen — before you even order title or geotech reports. Deepblocks (Ch 15, §15.8) can model the buildable-area impact of easements automatically. See Appendix F §F.7 for the PropTech platforms that automate constraint mapping.

\ornsep

## Key Takeaway

> **Title, survey, and geotech are your deal-evaluation trifecta.** Title tells you what encumbers the property. Survey tells you the physical truth of the site. Geotech tells you what's underground and what it costs to build on. Read all three before committing capital.

*Next: Chapter 14 covers civil engineering, stormwater, and environmental compliance — the infrastructure layer that transforms raw land into a buildable site. -> Chapter 14: Civil Engineering, Stormwater & Environmental*


# Civil Engineering, Stormwater & Environmental Site Assessment

**Statutory Reference**: Clean Water Act §402 (NPDES/SWPPP); 40 CFR Part 312 (ASTM E1527, Phase I ESA); California Water Code §13000+; Health & Safety Code §25300+ (Hazardous Substances)

\ornsep

## Executive Summary, Key Takeaways, and Action Checklist

\ornsep

::: execsummary

Civil engineering, stormwater management, and environmental assessment are three interconnected disciplines that directly impact site design, construction cost, and regulatory compliance—yet are frequently underestimated in pro forma budgeting. This chapter decodes Bay Area stormwater (C.3) requirements that consume 5–10% of buildable site area, plus the environmental screening protocols that catch contamination liabilities early. Grading plans establish cut/fill balance, drainage adequacy, and retaining wall requirements ($50–$150/SF per wall face). More critically, Provision C.3 of the Bay Area Municipal Regional Permit mandates stormwater treatment for projects creating 10,000+ SF of impervious surface—bioretention areas that must sit 10 feet minimum from building foundations, often eating 8–15% of site area on typical projects. Hotels with large parking footprints and restaurant grease systems face additional requirements (grease interceptors, oil/water separators, parking area containment). Phase I ESAs ($3K–$6K) identify Recognized Environmental Conditions (RECs) requiring Phase II investigation ($15K–$100K); adjacent property contamination (within 1,000 ft) must be evaluated for vapor intrusion risk. Menlo Park case study: C.3 stormwater consumed 12% of site area, forcing redesign of building footprint and loss of 1 unit + 8 parking spaces. Palo Alto case: SWPPP violations resulted in $45K fine + 2-week stop-work order + mandatory third-party monitoring. San Carlos Hotel Indigo: $150K environmental assessment suite (Phase I, Phase II, vapor intrusion, hazmat surveys) uncovered nearby tetrachloroethylene plume requiring precautionary vapor mitigation. **Core principle**: design stormwater management and environmental constraints before finalizing building footprint, not after.

:::

\ornsep

::: keytakeaways

- **C.3 stormwater is the silent site-plan killer**: Budget 5–10% of site area for bioretention or other treatment measures.
