### Using Previous Projects as Templates & Prototypes

**The paradigm shift**: Stop designing from scratch. Build once, deploy many times.

The most successful developers don't start fresh for each project — they maintain a library of **proven prototypes** and **project templates** that they rapidly deploy and adapt. This is especially powerful for modular, repetitive projects (ADUs, hotel rooms, multifamily unit types, subdivisions).

#### **The Template Strategy**

| Asset Type | What You Save | How You Reuse | Time Savings |
|------------|---------------|---------------|--------------|
| **Revit Project Template** | Families, view templates, standards, schedules | Start every new project with proven components | 60-80% setup time |
| **AutoCAD Template (DWG)** | Title blocks, layer standards, approved details | Civil drawings start pre-configured | 40-60% setup time |
| **Parametric Module Families** | Fully-designed ADU/unit types | Place and adapt to new sites | 70-90% design time |
| **Detail Library** | Construction details that passed plan check | Copy proven details vs. redrawing | 80-95% detailing time |
| **Tentative Map Template** | City-approved street/utility standards | Adapt lot configuration to new parcel | 50-70% civil design |

\ornsep

#### **Workflow 1: Creating a Revit Project Template from Successful Project**

**Scenario**: You completed a successful ADU project. Save it as a template for future ADUs.

**Step-by-step:**

```
1. OPEN COMPLETED PROJECT
   - File -> Open -> Select your approved ADU project

2. SAVE AS TEMPLATE
   - File -> Save As -> Template (.rte file)
   - Name: "ADU-600SF-Studio-TEMPLATE.rte"
   - Location: Your templates folder

3. CLEAN UP PROJECT-SPECIFIC DATA (before saving)
   - Delete site-specific items:
     X Remove actual site address/parcel
     X Remove project-specific title block info
     X Remove specific lot dimensions

   - Keep reusable components:
     + All manufacturer families (fixtures, appliances, HVAC)
     + Wall/floor/roof types (proven assemblies)
     + Room/space types and schedules
     + View templates (floor plan, elevations, sections, 3D)
     + Construction details that passed plan check
     + Sheet layouts and title blocks (with blank fields)

4. CREATE MULTIPLE VARIANTS (optional)
   - Save additional templates for variants:
     - ADU-400SF-Studio-TEMPLATE.rte
     - ADU-600SF-1BR-TEMPLATE.rte
     - ADU-800SF-2BR-TEMPLATE.rte

5. USE TEMPLATE FOR NEW PROJECT
   - File -> New -> Project
   - Template: Select "ADU-600SF-Studio-TEMPLATE.rte"
   - New project opens with ALL your proven components pre-loaded
   - Just add site-specific info (address, lot, orientation)
```

**Time comparison:**

| Task | From Scratch | From Template | Savings |
|------|--------------|---------------|---------|
| Load manufacturer families | 3 hours | 0 minutes | 100% |
| Create wall/floor/roof types | 4 hours | 0 minutes | 100% |
| Set up views and sheets | 2 hours | 15 minutes | 87% |
| Create schedules | 1.5 hours | 5 minutes | 94% |
| Place fixtures/appliances | 3 hours | 30 minutes | 83% |
| Site-specific adaptation | 2 hours | 2 hours | 0% |
| **TOTAL** | **15.5 hours** | **2.8 hours** | **82%** |

**ROI: Save 12.7 hours per ADU project** = $1,905-$2,540 in labor cost (@$150-$200/hr)

\ornsep

#### **Workflow 2: Creating Parametric Prototype Families**

**Scenario**: You're developing SB 9 projects (2-4 ADUs per site). Create a master ADU family that you place multiple times.

**The power**: Design once, place 4 times, update globally.

**Step-by-step:**

```
1. CONVERT COMPLETED PROJECT TO FAMILY
   - Open your approved ADU Revit model
   - File -> Save As -> Family (.rfa file)
   - Name: "ADU-Studio-600SF-PROTOTYPE.rfa"

2. ADD PARAMETRIC VARIATIONS
   - Family Types -> Add parameters:
     - Foundation_Type: "Slab" | "Crawlspace" | "Basement"
     - Roof_Type: "Flat" | "Gable" | "Hip"
     - Facade_Option: "A" | "B" | "C" (different exterior finishes)
     - Mirror_Layout: Yes/No (flip floor plan)

3. CREATE VISIBILITY CONTROLS
   - Different foundation types show/hide based on parameter
   - Different facades show/hide based on parameter
   - This creates dozens of variants from one master family

4. BUILD FAMILY TYPE CATALOG
   - Save 8-12 pre-configured types:
     - Studio-Slab-Flat-FacadeA
     - Studio-Crawl-Gable-FacadeB
     - 1BR-Slab-Hip-FacadeC
     - (etc.)

5. DEPLOY TO SB 9 SUBDIVISION PROJECT
   - Open subdivision site plan project
   - Load family: "ADU-Studio-600SF-PROTOTYPE.rfa"
   - Place 4 instances on 2 lots
   - Select different type for each based on:
     - Soil conditions (foundation type)
     - Design review requirements (facade)
     - Market demand (unit size)

6. GLOBAL UPDATES
   - Edit master family (change kitchen layout)
   - Save family
   - All 4 placed ADUs update automatically
```

**Real example: SB 9 project with 4 ADUs**

**Traditional workflow:**
- Design ADU #1: 40 hours
- Design ADU #2: 35 hours (some reuse)
- Design ADU #3: 30 hours (more reuse)
- Design ADU #4: 25 hours
- **Total: 130 hours**
- Update all 4 when design review requires changes: 20 hours
- **Grand total: 150 hours**

**Prototype family workflow:**
- Design master prototype: 45 hours (slightly longer upfront)
- Place 4 instances: 2 hours
- Customize each (facade, orientation): 8 hours
- **Total: 55 hours**
- Update master when design review requires changes: 2 hours (updates all 4)
- **Grand total: 57 hours**

**Time savings: 93 hours = 62% reduction**
**Cost savings: $13,950-$18,600 (@$150-$200/hr)**

\ornsep

#### **Workflow 3: AutoCAD Template for Subdivision Projects**

**Scenario**: You got a tentative map approved in San Jose. Save it as template for future San Jose subdivisions.

**Step-by-step:**

```
1. OPEN APPROVED TENTATIVE MAP
   - Open your city-approved DWG file

2. CREATE TEMPLATE VERSION
   - Delete site-specific geometry:
     X Actual lot lines (keep example/sample)
     X Site address
     X Specific dimensions

   - Keep city-approved standards:
     + Title block format
     + Layer structure
     + Street cross-section details
     + Utility standard details
     + ADA ramp details
     + Fire hydrant symbols
     + Street tree plan format
     + All required notes/call-outs
     + City-specific requirements checklist

3. SAVE AS TEMPLATE
   - File -> Save As -> AutoCAD Drawing Template (.dwt)
   - Name: "San-Jose-Tentative-Map-TEMPLATE.dwt"
   - Location: Your templates folder

4. CREATE JURISDICTION-SPECIFIC TEMPLATES
   - San-Jose-Tentative-Map-TEMPLATE.dwt
   - Sunnyvale-Tentative-Map-TEMPLATE.dwt
   - Santa-Clara-Tentative-Map-TEMPLATE.dwt
   - (Each city has different requirements)

5. USE FOR NEW PROJECT
   - File -> New -> Select template
   - Opens with all city standards pre-loaded
   - Just add new lot configuration
   - All required details/notes already there
```

**Time comparison:**

| Task | From Scratch | From Template | Savings |
|------|--------------|---------------|---------|
| Research city requirements | 4 hours | 0 minutes | 100% |
| Create title block | 1 hour | 0 minutes | 100% |
| Set up layers/standards | 2 hours | 0 minutes | 100% |
| Draw street details | 6 hours | 0 minutes | 100% |
| Create utility standards | 4 hours | 0 minutes | 100% |
| Add required notes | 2 hours | 5 minutes | 96% |
| Design lot layout | 8 hours | 8 hours | 0% |
| **TOTAL** | **27 hours** | **8 hours** | **70%** |

**Plus: Eliminates plan check corrections** for missing city-specific requirements

\ornsep

#### **Workflow 4: AI-Assisted Template Optimization**

Use AI to improve your templates over time:

```
You: [Upload your ADU Revit template file]
"Analyze this Revit template. What manufacturer families are outdated?
What best practices am I missing? How can I make this more efficient?"

Claude: "Analyzing your ADU template:

OUTDATED COMPONENTS:
1. Kohler toilet family is from 2018 - update to 2025 model with WaterSense
2. HVAC mini-split is R-410A refrigerant - update to R-32 for Title 24 2025
3. Windows show U-factor 0.35 - Title 24 now requires 0.30 for Climate Zone 3

MISSING BEST PRACTICES:
1. No room data stamps - add for automated area calculations
2. No phasing configured - add for remodel/addition projects
3. No design options set up - should have 2-3 facade options
4. Material takeoff schedules missing - add for cost estimation

EFFICIENCY IMPROVEMENTS:
1. Consolidate 15 wall types down to 8 (you have redundant types)
2. Create shared parameters for custom data (lot number, APN, etc.)
3. Add view filters for design review submittals
4. Include worksets for multi-user collaboration

RECOMMENDED ADDITIONS:
1. Pre-configured solar panel family (common ADU feature now)
2. EV charger family (AB 2127 requirement)
3. Battery storage family (increasingly common)
4. Prefab bathroom pod option (Villa, Pacer)

UPDATE PRIORITY:
1. HVAC (Title 24 compliance) - CRITICAL
2. Windows (energy code) - CRITICAL
3. Material schedules (cost control) - HIGH
4. Design options (client presentation) - MEDIUM
5. Solar/EV (market demand) - MEDIUM"
```

**This continuous improvement of templates compounds over time.**

\ornsep

#### **Workflow 5: Cross-Project Knowledge Transfer with AI**

Combine templates + reference library + AI for maximum efficiency:

```
You: "I'm starting a new SB 9 project in San Jose. I have these previous projects:
- 2023: 8-lot subdivision (completed)
- 2024: SB 9 lot split with 2 ADUs (in construction)
- 2025: 4-unit small lot subdivision (approved)

Which should I use as template? What should I adapt?"

Claude: "Based on your project library, here's the optimal approach:

PRIMARY TEMPLATE:
Use: 2024 SB 9 lot split project
Why:
- Same regulatory pathway (SB 9 ministerial)
- Same jurisdiction (San Jose)
- ADU components already approved
- Smaller scope = cleaner starting point

COMPONENTS TO PULL FROM OTHER PROJECTS:

From 2023 8-lot subdivision:
+ Utility connection details (already approved by San Jose)
+ Street improvement standards
+ Fire hydrant spacing
X DON'T use: Tentative map format (SB 9 uses simpler parcel map)

From 2025 4-unit subdivision:
+ Recent Title 24 2025 compliance (energy code updated)
+ AB 2127 EV charger details (if included)
+ Updated solar-ready requirements
X DON'T use: Full subdivision process (overkill for SB 9)

ADAPTATION CHECKLIST:
 [ ]  Update ADU models to 2025 Title 24 (from 4-unit project)
 [ ]  Use utility details from 8-lot project (pre-approved)
 [ ]  Use parcel map format from SB 9 project (correct pathway)
 [ ]  Verify SB 9 still allows 2 ADUs per lot (law may have changed)
 [ ]  Check if San Jose updated local SB 9 standards since 2024

WORKFLOW:
1. Start new project from 2024 SB 9 template
2. Import updated families from 2025 project (Title 24 compliance)
3. Import utility details from 2023 project (reuse approved details)
4. Adapt lot configuration to new site
5. Submit parcel map application (60-day ministerial timeline)

ESTIMATED TIME:
- Starting from scratch: 60-80 hours
- Using templates + cross-project components: 12-18 hours
- TIME SAVINGS: 75-80%"
```

\ornsep

### The Compounding Template Library Strategy

**Year 1: Build Your First Templates**
- Complete 2-3 projects from scratch
- Save each as template
- Time investment: Normal (no savings yet)

**Year 2: Deploy Templates**
- Start projects from Year 1 templates
- Time savings: 60-70% on setup/design
- Refine templates based on lessons learned
- Add 2-3 new project type templates

**Year 3: Full Template Arsenal**
- Templates for all project types you do regularly
- Cross-project component library
- Time savings: 70-85% on typical projects
- Consultant costs drop (less design time)

**Year 4+: Competitive Moat**
- You deliver projects 2x faster than competitors
- 40% lower soft costs (design/engineering)
- Can bid aggressively on timeline
- Your templates are proven (less plan check risk)

#### **The ROI Math: Template Strategy**

**Example: Developer doing 4 ADU projects/year**

**Year 1 (Building Templates):**
- Project 1: 40 hours design (from scratch)
- Save as template: 2 hours
- Project 2: 35 hours (partial reuse)
- Improve template: 2 hours
- Project 3: 30 hours (more reuse)
- Refine template: 2 hours
- Project 4: 25 hours (using refined template)
- **Total Year 1: 136 hours**

**Year 2 (Using Templates):**
- Project 1: 8 hours (from template)
- Project 2: 7 hours (template + learning)
- Project 3: 6 hours (efficient now)
- Project 4: 6 hours
- **Total Year 2: 27 hours**

**Savings: 109 hours = $16,350-$21,800 (@$150-$200/hr)**

**And it compounds:**
- Year 3: 24 hours (4 projects)
- Year 4: 20 hours (4 projects)
- 5-year cumulative: 343 hours saved vs. from-scratch approach
- **5-year value: $51,450-$68,600**

\ornsep

### Practitioner Checklist: Template & Prototype Strategy

#### **Template Creation**

-  [ ]  Identify your 3 most common project types
-  [ ]  Save next completed project of each type as template
-  [ ]  Clean up project-specific data before saving
-  [ ]  Document what's included in template (README file)
-  [ ]  Test template by starting new project from it

#### **Template Library Organization**

```
[dir] Project-Templates/
|--- [dir] Revit-Templates/
|   |--- [file] ADU-Studio-600SF-TEMPLATE.rte
|   |--- [file] ADU-1BR-800SF-TEMPLATE.rte
|   |--- [file] Multifamily-8unit-TEMPLATE.rte
|   \--- [note] README.md (what's in each template)
|
|--- [dir] AutoCAD-Templates/
|   |--- [file] San-Jose-Tentative-Map-TEMPLATE.dwt
|   |--- [file] Sunnyvale-Site-Plan-TEMPLATE.dwt
|   \--- [note] README.md
|
|--- [dir] Revit-Families/ (Prototypes)
|   |--- [file] ADU-Modular-PROTOTYPE.rfa
|   |--- [file] Unit-Type-A-Studio-PROTOTYPE.rfa
|   \--- [note] README.md
|
\--- [dir] Detail-Library/
    |--- [dir] Foundation/ (approved details)
    |--- [dir] Utilities/ (approved connections)
    \--- [note] INDEX.md (quick reference)
```

#### **Template Maintenance**

-  [ ]  Review templates quarterly
-  [ ]  Update for code changes (Title 24, IBC, local amendments)
-  [ ]  Update manufacturer families (new products, discontinued items)
-  [ ]  Add lessons learned from recent projects
-  [ ]  Version control templates (v1.0, v1.1, v2.0)

#### **AI-Assisted Template Optimization**

-  [ ]  Upload template to AI for analysis
-  [ ]  Ask: "What's outdated? What's missing?"
-  [ ]  Update based on AI recommendations
-  [ ]  Test updated template on next project
-  [ ]  Document improvements

\ornsep

### The Template Strategy: Key Principles

1. **Build Once, Deploy Many**: First project is investment, subsequent projects are returns
2. **Cross-Project Learning**: Pull best components from all past projects
3. **Continuous Improvement**: Templates get better with each project
4. **AI-Accelerated**: AI helps optimize templates faster than manual review
5. **Competitive Advantage**: 70-85% time savings = 2x faster delivery than competitors

**Bottom line**: Your template library becomes your most valuable asset — it's your institutional knowledge in executable form.

\ornsep

## Part F: AI Jump-Start Fast Track Guide for Developer-Savvy Professionals

### The 30-60-90 Day AI-Powered Fast Track Transformation

**For the developer who asks**: "I'm ready to go all-in on AI + technical literacy + fast-track development. What's the fastest path to becoming competitive?"

This section synthesizes everything above into an aggressive, high-ROI implementation roadmap. If you're developer-savvy (comfortable with technology, willing to invest time upfront for exponential returns), this is your blueprint.

\ornsep

### The Transformation: Before vs. After

| Metric | Traditional Developer | AI-Powered Fast Track Developer | Advantage |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------------------|-----------|
| **Technical learning time** | 130 hours (generic tutorials) | 60-80 hours (AI-guided, project-based) | 50-70 hours saved |
| **Drawing review time** | 4-6 hours per submittal | 1-2 hours (AI-assisted) | 70% faster |
| **Plan check correction rate** | 60% of submittals | 15% of submittals | 75% reduction |
| **Design time (4 ADUs)** | 130 hours (from scratch each time) | 27 hours (template-based) | 79% faster |

| **Projects/year capacity** | 2-3 projects | 5-8 projects | 2-3x throughput |
| **Competitive timeline** | Match market | Beat market by 40% | Win on speed |

**Bottom line**: 90 days to transform from average developer to market leader.

### Phase 1: First 30 Days — Foundation & Quick Wins

**Week 1: AI Setup + GIS Mastery (15 hours)**

**Monday-Tuesday: AI Tools Setup (4 hours)**
```
 [ ]  Subscribe to Claude Pro ($20/month)
 [ ]  Test uploading PDFs, asking questions
 [ ]  Create "AI Prompts Library" document
 [ ]  Practice: Upload a past project drawing, ask 5 questions
 [ ]  Build template prompts:
  - "Analyze this tentative map for code compliance..."
  - "Compare this design to my reference project..."
  - "What manufacturer families do I need for..."
```

**Wednesday-Friday: GIS Deal Screening Mastery (11 hours)**
```
 [ ]  Complete §15.5-15.6 checklist (11-step protocol)
 [ ]  Bookmark all GIS resources (FEMA, CGS, CalEnviroScreen, etc.)
 [ ]  Practice on 10 real sites:
  - 5 successful past projects (understand why they worked)
  - 5 failed deals (understand why they didn't)
 [ ]  Build custom GIS screening spreadsheet
 [ ]  Time yourself: Get to 15-minute full screening
```

**Deliverable**: Can screen any site for development constraints in 15 minutes

\ornsep

**Week 2: AutoCAD for Plan Review (20 hours)**

**Monday-Wednesday: Core Commands with AI Tutor (12 hours)**
```
 [ ]  Get 3 past civil drawings (tentative maps, grading plans)
 [ ]  Open in AutoCAD
 [ ]  For EVERY symbol/line type you don't understand:
  - Screenshot it
  - Upload to Claude: "What does this symbol mean? Code requirements?"
  - Document answer in "AutoCAD Quick Reference" guide
 [ ]  Practice measuring:
  - DIST command: Verify 20 lot dimensions
  - AREA command: Calculate 10 irregular lot areas
  - Compare to title block schedules — find errors
```

**Thursday-Friday: Real Project Review (8 hours)**
```
 [ ]  Get current/upcoming project civil drawings
 [ ]  Review with AI assistance:
  - Upload drawing + zoning code
  - Ask: "Does this comply? What errors do you see?"
 [ ]  Generate red-line PDF with corrections
 [ ]  Compare AI findings to what civil engineer missed
 [ ]  Send corrections to engineer (impress them with thoroughness)
```

**Deliverable**: Can review tentative map and catch 80% of errors independently

\ornsep

**Week 3: Revit Navigation (25 hours)**

**Monday-Wednesday: Revit Basics with AI (15 hours)**
```
 [ ]  Get architect's Revit model from past project
 [ ]  Open model, navigate with AI as tutor:
  - Screenshot every task
  - Ask Claude: "How do I extract door schedule?"
  - Document in "Revit Navigation Guide"
 [ ]  Master core tasks:
  - View navigation (plans, elevations, sections, 3D)
  - Schedule generation (doors, windows, rooms, finishes)
  - Property checking (wall types, material specs)
  - Basic filters and selection sets
```

**Thursday-Friday: Real Model Review (10 hours)**
```
 [ ]  Get current/upcoming project Revit model
 [ ]  Review with AI assistance:
  - Screenshot concerning areas
  - Upload: "Is this wall assembly correct for Title 24?"
 [ ]  Generate findings report
 [ ]  Compare to what architect would charge for peer review ($5K-$10K)
 [ ]  Realize you just saved that cost
```

**Deliverable**: Can navigate any Revit model and extract information independently

\ornsep

**Week 4: Reference Library Setup (10 hours)**

**Monday-Tuesday: Organize Past Projects (6 hours)**
```
 [ ]  Create folder structure (§15.14)
[dir] Reference-Library/
  |--- [dir] Subdivisions/
  |--- [dir] Multifamily/
  |--- [dir] ADU/
  |--- [dir] Details-Library/
  \--- [dir] Checklists/

 [ ]  Collect ALL past project files:
  - Drawings (PDF, DWG, RVT)
  - Plan check corrections
  - RFIs and change orders
  - Final costs vs. budget
  - Permits and approvals
```

**Wednesday-Thursday: Document Lessons Learned (4 hours)**
```
 [ ]  For last 3 projects, create Lessons-Learned.md:
  - What went right (Check)
  - What went wrong (X)
  - Reusable assets [pkg]
  - Next time improvements (Target)
 [ ]  Be brutally honest — failures teach more than successes
```

**Deliverable**: Organized reference library with lessons documented

\ornsep

 [ ]  Update pro forma templates with learned patterns

## Key Takeaway

> **You don't need to draft — you need to read, measure, and analyze.** Revit lets you navigate BIM models and run coordination reviews. AutoCAD lets you verify dimensions and compliance. GIS lets you screen deals in minutes. **AI compresses your learning curve by 50-70 hours and catches errors you'd miss manually. Your reference library of past projects is worth more than any tutorial — it's your competitive moat.**
>
> **Most powerful of all: Convert past projects into templates and prototypes.** Design once, deploy many times. Your template library enables 70-85% time savings on subsequent projects, creating a compounding competitive advantage.
>
> **The AI Jump-Start Fast Track path delivers 592-822% ROI in 90 days** and creates $1.5M-$2.5M in cumulative advantage over 5 years. The question isn't whether to invest in technical literacy + AI tools — it's whether you can afford NOT to. Your competitors are reading this same chapter. The only question is: who executes first?
>
> Together, these tools make you a technically literate developer who can hold your own with engineers and architects — and beat the market on speed, quality, and cost.

# Entitlement Workflows & Financial Analysis

**Statutory Reference**: Government Code §65920–65964 (Permit Streamlining Act); §65941.1 (SB 330 Preliminary Application); §65864–65869.5 (Development Agreements); §66000–66025 (Mitigation Fee Act)

\ornsep

## Executive Summary, Key Takeaways, and Action Checklist

\ornsep

::: execsummary

Entitlement is the highest-risk development phase because substantial capital is deployed before revenue certainty exists. This chapter provides end-to-end workflows for residential and hotel projects, plus the financial analysis framework that integrates with Appendix G's 3-level decision methodology. Residential pathways range from ministerial (SB 35 multifamily in RHNA-deficient cities = 3–6 months, no CEQA) to discretionary (EIR required = 24–36 months, significant carrying costs). Hotel entitlement is inherently discretionary, requiring Conditional Use Permit (CUP) with design review, traffic/parking/noise studies, and 20–28 month timeline. The Permit Streamlining Act (Government Code §65920–65964) imposes hard statutory deadlines that are your enforcement levers: completeness review (30 days), EIR project decision (180 days), SB 35 approval (90–180 days depending on unit count). If cities miss deadlines, projects are "deemed approved" by operation of law. SB 330 preliminary applications (filed at site control) lock in fees and development standards before mid-entitlement increases of $5K–$10K/unit—a single one-page filing can save $600K–$935K across multiple projects. Impact fees vary wildly by jurisdiction and project type ($30K–$100K/unit residential, $10K–$35K/key hotels); concurrent filing of building permits while entitlement proceeds saves 4–8 months on critical path, worth $340K–$600K in carrying cost reduction on typical Bay Area project. The Palo Alto/Redwood City case study demonstrates the $2.8M entitlement premium difference between discretionary (28 months) and ministerial (5 months) pathways on identical 80-unit buildings. **Core principle**: jurisdiction selection determines entitlement timeline and cost more than any other factor. Know your pathway before site acquisition.

:::

\ornsep

::: keytakeaways

- **SB 35 ministerial pathway compresses entitlement from 28 months to 5–6 months**: Multifamily projects in RHNA-deficient jurisdictions (most Bay Area cities) bypass CEQA, public hearings, and discretionary denial in favor of objective standards compliance. Check HCD's annual determination letter before committing to jurisdiction. If eligible, timeline is 90–180 days vs. 24–36 months for discretionary—equivalent to $2.44M in carrying cost savings on typical Bay Area multifamily.

- **SB 330 preliminary application locks fees before mid-entitlement increases**: One-page filing (site address, unit count, footprint sketch) at site control freezes development fees, exactions, building standards for 3 years. Typical Bay Area cities increase fees $4K–$8K/unit during entitlement. Three projects filed across San Jose, Mountain View, Sunnyvale = $2.375M fee savings total. Highest-ROI activity in residential development—takes 1 hour, saves $600K–$935K per project.

- **Jurisdiction selection is the $2.8M question**: Identical 80-unit multifamily in Palo Alto (discretionary, 28 months, $3.42M entitlement cost) vs. Redwood City (SB 35 ministerial, 5 months, $615K entitlement cost). $2.8M difference is purely jurisdiction choice, not site quality or building type. Before site acquisition, map entitlement pathway by jurisdiction. "Cheaper" land in restrictive city costs more in total development cost.

- **Concurrent building permit filing saves 4–8 months on critical path**: File building permit plans while entitlement proceedings continue (not after approval). Risk: plan revisions if conditions change. Upside: $340K–$600K carrying cost savings. Expected-value calculation strongly favors concurrent filing in most scenarios. Requires close coordination between entitlement and design teams, but compresses Death Point (§Appendix G) significantly.

- **Permit Streamlining Act deadlines are enforcement levers**: Cities have 30 days completeness review, 180 days EIR project decision, 90–180 days SB 35 approval (by statute). If city misses deadline, project is "deemed approved" by operation of law. Track every deadline meticulously in project calendar; send formal notice if city misses. This is your leverage to accelerate sluggish reviews.

- **Impact fee variation across jurisdictions creates $1M+ swings**: Residential impact fees: $30K–$100K/unit depending on city, affordable component, density bonus eligibility. Hotel impact fees: $10K–$35K/key plus ongoing TOT (8–14% room revenue annually). Same building type in different cities has vastly different fee burden. Include fee estimates in Level 1 Napkin Test; lock in via SB 330 if residential.

- **Development agreements lock regulatory certainty for 10–25 years**: For large, phased projects where regulatory risk is high, negotiate development agreement with city (requires public hearing, discretionary, subject to CEQA). Developer provides enhanced public benefits (affordable housing, parks, infrastructure); city provides certainty that entitlement conditions won't change. Useful when current entitlement conditions are favorable and you want to lock them in before city planning changes.

- **Hotel entitlement requires 20–28 months and full discretionary process**: CUP application with traffic, parking, noise studies; design review hearing; planning commission CUP hearing; ABC liquor license (parallel track). No ministerial pathways exist for hotels. Budget full CEQA (MND or EIR), design review process, and political risk. Impact fees per key are lower than residential per unit, but TOT obligation ($1.4M/year on 188-key hotel) is ongoing burden residential doesn't face.

:::

\ornsep

::: checklist

**Residential Entitlement Pathway:**

-  [ ]  Determine ministerial vs. discretionary pathway before site acquisition
-  [ ]  If multifamily residential: check HCD SB 35 annual determination letter (confirms RHNA-deficient status)
-  [ ]  If eligible for SB 35: 90–180 day timeline, no CEQA, objective standards compliance only
-  [ ]  If discretionary: budget 24–36 months, CEQA (likely MND or EIR), design review process, political risk
-  [ ]  For SB 9 (single-family lot splits + ADUs): 60–90 day ministerial timeline, no CEQA

**Site Control & Preliminary Application (Week 1–2):**

-  [ ]  File SB 330 preliminary application immediately upon site control (day 1 if possible)
-  [ ]  Preliminary application requires only: site address, unit count, approximate footprint sketch
-  [ ]  SB 330 locks fees and development standards for 3 years
-  [ ]  Estimated fee savings: $600K–$935K depending on jurisdiction
-  [ ]  Estimated time to file: 30 minutes
-  [ ]  Have city planning staff confirm preliminary application receipt and vesting date

**Pre-Application Conference (Week 2–3):**

-  [ ]  Request pre-application conference with planning director or entitlement planner
-  [ ]  Presentation: project summary, land use, unit count, parking, height/massing, affordable component (if applicable)
-  [ ]  Deliverable: written summary from city confirming requirements, identified issues, recommended pathway
-  [ ]  For hotels: confirm CUP pathway and required studies (traffic, parking, noise, design review)
-  [ ]  Document any oral feedback in writing before proceeding with design

**Environmental & Technical Studies (Month 2–4):**

-  [ ]  Determine CEQA pathway: exempt, categorical exemption, ND, MND, or EIR
-  [ ]  If exempt or categorical exemption: budget 6–12 months
-  [ ]  If MND required: budget 12–18 months (environmental assessment + public review)
-  [ ]  If EIR required: budget 18–36 months (full environmental review + alternatives analysis)
-  [ ]  For hotels: order traffic impact study, parking study, noise study (required for CUP)

**Application Submittal (Month 4–6):**

-  [ ]  Submit complete application with all required studies, architectural drawings, technical documentation
-  [ ]  Permit Streamlining Act: city has 30-day completeness deadline
-  [ ]  Track completeness review deadline in project calendar
-  [ ]  If city requests additional information on day 25: deadline resets for another 30 days (respond quickly to avoid delays)

**Public Hearings (Month 8–18, depending on pathway):**

-  [ ]  Ministerial (SB 35): no public hearings required
-  [ ]  Discretionary residential: design review + planning commission hearing (1–3 hearings)
-  [ ]  Hotels: design review + planning commission CUP hearing (2–3 hearings minimum)
-  [ ]  SB 330 limits housing projects to maximum 5 hearings
-  [ ]  Budget contingency for appeal periods (typically 10 calendar days)

**Conditions of Approval Negotiation:**

-  [ ]  Conditions of approval are negotiated at hearing (sometimes before, if city willing)
-  [ ]  Typical residential conditions: design revisions, traffic calming, parking management, affordable housing monitoring
-  [ ]  Typical hotel conditions: operating hours, noise limits, delivery times, valet parking, community relations
-  [ ]  Red-flag conditions: those that change project economics (height reduction, unit loss, increased parking requirement)
-  [ ]  Request written conditions before leaving hearing; clarify any ambiguous language

**Permit Streamlining Act Calendar:**

-  [ ]  Day 0: Application deemed complete (or city requests additional information)
-  [ ]  Day 30: Completeness review deadline (PSA §65943)
-  [ ]  Day 60–180: Project decision deadline (varies by CEQA pathway, §65950):
  - Day 60 for categorical exemption or ND/MND projects
  - Day 180 for EIR projects
  - Day 90–180 for SB 35 (90 days for <150 units, 180 for 150+ units)
-  [ ]  If city misses deadline: project is "deemed approved" by operation of law
-  [ ]  Document all deadline misses in writing; escalate to city manager if pattern emerges

**Concurrent Building Permit Filing (Month 8–12 during entitlement):**

-  [ ]  Begin building permit design while entitlement still in progress (not after approval)
-  [ ]  File building permit simultaneously with entitlement approval (or 2–4 months before, if confident in entitlement outcome)
-  [ ]  Risk mitigation: coordinate with entitlement attorney regarding condition change risk
-  [ ]  Expected value: $340K–$600K carrying cost savings vs. $30K–$60K plan revision cost
-  [ ]  For projects with clear entitlement path: concurrent filing is nearly always ROI-positive

**Building Permit Coordination (Month 12–18):**

-  [ ]  Plan check with building department (4–8 weeks)
-  [ ]  Parallel submittals with fire marshal, public works, stormwater, water/sewer districts
-  [ ]  Use phased permits if available: grading/foundation permit before full building permit (start site work 2–3 months earlier)
-  [ ]  Track AB 1114 plan check deadlines (30 days for <25 units, 60 days for 26+ units)
-  [ ]  If city misses AB 1114 deadline: applicant can use third-party plan check at city expense

**Hotel Entitlement Specifics:**

-  [ ]  No ministerial pathways for hotels (full discretionary process required)
-  [ ]  Timeline: 20–28 months from application to building permit (vs. 5–6 months SB 35 residential)
-  [ ]  Required studies: market study, traffic impact, parking analysis, noise study, design review
-  [ ]  Parallel track: ABC liquor license application (month 6, concurrent with entitlement)
-  [ ]  Timeline for ABC license: 6–12 months (separate process, often faster than building permit)
-  [ ]  CUP conditions typically include: operating hours, delivery windows, valet parking requirements, community relations

**Fee & Financial Tracking:**

-  [ ]  Impact fees (residential): budget $40K–$100K/unit depending on jurisdiction and inclusionary ordinance
-  [ ]  Impact fees (hotel): budget $10K–$35K/key
-  [ ]  SB 330 preliminary application savings: document fees locked at application date
-  [ ]  TOT (hotels): budget 8–14% of gross room revenue as ongoing operating expense
-  [ ]  School fees: statutory cap ($4–$5/SF), no negotiation available
-  [ ]  Park/Quimby fees: potentially negotiable via land dedication alternative

**Financial Stress Testing (Integration with Appendix G):**

-  [ ]  Level 1 Napkin Test: use impact fee estimates from §16.3; confirm projects pencils at maximum zoning buildout
-  [ ]  Level 2 Development Pro Forma: include impact fees as line items in Sources & Uses; include SB 330 fee vesting date
-  [ ]  Level 3 Stress Test: model timeline delay scenarios (6, 12, 18 months); calculate carrying cost impact
-  [ ]  Sensitivity analysis: vary impact fees ±20%, entitlement timeline ±6 months, interest rates ±100 basis points
-  [ ]  If concurrent filing strategy used: stress test impact of plan revision costs if conditions change

:::

\ornsep

## Overview

Entitlement is the process of obtaining all governmental approvals necessary to develop a property. It is typically the **highest-risk phase** of development because significant capital is deployed before any revenue certainty exists. This chapter covers the complete workflow for both residential and hotel projects, plus the financial analysis framework.

\ornsep

## Part A: Residential Entitlement Workflow

### Step-by-Step Process

| Step | Action | Timeline |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | **Pre-Application Conference** | Week 1–2 |
| 2 | **File SB 330 Preliminary Application** | Week 2–3 (locks in fees/standards) |
| 3 | **Determine Pathway**: ministerial vs. discretionary | Week 3–4 |
| 4 | **Submit Application** with all required studies | Week 4–8 |
| 5 | **Completeness Review** (30 days per PSA) | 30 days |
| 6 | **CEQA Determination** | 0–24 months (depends on pathway) |
| 7 | **Public Hearings** (max 5 under SB 330) | 1–3 months |
| 8 | **Negotiate Conditions of Approval** | At hearing |
| 9 | **Building Permit** submission | After entitlement |

### Pathway Decision Matrix

| Pathway | When It Applies | Timeline | CEQA |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **SB 35** | Multifamily, RHNA-deficient jurisdiction, affordable component | 3–6 months | None |
| **SB 9** | Single-family lot, duplex/lot split | 2–4 months | None |
| **ADU** | By-right on residential/mixed-use lot | 60 days | None |
| **Categorical Exemption** | Class 3 (up to 6 units) or Class 32 (infill) | 6–12 months | Exempt |
| **MND** | Mitigable impacts | 12–18 months | MND |
| **EIR** | Significant unmitigable impacts | 24–36 months | Full EIR |

\ornsep

## Part B: Hotel Entitlement Workflow

### Step-by-Step Process

| Step | Action | Timeline |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | **Market Study + Brand Selection** (franchise LOI) | Month 1–3 |
| 2 | **Pre-Application Conference** (critical for CUP) | Month 2–3 |
| 3 | **Site Plan with Brand Building Program** | Month 3–6 |
| 4 | **CUP Application** + traffic, parking, noise studies | Month 4–8 |
| 5 | **CEQA** (likely MND or EIR) | Month 6–24 |
| 6 | **Design Review Hearing** | Month 18–24 |
| 7 | **Planning Commission CUP Hearing** | Month 20–28 |
| 8 | **ABC License** (parallel track from Month 6) | Month 6–12 |
| 9 | **Building Permit** + franchise construction doc approval | Month 24–30 |
| 10 | **Fire Marshal + Health Department** | Month 28–32 |

\ornsep

## Part C: Permit Streamlining Act Timelines

**Statutory Reference**: Government Code §65920–65964

| Action | Deadline | Code Section | If Missed |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Completeness Determination** | 30 days from submittal | §65943 | Application deemed complete |
| **EIR Project Decision** | 180 days from EIR certification | §65950 | Project deemed approved |
| **ND/MND Project Decision** | 60 days from ND/MND adoption | §65950 | Project deemed approved |
| **Exempt Project Decision** | 60 days from exemption determination | §65950 | Project deemed approved |
| **SB 35 Review (under 150 units)** | 90 days from submittal | §65913.4 | Project deemed approved |
| **SB 35 Review (150+ units)** | 180 days from submittal | §65913.4 | Project deemed approved |

> These deadlines are your enforcement tools. If a jurisdiction misses them, the project is **deemed approved** by operation of law. Track every deadline meticulously.

\ornsep

## Part D: Development Agreements

**Statutory Reference**: Government Code §65864–65869.5

A development agreement is a **contract between a developer and a local government** that locks in the developer's right to develop under current regulations for a specified term (typically 10–25 years).

### When to Use

- Large, phased projects where regulatory certainty is essential
- Projects requiring significant infrastructure investment
- Situations where current entitlement conditions are favorable

### Structure

- Developer provides **enhanced public benefits** beyond normal conditions
- Government provides **regulatory certainty** for the agreement term
- Agreement is recorded against the property and runs with the land
- Requires public hearing, is discretionary, and subject to CEQA

\ornsep

## Part E: Impact Fees and Financial Analysis

### Impact Fee Comparison: Residential vs. Hotel

| Fee Category | Residential | Hotel | Legal Basis |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **School Fees** | $4–$5 per SF | $0.66 per SF | Gov. Code §65995 |
| **Park / Quimby** | $5K–$30K per unit | N/A | Gov. Code §66477 |
| **Traffic Impact** | $5K–$25K per unit | $2K–$8K per key | Local ordinance |
| **Affordable Housing In-Lieu** | $20–$75 per SF | Varies | Local inclusionary ordinance |
| **Sewer/Water Connection** | $5K–$20K per unit | $3K–$10K per key | Utility district |
| **Public Art** | 1% of construction cost | 1% of construction cost | Local ordinance |
| **TOT** | N/A | 8–14% room revenue | Local (ongoing) |

### Fee Negotiation Strategies

| Fee Type | Negotiability | Strategy |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **School Fees** | None — statutory cap | Pay at permit issuance |
| **Park / Quimby** | Moderate | Land dedication may substitute for fees |
| **Traffic Impact** | Moderate | Trip reduction measures, TDM programs |
| **Affordable Housing** | High | Build affordable units instead of paying in-lieu |
| **Sewer/Water** | Low | Limited to utility district schedule |
| **Public Art** | Moderate | On-site art may substitute for fees |

### Total Fee Estimates (Bay Area)

| Project Type | Fee Range Per Unit/Key |
| --- | --- |
| **Single-Family Home** | $30K–$80K per unit |
| **Multifamily (market rate)** | $40K–$100K per unit |
| **Multifamily (with density bonus)** | $25K–$60K per unit |
| **Hotel (select-service)** | $10K–$25K per key |
| **Hotel (full-service)** | $15K–$35K per key |

\ornsep

### Pro Forma Sensitivity Analysis

Key variables to stress-test in your development pro forma:

| Variable | Typical Range | Impact on Returns |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Construction cost per SF** | +/- 10–15% | High — directly impacts total development cost |
| **Entitlement timeline** | +/- 6–12 months | Medium — carrying costs and opportunity cost |
| **Impact fees** | +/- 20% | Medium — $5K–$10K per unit swing common |
| **Interest rates** | +/- 100 bps | High — affects both construction and permanent financing |
| **Rental rates / RevPAR** | +/- 5–10% | High — directly impacts stabilized value |
| **Vacancy / occupancy** | +/- 3–5% | Medium — affects NOI and debt service coverage |
| **Exit cap rate** | +/- 25–50 bps | Very High — small changes create large value swings |

\ornsep

## Part F: Statute Quick Reference

| Topic | Primary Statute | Applies To |
| --- | --- | --- |
| General Plans | Gov. Code §65000–65763 | All |
| Zoning | Gov. Code §65800–65912 | All |
| Subdivision | Gov. Code §66410–66499.38 | All |
| CEQA | PRC §21000+ | Discretionary |
| HAA | Gov. Code §65589.5 | Housing only |
| SB 35 | Gov. Code §65913.4 | Housing only |
| SB 9 | Gov. Code §65852.21, §66452.6 | SFR lots |
| SB 330 | Gov. Code §65941.1 et al. | Housing (thru 2030) |
| Density Bonus | Gov. Code §65915–65918 | Housing |
| ADU | Gov. Code §65852.2 | Residential |
| Dev. Agreements | Gov. Code §65864–65869.5 | All |
| Permit Streamlining | Gov. Code §65920–65964 | All |
| Mitigation Fee Act | Gov. Code §66000–66025 | All |
| ABC Licensing | Bus. & Prof. §23000+ | Alcohol service |
| Coastal Act | PRC §30000+ | Coastal zone |

\ornsep

## Part G: Concurrent Filing & Building Permit Coordination

### Compressing the Critical Path

The entitlement-to-construction timeline has a hidden bottleneck: the **sequential handoff** between planning approval and building permit issuance. Most developers wait for final entitlement before submitting building permit plans. This creates a 4–8 month dead zone where the project generates zero progress but full carrying costs.

### Concurrent Filing Strategy

Filing building permit plans **while entitlement is still in progress** can save 3–6 months on the critical path. The risk is plan revision if conditions of approval change — but the expected-value calculation strongly favors concurrent filing in most scenarios.

| Phase | Sequential Approach | Concurrent Approach | Time Saved |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Entitlement review | Months 1–12 | Months 1–12 | — |
| Building permit design | Months 13–16 | Months 8–12 (overlap) | 4 months |
| Plan check cycle 1 | Months 17–19 | Months 13–15 | 2 months |
| Plan check corrections | Months 20–22 | Months 16–18 | 2 months |
| Permit issuance | Month 23 | Month 19 | **4 months** |

### Building Permit Coordination Checklist

The building permit phase involves multiple departments that must sign off before issuance. Coordinating these in parallel — not sequentially — is critical.

| Department | Submittal Requirements | Typical Review Time | Parallel? |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Building Division** | Full plan set, Title 24 energy calcs, structural calcs | 4–8 weeks | Primary track |
| **Fire Marshal** | Fire sprinkler plans, fire access, alarm system | 3–6 weeks | Yes — file with building |
| **Public Works** | Grading plan, encroachment permit, utility connections | 4–8 weeks | Yes — file with building |
| **Stormwater (C.3)** | Stormwater management plan, BMP sizing | 3–6 weeks | Yes — file with building |
| **Water District** | Will-serve letter, connection application | 2–4 weeks | Yes — can file during entitlement |
| **Sanitary District** | Sewer connection permit, capacity verification | 2–4 weeks | Yes — can file during entitlement |
| **School District** | School fee calculation | 1–2 weeks | Yes — pay at permit issuance |
| **Green Building** | CalGreen compliance, energy modeling (if required) | 2–4 weeks | Yes — submit with plans |

### Plan Check Acceleration Tactics

| Tactic | How It Works | Expected Savings |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Pre-submittal meeting** | Meet with plan check supervisor before submitting; walk through complex items | Reduces correction cycles by 1 round |
| **Over-the-counter corrections** | For minor corrections, request OTC review instead of formal resubmittal | Saves 2–4 weeks per correction |
| **Expedited plan check** | Some jurisdictions offer paid expedited review (1.5–2x fees) | Cuts plan check time 50% |
| **Third-party plan check** | Use a city-approved third-party plan check firm | Saves 4–8 weeks vs. city review |
| **Phased permits** | Get grading/foundation permit before full building permit | Allows site work to begin 2–3 months earlier |

### AB 1114 Plan Check Deadlines (Effective 2025)

California now requires building departments to return **comprehensive plan check comments** within statutory deadlines:

| Project Size | First Review Deadline | Subsequent Reviews |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **25 units or fewer** | 30 business days | 30 business days |
| **26+ units** | 60 business days | 60 business days |

> **Enforcement**: If the jurisdiction misses the deadline, the applicant may use a third-party plan check firm at the jurisdiction's expense. Track these deadlines as aggressively as PSA deadlines.

### Financial Impact of Schedule Compression

| Variable | 80-Unit MF Project | 188-Key Hotel |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Monthly carrying cost (land + soft costs) | ~$85K/month | ~$120K/month |
| Sequential timeline (entitlement -> permit) | 23 months | 32 months |
| Concurrent timeline (with parallel filing) | 19 months | 27 months |
| **Carrying cost savings** | **$340K** | **$600K** |
| Opportunity cost (earlier revenue) | ~$200K | ~$350K |
| **Total value of compression** | **~$540K** | **~$950K** |

> The concurrent filing strategy has a downside risk of $30K–$60K in plan revisions if conditions change, against an expected upside of $340K–$600K in carrying cost savings. The expected value is overwhelmingly positive for projects with a clear entitlement path.

\ornsep

## Bay Area Case Studies & Lessons Learned

### Case 1: Palo Alto vs. Redwood City — Entitlement Timeline Comparison

Two identical projects — 80-unit multifamily buildings — were proposed simultaneously in Palo Alto and Redwood City by the same development team. Both projects were on similarly zoned parcels (multi-family residential).

#### Side-by-Side Comparison

| Factor | Palo Alto (Discretionary) | Redwood City (SB 35 Ministerial) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Entitlement pathway** | Discretionary — full design review | SB 35 ministerial — objective standards only |
| **Pre-application** | Neighborhood concerns flagged; architectural review required | Confirmed SB 35 eligibility (city behind RHNA) |
| **Environmental review** | MND challenged by neighbors -> upgraded to focused EIR (traffic, aesthetics) | None — CEQA exempt under SB 35 |
| **Public hearings** | 3 hearings before ARB/Planning Commission | None — no public hearings |
| **Conditions of approval** | 14 conditions imposed | Objective standards compliance only |
| **Affordable component** | Per city inclusionary ordinance | 10% affordable (SB 35 minimum) |
| **Total timeline** | **28 months** (application -> building permit) | **5 months** (application -> building permit) |
| **Total soft costs** | **$450K** (environmental, legal, design revisions, consultants) | **$85K** (design, application fees) |

#### Financial Impact: The $2.8M Question

| Cost Component | Palo Alto | Redwood City | Difference |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Land acquisition | $13M | $13M | — |
| Monthly carrying cost | ~$106K/mo (loan interest + taxes + insurance) | ~$106K/mo | — |
| Entitlement timeline | 28 months | 5 months | **23 months** |
| **Carrying cost during entitlement** | **$2.97M** | **$530K** | **$2.44M** |
| **Soft costs** | **$450K** | **$85K** | **$365K** |
| **Total entitlement cost** | **$3.42M** | **$615K** | **$2.8M** |

Same developer, same building type, same land cost — **$2.8M entitlement premium** for choosing the wrong jurisdiction (see Introduction: The $2.8M Question).

**Lesson Learned**: Jurisdiction selection is the single highest-impact decision in Bay Area development. A project that takes 5 months in Redwood City takes 28 months in Palo Alto — a $2.8M entitlement premium on the same building type. Before committing to a site, map out the entitlement pathway by jurisdiction. The "cheaper" land in a restrictive city may cost far more in total development cost than "expensive" land in a streamlined city.

\ornsep

### Case 2: Hotel Indigo San Carlos — Impact Fee Analysis

The Hotel Indigo (188 keys) at 501 Industrial Road illustrates Bay Area hotel fee structures. Based on San Carlos's fee schedules and the project's 119,036 SF building:

| Fee Category | Estimated Amount | Basis |
| --- | --- | --- |
| School impact fees | ~$78K | $0.66/SF x 119,036 SF |
| Traffic impact fees | ~$564K | ~$3K/key x 188 keys |
| Sewer/water connection | ~$940K | ~$5K/key x 188 keys |
| Building permit fees | ~$350K | Estimated at plan check |
| **Total one-time fees** | **~$1.93M** | **~$10.3K/key** |
| TOT (ongoing) | ~$1.4M/year | 12% x estimated $12M gross room revenue |

By comparison, a 188-unit multifamily residential project on the same site would face approximately $40K–$60K/unit in impact fees ($7.5M–$11.3M total) — significantly higher per-unit costs. However, the residential project would benefit from SB 330 fee vesting and potential density bonus concessions.

**Lesson Learned**: Hotel impact fees per key ($10K–$25K) are typically much lower than residential fees per unit ($40K–$100K) in Bay Area jurisdictions. However, hotels face ongoing TOT obligations (8–14% of room revenue annually) that residential projects do not. When comparing hotel vs. residential on the same site, model the total tax/fee burden over 10 years, including TOT, to get an accurate comparison.

\ornsep

### Case 3: Bay Area — SB 330 Preliminary Application Fee Savings Across Jurisdictions

A developer filed SB 330 preliminary applications for three projects simultaneously across Bay Area jurisdictions:

| Jurisdiction | Units | Fee Increase During Entitlement | Savings from SB 330 Vesting |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **San Jose** | 120 units | Parks: +$4K/unit; traffic: +$2K/unit | **$720K saved** |
| **Mountain View** | 85 units | Parks: +$8K/unit; sewer: +$3K/unit | **$935K saved** |
| **Sunnyvale** | 60 units | Below-market-rate in-lieu: +$12K/unit | **$720K saved** |

Total savings across three projects: **$2.375M** — from filing three one-page preliminary applications.

**Lesson Learned**: The SB 330 preliminary application is the highest-ROI activity in California residential development. Bay Area cities increase fees regularly and often dramatically. Filing a preliminary application costs essentially nothing and can save six or seven figures. Develop a practice of filing SB 330 preliminary applications within the first week of site control on every residential project.

\ornsep

## Practitioner Checklist

### Entitlement
-  [ ]  Hold pre-application conference; request written summary
-  [ ]  File SB 330 preliminary application immediately (residential)
-  [ ]  Determine ministerial vs. discretionary pathway
-  [ ]  Calendar all PSA deadlines
-  [ ]  Prepare for maximum 5 hearings (SB 330)
-  [ ]  Negotiate conditions of approval before and at hearing
-  [ ]  For hotels: start ABC licensing on parallel track

### Financial
-  [ ]  Estimate total impact fees per unit/key early in underwriting
-  [ ]  Run pro forma sensitivity analysis on key variables
-  [ ]  Lock in fees via SB 330 preliminary application
-  [ ]  Evaluate density bonus to offset affordable housing requirements
-  [ ]  For hotels: include TOT in operating pro forma
-  [ ]  Stress-test exit cap rate assumptions
-  [ ]  Model schedule delay scenarios (6, 12, 18 month delays)

\ornsep

## Financial Decision Gateway

Chapter 16 and Appendix G form a complementary pair — this chapter provides the entitlement and fee framework; Appendix G provides the 3-level financial decision methodology that uses this chapter's data as inputs.

| This Chapter Provides | Appendix G Uses It For |
| --- | --- |
| **Entitlement timeline estimates** (Part A, B) | Death Point calculation (§2D) — months until holding costs consume profit |
| **Impact fee schedules** (§16.3) | Level 2 Sources & Uses — fee line items |
| **Pro forma sensitivity variables** (§16.4) | Level 3 Stress Test — which variables to stress and by how much |
| **PSA deadlines** (Part C) | Kill Criteria #8 — timeline exceeds financing term |
| **Concurrent filing strategy** (Part G) | Death Point mitigation — compresses timeline, extends runway |
| **SB 330 fee vesting** (Case 3) | Level 3 — eliminates fee-escalation risk from Stress Test |

> **Recommended Workflow**: Use this chapter's data to populate Appendix G's framework. Start with the Level 1 Napkin Test using §16.3 fee estimates. If it passes, build the Level 2 Development Pro Forma using §16.4 sensitivity ranges. Then run the Level 3 Stress Test using the concurrent-filing timeline savings from §16.5–16.7 to see if schedule compression moves the project from "kill" to "go."

> **Frontier Builder Connection**: Appendix F's case studies demonstrate that the most successful builders (TJH, Aro, citizenM) have internalized the entitlement-to-financial analysis workflow described in this chapter. TJH's 12-month delivery guarantee (§F.1) is underwritten against precisely these timeline and fee variables. Aro's 90-day assembly model (§F.2) compresses the timeline so aggressively that many of this chapter's carrying-cost concerns become irrelevant. The lesson: operational speed is a financial strategy, not just an engineering achievement.

\ornsep

## Key Takeaway

> Entitlement is where **time, money, and political skill converge**. For residential, use every ministerial pathway available to compress timelines. For hotels, budget for the full discretionary process. In both cases, **file your SB 330 preliminary application early**, track every PSA deadline, and model the financial impact of delays. The developer who masters entitlement workflow and fee management has a structural advantage over the competition.

*Next: The Appendices provide the reference tools, templates, checklists, and decision frameworks that support every chapter — starting with Appendix A on wildfire reconstruction through Appendix J on the Frontier Builder model. -> Appendices*


\appendix
\part{Appendices}

# California Wildfire Reconstruction — Cases & Lessons Learned

**Pacific Palisades | Santa Monica | Paradise | Santa Rosa**

\ornsep

## Overview

California's devastating wildfires have created **urgent reconstruction demand** across the state. Wildfire rebuilding operates under a distinct regulatory framework — streamlined permitting, relaxed zoning in some cases, and massive demand for construction labor and materials. For developers, wildfire zones present both opportunities (fast-track permits, modular demand) and challenges (contamination, infrastructure gaps, insurance crises).

\ornsep

## Los Angeles — Pacific Palisades & Eaton Fires (January 2025)

### What Happened

The Palisades Fire destroyed approximately **5,300+ structures** in Pacific Palisades, Malibu, and surrounding areas in January 2025. The Eaton Fire destroyed approximately **7,000+ structures** in Altadena and Pasadena. Combined, these fires caused an estimated **$250B+ in damage** — the costliest natural disaster in US history.

### Regulatory Response

| Action | Authority | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Executive Order N-4-25** | Governor Newsom | Suspended CEQA for rebuilding; waived permit fees; streamlined plan check |
| **Emergency permits** | LA City/County | Same-footprint rebuilding permitted by right with expedited plan check |
| **FEMA debris removal** | Federal/FEMA | Government-funded hazardous debris removal before rebuilding |
| **SB 9/ADU overlay** | State law | Rebuilding may include additional units under SB 9 and ADU law |
| **Insurance emergency** | CDI | Moratorium on policy cancellations in fire zones |

### Reconstruction Challenges

| Challenge | Impact | Developer Implication |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Hazardous debris** | Ash contains lead, asbestos, heavy metals, household chemicals | Phase II-level environmental clearance before rebuilding |
| **Soil contamination** | Fire debris contaminates topsoil; some sites require remediation | Budget $10K–$50K per lot for soil testing and remediation |
| **Infrastructure destruction** | Water mains, sewer lines, gas lines, electrical grid damaged | Rebuilding depends on utility restoration timeline |
| **Labor shortage** | 5,000+ homes competing for same contractors simultaneously | Construction costs inflate 20–40% during peak rebuild |
| **Insurance crisis** | Many homeowners underinsured; insurers leaving CA market | Rebuilding cost may exceed insurance proceeds |
| **Coastal Act** | Palisades is in Coastal Zone; Coastal Commission jurisdiction | Even with CEQA waiver, Coastal permits may still apply |

### Modular Opportunity

The Palisades/Eaton rebuild represents the **largest-ever opportunity** for modular and prefab construction in California:

- **5,000+ homes** need to be rebuilt in a compressed timeline
- Conventional construction labor is severely constrained
- Factory-built homes can bypass on-site labor shortages
- Panelized and volumetric systems can deliver homes in 4–6 months vs. 12–18 months conventional
- Governor's executive order explicitly encourages prefab/modular approaches

### Lessons Learned

> **Lesson 1**: Wildfire rebuilding is a **speed game** — homeowners who deploy modular/prefab are back in their homes 6–12 months faster than those waiting for conventional construction. Position modular as the solution to labor scarcity, not just cost savings.

> **Lesson 2**: Even with CEQA waivers, the **Coastal Act still applies** in Pacific Palisades. Developers must navigate Coastal Commission requirements for any rebuilding that changes the building footprint, increases height, or modifies lot coverage. The CEQA waiver does not override Coastal Act jurisdiction.

> **Lesson 3**: **Soil contamination from fire debris** is the hidden cost of wildfire rebuilding. Every lot requires environmental clearance before foundation work. Budget for this in your pro forma — it's not optional.

\ornsep

## Paradise (Camp Fire — November 2018)

### What Happened

The Camp Fire destroyed **18,804 structures** in Paradise and surrounding areas — the deadliest and most destructive wildfire in California history at that time. The town of Paradise (population 26,000) was essentially erased.

### Reconstruction Timeline (7+ Years)

| Phase | Timeline | Status |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Debris removal** | 2019–2020 | Complete (FEMA/CalOES funded) |
| **Infrastructure restoration** | 2019–2023 | Water system rebuilt; some roads still pending |
| **Permit applications** | 2019–present | ~4,500 permits issued (of 11,000+ destroyed homes) |
| **Homes rebuilt** | 2019–2026 | ~3,500 completed (less than 20% of pre-fire housing stock) |
| **Population recovery** | 2019–2026 | ~10,000 residents returned (40% of pre-fire) |

### Why Reconstruction Has Been So Slow

1. **Insurance gaps**: Average insurance payout covered only 60–70% of rebuilding cost; many residents couldn't afford the difference
2. **Infrastructure bottleneck**: Water system had to be completely rebuilt before building permits could be issued
3. **Labor scarcity**: Remote location made it difficult to attract construction crews
4. **Contractor fraud**: Multiple cases of contractor fraud against vulnerable homeowners
5. **Code upgrades**: Rebuilding to current code (WUI fire standards, Title 24 energy) significantly more expensive than original construction
6. **Emotional/financial exhaustion**: Many residents chose to relocate rather than rebuild

### Modular in Paradise

Several modular/manufactured home companies entered the Paradise market. Factory-built homes were the **fastest path to occupancy** — some homeowners were in modular homes within 6 months while neighbors waited 2+ years for conventional construction. However, modular homes faced stigma from some residents who associated them with "temporary" housing.

### Lessons Learned

> **Lesson 1**: **Infrastructure restoration is the gating factor**, not permitting. In Paradise, building permits were available within months, but the water system took 3+ years to fully restore. Developers must assess infrastructure timelines before committing to wildfire rebuild projects.

> **Lesson 2**: The **insurance gap** is the largest barrier to rebuilding. Many homeowners are 30–40% short of actual rebuilding costs. Developers who can offer cost-efficient solutions (modular, panelized, standardized designs) capture the market. Premium custom homes are not what this market needs.

> **Lesson 3**: Wildfire reconstruction at the **community scale** (thousands of homes) takes 7–10+ years. Paradise is still less than 20% rebuilt after 7 years. Factor this reality into any development strategy — the demand is real but the absorption is slow.

\ornsep

## Santa Rosa (Tubbs Fire — October 2017)

### What Happened

The Tubbs Fire destroyed approximately **5,300 structures** in Santa Rosa, including the **Coffey Park** and **Fountaingrove** neighborhoods. These were established suburban neighborhoods with homes valued at $400K–$1.5M.

### Reconstruction: A Success Story

Santa Rosa's rebuilding has been **the most successful** large-scale wildfire reconstruction in California history:

| Metric | Result |
| --- | --- |
| **Permits issued** | ~3,800 (of ~5,300 destroyed) |
| **Homes rebuilt** | ~3,200 completed by 2024 |
| **Average rebuild time** | 18–24 months per home |
| **Modular/prefab share** | ~15% of rebuilds |
| **Population recovery** | ~85% of pre-fire levels |

### Why Santa Rosa Succeeded Where Paradise Struggled

| Factor | Santa Rosa | Paradise |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Location** | Urban/suburban; near SF Bay Area labor market | Rural; 90 min from Sacramento |
| **Infrastructure** | Largely intact (underground utilities survived) | Completely destroyed |
| **Insurance coverage** | Higher policy limits; better-insured homeowners | Many underinsured |
| **Property values** | Higher values justified rebuilding costs | Lower values made rebuilding uneconomical for some |
| **City response** | Expedited permits; dedicated rebuild center | Small town with limited staff |
| **Labor access** | Bay Area construction labor pool | Remote; labor had to be imported |

### Coffey Park — Tract Rebuild Model

Coffey Park (1,500+ homes destroyed) was rebuilt largely as **tract housing** by production builders who purchased burned lots in bulk. This approach offered:
- **Standardized designs** that reduced plan check time to 2 weeks
- **Economies of scale** in purchasing and subcontracting
- **Faster permitting** because repetitive plans only needed review once
- **Lower cost per SF** than custom rebuilding

Several production builders used **panelized construction** for wall and roof assemblies, reducing framing time from 3 weeks to 1 week per home.

### Fountaingrove — Custom Rebuild Challenges

Fountaingrove (higher-value homes, $800K–$1.5M) faced longer rebuild timelines because:
- Custom homes required individual architectural design and plan check
- Steep hillside sites required new geotechnical reports and retaining walls
- WUI fire-hardening requirements (Class A roof, ember-resistant vents, noncombustible decking) added $40K–$80K per home
- Some lots had access issues that complicated construction staging

### Lessons Learned

> **Lesson 1**: **Production building at scale** is the fastest path to community recovery. Coffey Park's tract rebuild model — standardized designs, bulk lot acquisition, repetitive plan checks — outperformed custom rebuilding by 6–12 months per home. Developers who can offer standardized designs with limited customization options will capture the largest share of wildfire rebuild demand.

> **Lesson 2**: **WUI (Wildland-Urban Interface) fire-hardening** is now mandatory for all rebuilding in fire zones. Budget $40K–$80K per home for: Class A roofing, ember-resistant vents, tempered glass windows, noncombustible 5-ft defensible space zone, fire-resistant siding. These requirements significantly increase rebuilding costs compared to the original construction.

> **Lesson 3**: Santa Rosa succeeded because it had **urban advantages** that Paradise lacked: intact infrastructure, proximity to labor, higher property values, and a city government that dedicated resources to expedited permitting. When evaluating wildfire rebuild opportunities, assess these structural factors before committing — not all fire zones offer equal rebuilding economics.

\ornsep

## Regulatory Framework for Wildfire Rebuilding

### Expedited Permitting

| Provision | What It Does | Limitations |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Governor's Executive Orders** | Suspend CEQA, waive fees, streamline review | Temporary; typically 1–3 year duration |
| **Same-footprint rebuilding** | Rebuild to same size/location by right | May not apply if lot has changed (erosion, debris flow) |
| **GC §65852.25** | Expedited ADU permits in disaster zones | Encourages additional density during rebuild |
| **SB 9 overlay** | Lot splits/duplexes available during rebuild | Can add 2–4 units to former single-family lots |
| **WUI fire standards** | CBC Chapter 7A requirements | Mandatory; cannot be waived |

### Environmental Considerations

- **Debris removal**: FEMA-funded removal of hazardous materials required before rebuilding
- **Soil testing**: Post-fire lots may have elevated levels of lead, arsenic, asbestos
- **Erosion/debris flow**: Burned hillsides are susceptible to mudslides for 3–5 years post-fire
- **Stormwater**: Post-fire runoff carries contaminants; enhanced stormwater treatment may be required
- **Biological**: Fire can destroy protected habitat, triggering biological resource reviews for rebuilding

\ornsep

## Modular Construction for Wildfire Reconstruction

### Why Modular Fits

| Advantage | Wildfire Context |
| --- | --- |
| **Speed** | Homes delivered 4–6 months vs. 12–18 conventional |
| **Labor independence** | Factory workers are not competing with on-site labor pool |
| **Quality control** | Consistent fire-hardening standards in factory environment |
| **Cost predictability** | Fixed-price contracts reduce financial risk for homeowners |
| **Scalability** | Factory can produce dozens of homes per month |

### Modular Challenges in Fire Zones

| Challenge | Mitigation |
| --- | --- |
| **Site access** | Fire-damaged roads may not accommodate module transport; verify route |
| **Foundation readiness** | Debris removal and soil remediation must be complete before foundation pour |
| **Utility connections** | Must coordinate with utility restoration timeline |
| **Community perception** | Some residents associate modular with "temporary"; use quality finishes and marketing |
| **WUI compliance** | Modules must be manufactured with CBC Chapter 7A fire-hardening features |

\ornsep

## Frontier Developer Profile: Thomas James Homes (TJH) — Palisades Rebuild

**Thomas James Homes** (tjh.com) is the most significant production-scale player in the Pacific Palisades wildfire rebuild. With **350+ homes in annual production** and divisions across California, the Pacific Northwest, Colorado, and Arizona, TJH has pivoted its infill teardown-rebuild expertise to wildfire reconstruction at scale.

### TJH Business Model

TJH operates in a niche that is uniquely suited to wildfire rebuilding: **single-lot infill teardown-rebuild** in established neighborhoods. Their core model — demolishing aging homes and building new ones on the same lot — is operationally identical to post-fire lot reconstruction.

| Metric | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Annual Production** | 350+ homes |
| **Markets** | SoCal, NorCal (Silicon Valley), Pacific Northwest, Colorado, Arizona |
| **Palisades Price Guarantee** | $650/SF firm price upon contract execution |
| **Delivery Guarantee** | 12 months from permit approval, or TJH pays the homeowner per-day penalties |
| **Construction Debt Facility** | $400M (from Ascent Developer Solutions) |
| **Floorplan Library** | ~50 floorplans, 3-4 elevations, 4-5 structural options |

### TJH Palisades Rebuild Program

TJH completed the **first showcase rebuild home in Pacific Palisades** after the January 2025 fires. Their program offers:

- **Guaranteed firm price** of $650/SF — eliminating cost overrun risk for fire survivors
- **12-month completion guarantee** from permit approval — with daily penalties paid to the homeowner for delays
- **BUILD by TJH** program — turnkey design-build solution on the homeowner's existing lot
- **Pre-designed floorplans** — avoiding the 6-12 month custom design process

### Why TJH Matters for Wildfire Reconstruction

The conventional custom rebuild model fails at scale in wildfire zones because:

1. Custom architects take 6-12 months for design alone
2. Custom bids have no price certainty — costs escalate in labor-scarce markets
3. Individual homeowners have no negotiating leverage with subcontractors

TJH solves all three: pre-designed plans (no design delay), guaranteed pricing (no cost escalation), and production-scale procurement (subcontractor leverage). Their $400M construction debt facility gives them the balance sheet to carry dozens of projects simultaneously.

**Lesson Learned**: TJH's model is the closest thing to **production homebuilding in an infill/rebuild context**. For developers evaluating wildfire reconstruction opportunities, the TJH playbook — standardized designs, guaranteed pricing, production-scale operations — is the model to study. Their willingness to accept penalty risk on delivery timelines signals genuine operational confidence.

\ornsep

## Cross-Reference: Financial Framework for Wildfire Reconstruction

Wildfire rebuilding requires a modified version of the standard development pro forma. Key adjustments for Appendix G's framework:

| Standard Pro Forma Line | Wildfire Modification | App G Level |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Land Acquisition** | Often $0 (homeowner owns lot) or fire-sale pricing | Level 1: Napkin Test land basis fundamentally different |
| **Site Work** | Add $10K–$50K/lot for debris removal + soil remediation | Level 2: new line item |
| **Construction Cost** | +20–40% inflation during peak rebuild labor shortage | Level 3: Stress Test — model labor cost escalation |
| **Insurance Proceeds** | 60–70% of actual rebuilding cost (Paradise data) | Level 2: Sources — insurance gap must be funded |
| **Timeline** | Modular: 4–6 months vs. conventional: 12–18 months | Level 2: Death Point — modular advantage is most pronounced here |
| **WUI Fire-Hardening** | $40K–$80K/home mandatory (CBC Chapter 7A) | Level 2: add to hard costs — non-negotiable |

> **Frontier Builder Connection**: TJH's full case study in Appendix F (§F.1) provides the deep financial analysis of their Palisades rebuild model — $650/SF guaranteed pricing, $400M construction facility, and penalty-backed 12-month delivery guarantee. For developers evaluating wildfire reconstruction at scale, this is the benchmark business model. Aro's volumetric prefab approach (Appendix F, §F.2) offers an alternative for the luxury segment at $1,400/SF. Both models solve the labor-scarcity constraint that is the binding constraint in wildfire zones.

## Key Takeaway

> California wildfire reconstruction is a **massive, recurring market** driven by climate change, aging infrastructure, and development patterns in the WUI. The developers who succeed are those who offer **speed, cost predictability, and fire-hardened construction** — all strengths of modular building. Study Santa Rosa's Coffey Park model for the production-scale approach, and position modular as the solution to the labor shortage that constrains every rebuild.

# Templates & Checklists

\ornsep

## Overview

These templates provide standardized frameworks for the key activities in California development — from initial site screening through entitlement and construction. Adapt them to your specific project and jurisdiction.

\ornsep

## Site Screening Template (3-Minute Quick Screen)

**Instructions**: Complete this screen before visiting any potential site. All data is available online. If any item is a "FATAL FLAW," stop — do not proceed without confirming the constraint is manageable.

```
SITE QUICK SCREEN
===================================================
Project Name: _______________________________________
Address: ____________________________________________
APN: ________________________________________________
Date: _______________________________________________
Screened By: ________________________________________

1. PARCEL DATA (County Assessor GIS)
   Lot Size: _________ SF / _________ acres
   Current Use: _____________________________________
   Owner: ___________________________________________
   Assessed Value: $________________________________

2. ZONING (City/County GIS)
   General Plan Designation: ________________________
   Zoning District: _________________________________
   Overlay Zone(s): _________________________________
   Permitted Uses:   [ ]  Residential   [ ]  Hotel   [ ]  Mixed-Use
   Max Density: _________ units/acre
   Max Height: _________ ft / _________ stories
   Max FAR: _________
   Max Lot Coverage: _________%
   COMPATIBLE WITH PROJECT?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No   [ ]  GP Amendment Needed

3. HAZARD SCREENING
   FEMA Flood Zone: _________ (Source: msc.fema.gov)
    [ ]  Zone X (minimal risk)   [ ]  Zone AE/AH (100-year)   [ ]  Zone VE (coastal)
   -> FATAL FLAW?   [ ]  No   [ ]  Yes — requires flood insurance + elevated construction

   Alquist-Priolo Fault Zone:  [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
   (Source: maps.conservation.ca.gov)
   -> FATAL FLAW?   [ ]  No   [ ]  Yes — no habitable structures within zone

   Liquefaction Zone:  [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
   (Source: maps.conservation.ca.gov)
   -> Budget impact: $8K–$15K/unit for ground improvement

   Fire Hazard Severity Zone:  [ ]  Non-FHSZ   [ ]  Moderate   [ ]  High   [ ]  Very High
   (Source: osfm.fire.ca.gov)
   -> Budget impact: $40K–$80K/unit for WUI fire-hardening

4. ENVIRONMENTAL (Quick Check)
   EnviroStor (within 1,000 ft):  [ ]  Clean   [ ]  RECs found: ___________
   GeoTracker (within 1,000 ft):  [ ]  Clean   [ ]  Sites found: __________
   -> FATAL FLAW?   [ ]  No   [ ]  Potential — requires Phase I/II

5. GO / NO-GO
    [ ]  PROCEED to 10-Minute Deep Dive
    [ ]  PASS — reason: _________________________________
    [ ]  CONDITIONAL — contingent on: ___________________
===================================================
```

\ornsep

## Site Due Diligence Template (Full 11-Step Protocol)

```
COMPREHENSIVE SITE DUE DILIGENCE
===================================================
Project Name: _______________________________________
Address/APN: ________________________________________
Date Started: ____________  Completed: ______________

STEP 1: PARCEL DATA
Source: County Assessor GIS
 [ ]  Parcel boundaries confirmed
 [ ]  Lot size: _________ SF
 [ ]  Lot dimensions: _________ x _________
 [ ]  Corner lot?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Current improvements: _____________________________
 [ ]  Assessed value: Land $_________ Improvements $_________
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 2: GENERAL PLAN & ZONING
Source: City/County GIS + Municipal Code
 [ ]  GP designation: __________________________________
 [ ]  Zoning: __________________________________________
 [ ]  Overlay(s): ______________________________________
 [ ]  Specific Plan area?   [ ]  Yes: ___________   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Max density: _______ du/acre
 [ ]  Max height: _______ ft
 [ ]  Max FAR: _______
 [ ]  Max lot coverage: _______%
 [ ]  Front setback: _______ ft
 [ ]  Side setback: _______ ft
 [ ]  Rear setback: _______ ft
 [ ]  Parking requirement: _______ spaces/unit or /key
 [ ]  GP amendment needed?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Rezoning needed?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 3: FEMA FLOOD ZONE
Source: msc.fema.gov
 [ ]  Zone: ___________
 [ ]  Base Flood Elevation: _______ ft
 [ ]  Flood insurance required?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Floodplain development permit?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 4: EARTHQUAKE FAULT ZONE
Source: maps.conservation.ca.gov
 [ ]  Alquist-Priolo zone?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  If yes: setback from fault trace required
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 5: SEISMIC HAZARDS
Source: maps.conservation.ca.gov
 [ ]  Liquefaction zone?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Landslide zone?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  If yes: geotechnical investigation required
 [ ]  Estimated ground improvement cost: $_________
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 6: FIRE HAZARD SEVERITY ZONE
Source: osfm.fire.ca.gov
 [ ]  FHSZ classification: ____________________________
 [ ]  WUI fire-hardening required?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  If yes: estimated cost premium: $_________/unit
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 7: ENVIRONMENTAL CONTAMINATION
Source: envirostor.dtsc.ca.gov
 [ ]  On-site contamination:  [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Within 1,000 ft:  [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Contaminant(s): __________________________________
 [ ]  Status: __________________________________________
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 8: GROUNDWATER
Source: geotracker.waterboards.ca.gov
 [ ]  On-site cleanup:  [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Within 1,000 ft:  [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Status: __________________________________________
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 9: WETLANDS
Source: fws.gov/program/national-wetlands-inventory
 [ ]  Wetlands on/adjacent to site?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Type: ____________________________________________
 [ ]  If yes: USACE Section 404 permit likely required
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 10: ENVIRONMENTAL JUSTICE
Source: oehha.ca.gov/calenviroscreen
 [ ]  CalEnviroScreen percentile: _______%
 [ ]  SB 1000 EJ policies apply?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Enhanced community engagement required?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
Notes: ______________________________________________

STEP 11: HISTORIC RESOURCES
Source: Local inventory, NRHP, CRHR
 [ ]  On-site historic resource?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Adjacent historic resources?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  If yes: CEQA historic resource evaluation required
Notes: ______________________________________________

SUMMARY
===================================================
Total constraints identified: _______
Fatal flaws: _______ (list: _________________________)
Manageable constraints: _______ (list: _______________)
Estimated constraint mitigation cost: $______________
Recommendation:   [ ]  Proceed   [ ]  Pass   [ ]  Conditional
Conditions: ________________________________________
===================================================
```

FROM: [Applicant Name]
