```
   applicable fees, standards, and requirements in effect
   on the date of this application.

   Signature: _______________________________________

INTERNAL USE ONLY
```

## CEQA Pathway Determination Checklist

```
CEQA PATHWAY DETERMINATION
===================================================
Project Name: _______________________________________
Date: _______________________________________________

1. IS THE PROJECT MINISTERIAL?
    [ ]  SB 35 eligible (multifamily, RHNA-deficient city, objective standards)
    [ ]  SB 9 eligible (duplex/lot split on SFR lot)
    [ ]  ADU by-right (Gov. Code §65852.2)
    [ ]  Other ministerial pathway: _____________________
   -> If YES to any: NO CEQA REQUIRED. Stop here.

2. IS THE PROJECT EXEMPT?
    [ ]  Class 1 — Existing facilities (minor alteration)
    [ ]  Class 3 — New construction, small structures (≤6 units in urban)
    [ ]  Class 32 — Infill development (urban, consistent with GP/zoning,
     no significant effect, adequate utilities)
    [ ]  Common sense exemption (§15061(b)(3))
    [ ]  Other exemption: _______________________________
   -> If YES: File Notice of Exemption. Stop here.
   -> EXCEPTION CHECK: Does any exception apply? (§15300.2)
      [ ]  Location (sensitive environment)
      [ ]  Cumulative impact
      [ ]  Significant effect (unusual circumstances)
      [ ]  Scenic highway
      [ ]  Hazardous waste site
      [ ]  Historical resource
     -> If any exception applies: exemption does NOT apply. Continue.

3. INITIAL STUDY — IMPACT ASSESSMENT
   Can all impacts be mitigated to less than significant?
    [ ]  Yes -> Mitigated Negative Declaration (MND)
     Timeline: 12–18 months
     Cost: $50K–$150K
    [ ]  No -> Environmental Impact Report (EIR) required
     Timeline: 18–36 months
     Cost: $200K–$1M+

4. EIR TYPE (if required)
    [ ]  Project EIR (single project)
    [ ]  Program EIR (general plan, specific plan)
    [ ]  Supplemental/Subsequent EIR (changed conditions)
    [ ]  Addendum (minor modifications to approved project)
    [ ]  Tiered EIR (project tiers from program EIR)

DETERMINATION
===================================================
CEQA Pathway: ______________________________________
Estimated Timeline: ________________________________
Estimated Cost: $___________________________________
Key Impact Areas: __________________________________
===================================================
```

\ornsep

## Financial Decision Framework Quick Reference

For the complete 3-level financial decision framework, see **Appendix G**. Below is a quick-reference summary:

```
FINANCIAL DECISION GATEWAY — QUICK REFERENCE
===================================================

LEVEL 1: 5-MINUTE NAPKIN TEST
 [ ]  Land cost per unit/key: $_______________
 [ ]  Construction cost per SF: $_______________
 [ ]  Total estimated cost: $_______________
 [ ]  Comparable sales/revenue: $_______________
 [ ]  Gross margin > 15%?   [ ]  Yes -> PROCEED   [ ]  No -> KILL

LEVEL 2: DEVELOPMENT PRO FORMA (see App G for full template)
 [ ]  Sources & Uses complete
 [ ]  Revenue projections supported by comps
 [ ]  Returns meet minimum thresholds:
   [ ]  Development margin > 20%
   [ ]  Cash-on-cash > 8%
   [ ]  IRR (5-year) > 15%
 [ ]  DEATH POINT CALCULATED: Month ___
 [ ]  Death Point > financing term?   [ ]  Yes -> KILL   [ ]  No -> PROCEED

LEVEL 3: STRESS TEST & KILL CRITERIA (see App G for 12 criteria)
 [ ]  Construction cost +15%: still viable?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Timeline +6 months: still viable?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Revenue -10%: still viable?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Interest rate +100 bps: still viable?   [ ]  Yes   [ ]  No
 [ ]  Any of 12 Kill Criteria triggered?   [ ]  Yes -> KILL   [ ]  No -> GO

DECISION:   [ ]  GO   [ ]  KILL   [ ]  CONDITIONAL
===================================================
```

> **Full Framework**: See Appendix G for the complete Pro Forma Financial Decision Framework with detailed methodology, worked examples, and all 12 kill criteria.

**Last Updated**: February 2026


# Flowcharts & Decision Trees

\ornsep

## Overview

These decision trees and flowcharts provide step-by-step guidance for the most common decisions in California development. Use them as quick-reference tools during project evaluation and entitlement.

\ornsep

## CEQA Decision Tree

```
START: Is Your Project Subject to CEQA?
|
|--- Is the project ministerial (no discretion)?
|   |--- YES -> NO CEQA REQUIRED
|   |   Examples: SB 35, SB 9, ADU by-right,
|   |   building permits with no discretionary action
|   |
|   \--- NO -> Continue v
|
|--- Does a statutory exemption apply?
|   |
|   |--- SB 35/423, SB 9, SB 684, AB 2011?
|   |   \--- YES -> NO CEQA REQUIRED (ministerial)
|   |
|   |--- AB 130 infill housing exemption? (2025)
|   |   Requirements:
|   |    [ ]  Housing (not hotel/transient lodging)
|   |    [ ]  Urban infill, ≤20 acres
|   |    [ ]  ≥15 du/ac (metro) or ≥10 du/ac (suburban)
|   |    [ ]  ≥2/3 residential
|   |    [ ]  GP OR zoning consistent (only one needed)
|   |    [ ]  Not on protected/hazardous lands
|   |   \--- YES -> CEQA EXEMPT
|   |       Phase I ESA + tribal consultation required
|   |       Timeline: 104–178 days
|   |       See Ch 3, §3.6.2 for full criteria
|   |
|   |--- Other statutory exemption?
|   |   \--- YES -> NO CEQA REQUIRED
|   |       File Notice of Exemption (recommended)
|   |
|   \--- NO -> Continue v
|
|--- Does a categorical exemption apply?
|   |
|   |--- Class 1: Existing Facilities
|   |   (Minor alteration of existing structures)
|   |
|   |--- Class 3: New Construction, Small Structures
|   |   (Up to 6 residential units in urbanized area)
|   |
|   |--- Class 32: Infill Development
|   |   Requirements:
|   |    [ ]  Consistent with GP and zoning
|   |    [ ]  Within city limits, on ≤5 acres
|   |    [ ]  Substantially surrounded by urban uses
|   |    [ ]  No habitat for endangered species
|   |    [ ]  No significant traffic/noise/air/water effects
|   |    [ ]  Adequately served by utilities
|   |
|   |--- YES (exemption applies) ->
|   |   |
|   |   \--- Does any EXCEPTION apply? (§15300.2)
|   |        [ ]  Sensitive environment (wetlands, habitat)
|   |        [ ]  Cumulative impacts
|   |        [ ]  Unusual circumstances -> significant effect
|   |        [ ]  Scenic highway
|   |        [ ]  Hazardous waste site (listed)
|   |        [ ]  Historical resource
|   |       |
|   |       |--- NO exceptions -> EXEMPT
|   |       |   File Notice of Exemption
|   |       |
|   |       \--- YES exception -> NOT EXEMPT -> Continue v
|   |
|   \--- NO -> Continue v
|
|--- SB 131 "NEAR-MISS" CHECK (2025)
|   Does the project miss an exemption by
|   ONLY ONE condition?
|   |
|   |--- YES -> LIMITED FOCUSED CEQA REVIEW
|   |   Analyze ONLY the impacts from that
|   |   single disqualifying condition
|   |   No alternatives analysis required
|   |   No growth-inducing impacts required
|   |   See Ch 3, §3.6.2 for details
|   |
|   \--- NO -> Continue v
|
|--- INITIAL STUDY REQUIRED
|   Evaluate all environmental impact categories
|   |
|   |--- Can ALL impacts be mitigated to
|   |   less than significant?
|   |   |
|   |   |--- YES -> MITIGATED NEGATIVE DECLARATION (MND)
|   |   |   Timeline: 12–18 months
|   |   |   Cost: $50K–$150K
|   |   |   30-day public review period
|   |   |
|   |   \--- NO -> Continue v
|   |
|   \--- Are there potentially significant
|       unmitigable impacts?
|       |
|       \--- YES -> ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT REPORT (EIR)
|           Timeline: 18–36 months
|           Cost: $200K–$1M+
|           45-day public review period
|           Public hearings required
|
\--- FAIR ARGUMENT WARNING
    If ANY substantial evidence of a potentially
    significant effect exists (even one public comment
    with factual basis), an EIR may be required
    regardless of your Initial Study conclusions.
```

\ornsep

## Entitlement Pathway Decision Tree — Residential

```
START: Residential Project in California
|
|--- 1. Is it an ADU or JADU?
|   |--- YES -> ADU BY-RIGHT PATHWAY
|   |   • Gov. Code §65852.2
|   |   • 60-day approval timeline
|   |   • No CEQA, no public hearing
|   |   • No impact fees for <750 SF
|   |   -> DONE (fastest pathway)
|   |
|   \--- NO -> Continue v
|
|--- 2. Is it a duplex or lot split on a
|      single-family lot?
|   |--- YES -> SB 9 PATHWAY
|   |   Requirements:
|   |    [ ]  Existing SFR zoning
|   |    [ ]  Urban lot (not rural/ag)
|   |    [ ]  Not historic district
|   |    [ ]  Not in flood/fire/fault zone*
|   |    [ ]  Meets objective standards
|   |   |
|   |   |--- All met -> MINISTERIAL APPROVAL
|   |   |   • 60-day timeline
|   |   |   • No CEQA, no hearing
|   |   |   • Up to 4 units (lot split + duplexes)
|   |   |
|   |   \--- Not all met -> Continue v
|   |
|   \--- NO -> Continue v
|
|--- 3. Is the jurisdiction SB 35-eligible?
|      (Check HCD determination)
|   |
|   |--- YES -> Does project meet SB 35 requirements?
|   |    [ ]  Multifamily (2+ units)
|   |    [ ]  On site with ≥75% residential
|   |    [ ]  Complies with ALL objective standards
|   |    [ ]  Includes affordable component:
|   |     • 10% affordable (if city missed above-moderate RHNA)
|   |     • 50% affordable (if city missed low-income RHNA)
|   |    [ ]  Pays prevailing wages
|   |    [ ]  Not on environmentally sensitive site
|   |    [ ]  Not on farmland or fire hazard zone
|   |   |
|   |   |--- All met -> SB 35 MINISTERIAL APPROVAL
|   |   |   • <150 units: 90-day review
|   |   |   • 150+ units: 180-day review
|   |   |   • No CEQA, no public hearing
|   |   |   • No subjective design review
|   |   |
|   |   \--- Not all met -> Continue v
|   |
|   \--- NO -> Continue v
|
|--- 4. DISCRETIONARY PATHWAY
|   |
|   |--- Is the project consistent with
|   |   GP and zoning as proposed?
|   |   |
|   |   |--- YES -> Site plan / design review only
|   |   |   \--- CEQA determination (see D.1)
|   |   |
|   |   \--- NO -> What approvals needed?
|   |        [ ]  General Plan amendment
|   |        [ ]  Rezoning
|   |        [ ]  Variance
|   |        [ ]  CUP
|   |        [ ]  Specific plan amendment
|   |       \--- Each requires CEQA + public hearing
|   |
|   \--- Apply SB 330 protections:
|        [ ]  File preliminary application IMMEDIATELY
|        [ ]  Maximum 5 public hearings
|        [ ]  No downzoning during review
|        [ ]  Fees locked at pre-app date
|        [ ]  HAA denial protections apply
|
\--- TIMELINE SUMMARY
    ADU:              60 days
    SB 9:             60 days
    SB 35 (<150):     90 days
    SB 35 (150+):     180 days
    Discretionary:    6–36 months
```

\ornsep

## Entitlement Pathway Decision Tree — Hotel

```
START: Hotel Project in California
|
|--- NOTE: Hotels do NOT qualify for:
|   • SB 35 (residential only)
|   • SB 9 (residential only)
|   • SB 330 protections (residential only)
|   • ADU law (residential only)
|   Hotels follow the FULL DISCRETIONARY pathway.
|
|--- 1. ZONING CHECK
|   |
|   |--- Is hotel a permitted use in the zone?
|   |   |--- YES (by right) -> Site plan review only
|   |   |   Still requires CEQA determination
|   |   |
|   |   |--- YES (with CUP) -> CUP APPLICATION REQUIRED
|   |   |   Most common pathway for hotels
|   |   |
|   |   \--- NO -> Options:
|   |        [ ]  Rezoning required
|   |        [ ]  General Plan amendment required
|   |        [ ]  Find alternative site
|   |       -> Each adds 12–24 months
|   |
|   \--- Continue v
|
|--- 2. BRAND & FRANCHISE (Parallel Track)
|   |
|   |--- Market study complete?
|   |   \--- Required for franchise LOI
|   |
|   |--- Brand selected?
|   |   \--- Determines building program
|   |
|   |--- Franchise LOI executed?
|   |   \--- Critical: LOI has expiration date
|   |       Typical: 12–18 months
|   |       Must align with entitlement timeline
|   |
|   \--- Continue v
|
|--- 3. CEQA PATHWAY (see D.1)
|   |
|   |--- Hotel projects RARELY qualify for:
|   |    [ ]  Class 3 exemption (too large)
|   |    [ ]  Class 32 infill (traffic/noise usually significant)
|   |
|   |--- MOST COMMON: MND or EIR
|   |   |
|   |   |--- Key impact areas for hotels:
|   |   |    [ ]  Traffic (high trip generation)
|   |   |    [ ]  Noise (HVAC, loading, events)
|   |   |    [ ]  Air quality (operations, backup generators)
|   |   |    [ ]  Aesthetics (height, lighting, signage)
|   |   |    [ ]  Parking
|   |   |    [ ]  GHG emissions
|   |   |
|   |   \--- EXCEPTION: Tiering from Program EIR
|   |       If site is within a specific plan or
|   |       master plan area with certified Program EIR,
|   |       hotel may tier -> saves $500K+ and 12+ months
|   |
|   \--- Continue v
|
|--- 4. ABC LICENSING (Parallel Track — Start Month 6)
|   |
|   |--- License type needed?
|   |    [ ]  Type 47 (On-sale general — full bar)
|   |    [ ]  Type 41 (On-sale beer/wine)
|   |    [ ]  Type 57 (Special on-sale general — restaurants)
|   |
|   |--- Transfer vs. new license?
|   |   Transfer: faster, may avoid census restriction
|   |   New: subject to census, may be denied
|   |
|   \--- Timeline: 6–12 months
|       Start early to avoid delaying opening
|
|--- 5. APPROVALS & HEARINGS
|   |
|   |--- Design Review -> Planning Commission
|   |--- CUP Hearing -> Planning Commission
|   |--- EIR Certification -> Planning Commission or City Council
|   |--- Appeal (if filed) -> City Council
|   |
|   \--- Total hearings: typically 3–5
|
\--- 6. BUILDING PERMIT
    |
    |--- Submit construction documents
    |--- Franchise brand review (parallel)
    |--- Fire Marshal review
    |--- Health Department review (food service)
    |
    \--- Timeline: 3–6 months for plan check

TOTAL HOTEL TIMELINE: 24–36 months typical
```

\ornsep

## Site Screening Flowchart

```
START: Potential Development Site Identified
|
|--- PHASE 1: 3-MINUTE SCREEN (Online GIS)
|   |
|   |--- Check parcel size -> Adequate for project?
|   |   \--- NO -> PASS
|   |
|   |--- Check zoning -> Compatible with project?
|   |   \--- NO -> GP amendment needed?
|   |       \--- YES -> Add 12–24 months + cost
|   |
|   |--- Check FEMA flood zone -> Zone AE/VE?
|   |   \--- YES -> FATAL FLAW unless price reflects
|   |
|   |--- Check earthquake fault zone -> Alquist-Priolo?
|   |   \--- YES -> FATAL FLAW (no habitable structures)
|   |
|   \--- PASS 3-MINUTE SCREEN? -> Continue v
|
|--- PHASE 2: 10-MINUTE DEEP DIVE
|   |
|   |--- CGS liquefaction zone?
|   |   \--- YES -> Budget $8K–$15K/unit extra
|   |
|   |--- CAL FIRE FHSZ (high/very high)?
|   |   \--- YES -> Budget $40K–$80K/unit for WUI
|   |
|   |--- EnviroStor (contamination within 1,000 ft)?
|   |   \--- YES -> Budget $100K–$200K for assessment
|   |
|   |--- GeoTracker (groundwater issues)?
|   |   \--- YES -> Evaluate vapor intrusion risk
|   |
|   |--- Wetlands inventory?
|   |   \--- YES -> USACE Section 404 permit needed
|   |
|   \--- PASS 10-MINUTE SCREEN? -> Continue v
|
|--- PHASE 3: 30-MINUTE ANALYSIS
|   |
|   |--- CalEnviroScreen percentile?
|   |   \--- High -> Additional community engagement
|   |
|   |--- Historic resources on/near site?
|   |   \--- YES -> CEQA historic evaluation
|   |
|   |--- SB 35 eligibility (residential)?
|   |   \--- YES -> Ministerial pathway available!
|   |
|   |--- RHNA status of jurisdiction?
|   |   \--- Behind -> City more likely to approve
|   |
|   \--- Preliminary pro forma pencils?
|       \--- NO -> PASS
|
\--- DECISION
    |
    |--- GREEN LIGHT: No fatal flaws, zoning compatible,
    |   economics work -> PROCEED TO LOI/CONTRACT
    |
    |--- YELLOW: Manageable constraints, economics
    |   marginal -> PROCEED WITH CONDITIONS
    |   (price adjustment, contingencies)
    |
    \--- RED: Fatal flaws or economics don't work -> PASS
```

\ornsep

## Density Bonus Decision Tree

```
START: Considering Density Bonus?
|
|--- 1. What affordability level can you provide?
|   |
|   |--- Very Low Income (≤50% AMI)
|   |   5% VLI -> 20% bonus
|   |   10% VLI -> 33.25% bonus
|   |   15% VLI -> 50% bonus (max)
|   |
|   |--- Low Income (≤80% AMI)
|   |   10% LI -> 20% bonus
|   |   20% LI -> 35% bonus
|   |   24% LI -> 43.75% bonus
|   |
|   |--- Moderate Income (≤120% AMI, for-sale only)
|   |   10% Mod -> 5% bonus
|   |   40% Mod -> 35% bonus
|   |
|   \--- 100% Affordable -> 80% bonus
|
|--- 2. CONCESSIONS / INCENTIVES
|   |
|   |--- How many can you request?
|   |   5% VLI or 10% LI -> 1 concession
|   |   10% VLI or 17% LI -> 2 concessions
|   |   15% VLI or 24% LI -> 3 concessions
|   |
|   \--- Common concessions:
|        [ ]  Reduced setbacks
|        [ ]  Reduced parking (0.5/unit minimum)
|        [ ]  Increased height
|        [ ]  Increased FAR
|        [ ]  Reduced open space
|
|--- 3. WAIVERS (Unlimited)
|   |
|   \--- Any standard that physically prevents
|       achieving the bonus density can be waived.
|       City must approve unless it would have
|       a specific adverse health/safety impact.
|       |
|       \--- Example: Height limit prevents building
|           the bonus units -> height waiver granted
|
\--- 4. COMBINED STRATEGY
    |
    |--- Density Bonus + SB 35 = Maximum power
    |   Bonus density + ministerial approval
    |
    |--- Density Bonus + ADU = Additional units
    |   Bonus units + ADU units (not counted)
    |
    \--- Density Bonus + SB 330 = Fee protection
        Bonus density + locked-in fees
```

\ornsep

## Modular vs. Conventional Decision Tree

```
START: Should This Project Use Modular Construction?
|
|--- 1. PROJECT TYPE CHECK
|   |
|   |--- Hotel (select-service, repetitive rooms)?
|   |   \--- STRONG CANDIDATE for modular
|   |
|   |--- Multifamily (studio/1BR/2BR repetitive)?
|   |   \--- STRONG CANDIDATE for modular
|   |
|   |--- Student/senior housing (repetitive units)?
|   |   \--- STRONG CANDIDATE for modular
|   |
|   |--- Custom single-family homes?
|   |   \--- WEAK CANDIDATE (consider panelized instead)
|   |
|   \--- Mixed-use with irregular floor plates?
|       \--- EVALUATE CAREFULLY (hybrid approach may work)
|
|--- 2. SITE ACCESS CHECK
|   |
|   |--- Can modules reach the site?
|   |    [ ]  Road width adequate (min 14 ft clear)?
|   |    [ ]  Turning radius adequate?
|   |    [ ]  Bridge weight limits OK?
|   |    [ ]  No overhead obstructions?
|   |   |
|   |   |--- YES to all -> Continue v
|   |   \--- NO to any -> CONVENTIONAL
|   |       (unless modules can be reduced in size)
|   |
|   |--- Is crane placement feasible?
|   |    [ ]  Crane pad area available?
|   |    [ ]  Adjacent buildings allow boom swing?
|   |   |
|   |   \--- NO -> CONVENTIONAL or PANELIZED
|   |
|   \--- Continue v
|
|--- 3. STRUCTURAL CHECK
|   |
|   |--- Seismic Design Category?
|   |    [ ]  SDC A–D -> Standard modular connections OK
|   |    [ ]  SDC E–F -> Special seismic connections required
|   |       (BRB frames, moment connections)
|   |       -> adds cost but still feasible
|   |
|   |--- Building height?
|   |    [ ]  1–5 stories -> Standard modular
|   |    [ ]  6–12 stories -> Modular with enhanced structure
|   |    [ ]  12+ stories -> Evaluate carefully
|   |       (hybrid: modular units on concrete podium)
|   |
|   \--- Continue v
|
|--- 4. ECONOMICS CHECK
|   |
|   |--- Is labor scarce/expensive in this market?
|   |   \--- YES -> Modular advantage increases
|   |
|   |--- Is schedule critical?
|   |   \--- YES -> Modular saves 40–50% time
|   |
|   |--- Module transport distance?
|   |    [ ]  <200 miles -> Favorable economics
|   |    [ ]  200–500 miles -> Evaluate transport cost
|   |    [ ]  >500 miles -> Transport cost may offset savings
|   |    [ ]  Overseas (shipping container) -> 4–8 week ocean transit
|   |
|   |--- Is there a manufacturer with capacity?
|   |   \--- NO -> Long lead time; evaluate alternatives
|   |
|   \--- Continue v
|
\--- DECISION MATRIX
    |
    |--- VOLUMETRIC MODULAR recommended if:
    |    [ ]  Repetitive room/unit types
    |    [ ]  Site access adequate
    |    [ ]  Schedule is priority
    |    [ ]  Manufacturer available with capacity
    |   -> Expected savings: 10–20% cost, 40–50% schedule
    |
    |--- PANELIZED recommended if:
    |    [ ]  Moderate repetition
    |    [ ]  Site access constrained (no crane)
    |    [ ]  Design flexibility needed
    |    [ ]  Local panel manufacturer available
    |   -> Expected savings: 5–15% cost, 20–30% schedule
    |
    |--- HYBRID recommended if:
    |    [ ]  Tall building (podium + modules)
    |    [ ]  Mixed uses (commercial ground floor + modules above)
    |    [ ]  Complex site (combine conventional + modular)
    |   -> Expected savings: varies by configuration
    |
    \--- CONVENTIONAL recommended if:
         [ ]  Unique/custom design
         [ ]  Site access severely constrained
         [ ]  No manufacturer available
         [ ]  Small project (<10 units)
         [ ]  Complex structural requirements
```

\ornsep

## Permit Streamlining Act (PSA) Timeline Tracker

```
APPLICATION FILED: [DATE]
|
|--- DAY 0 ------------------------------------
|   Application submitted
|
|--- DAY 30 -----------------------------------
|   COMPLETENESS DETERMINATION DUE (§65943)
|   |
|   |--- City determines COMPLETE -> Clock starts
|   |--- City determines INCOMPLETE -> Resubmit
|   |   (new 30-day clock on resubmittal)
|   \--- City MISSES DEADLINE ->
|       Application DEEMED COMPLETE by law
|
|--- CEQA PATHWAY DETERMINES NEXT DEADLINE ----
|   |
|   |--- EXEMPT PROJECT
|   |   \--- 60 days from exemption -> DECISION DUE
|   |       If missed -> DEEMED APPROVED
|   |
|   |--- ND/MND PROJECT
|   |   \--- 60 days from adoption -> DECISION DUE
|   |       If missed -> DEEMED APPROVED
|   |
|   |--- EIR PROJECT
|   |   \--- 180 days from certification -> DECISION DUE
|   |       If missed -> DEEMED APPROVED
|   |
|   |--- SB 35 (<150 units)
|   |   \--- 90 days from submittal -> DECISION DUE
|   |       If missed -> DEEMED APPROVED
|   |
|   \--- SB 35 (150+ units)
|       \--- 180 days from submittal -> DECISION DUE
|           If missed -> DEEMED APPROVED
|
\--- ENFORCEMENT
    |
    |--- Track EVERY deadline in writing
    |--- Send reminder letters at 15 days and 5 days
    |--- If deadline missed:
    |   1. Send formal notice citing §65950/§65913.4
    |   2. Demand approval by operation of law
    |   3. If city refuses -> legal action (mandamus)
    |
    \--- "Deemed approved" is your strongest tool.
        Use it.
```

\ornsep

## Project Evaluation Scorecard

```
PROJECT EVALUATION SCORECARD
===================================================
Rate each factor 1–5 (5 = best)     Score  Weight  Weighted
-------------------------------------------------
SITE FACTORS
  Zoning compatibility               ___    x3     = ___
  No fatal flaws (flood/fault/env)   ___    x3     = ___
  Infrastructure availability        ___    x2     = ___
  Access and visibility              ___    x1     = ___

ENTITLEMENT FACTORS
  Ministerial pathway available      ___    x3     = ___
  Jurisdiction track record          ___    x2     = ___
  Community support likelihood       ___    x1     = ___
  CEQA pathway (exempt = 5)          ___    x2     = ___

FINANCIAL FACTORS
  Land basis (cost/unit or /key)     ___    x3     = ___
  Construction cost competitiveness  ___    x2     = ___
  Revenue potential (rents/RevPAR)   ___    x3     = ___
  Exit market depth                  ___    x2     = ___

EXECUTION FACTORS
  Timeline to revenue                ___    x2     = ___
  Modular/prefab feasibility         ___    x1     = ___
  Team capability                    ___    x1     = ___
-------------------------------------------------
TOTAL WEIGHTED SCORE                         ___  / 155

SCORING GUIDE
  130–155:  Exceptional opportunity — move fast
  100–129:  Strong opportunity — proceed with diligence
   75–99:   Moderate — evaluate carefully, may need conditions
   50–74:   Weak — likely pass unless price reflects risk
    <50:    Pass
===================================================
```

\ornsep

## Financial Decision Gateway Flowchart

```
START: New Development Opportunity
|
|--- LEVEL 1: NAPKIN TEST (5 Minutes)
|   |
|   |--- Land cost / buildable units = Cost per unit
|   |   \--- > Market comp?  -> KILL (overpaying for land)
|   |
|   |--- Estimated total cost per unit
|   |   \--- > 80% of ARV?  -> KILL (insufficient margin)
|   |
|   |--- Estimated gross margin
|   |   \--- < 15%?  -> KILL (margin too thin)
|   |
|   \--- PASS?  -> Continue to Level 2
|
|--- LEVEL 2: DEVELOPMENT PRO FORMA
|   |
|   |--- SOURCES & USES
|   |    [ ]  Land + closing costs
|   |    [ ]  Hard costs (site + foundation + building + FF&E)
|   |    [ ]  Soft costs (A&E + CEQA + legal + permits + fees)
|   |    [ ]  Financing costs
|   |    [ ]  Contingency (10–15%)
|   |   \--- TOTAL DEVELOPMENT COST (TDC)
|   |
|   |--- REVENUE
|   |    [ ]  Residential: rent roll or sale comps
|   |    [ ]  Hotel: ADR x occupancy x keys x 365
|   |   \--- Stabilized NOI
|   |
|   |--- RETURNS
|   |    [ ]  Development margin (profit / TDC)
|   |    [ ]  Cash-on-cash (NOI / equity)
|   |    [ ]  IRR (5-year hold)
|   |    [ ]  Exit value (NOI / cap rate)
|   |
|   |--- DEATH POINT ANALYSIS (§2D of App G)
|   |   Monthly holding cost = $_______
|   |   Total profit = $_______
|   |   Death Point = Profit ÷ Monthly holding cost = _____ months
|   |   \--- Death Point < Financing term?  -> KILL
|   |
|   \--- PASS?  -> Continue to Level 3
|
|--- LEVEL 3: STRESS TEST & KILL CRITERIA
|   |
|   |--- STRESS TEST (vary each input +/- 10-15%)
|   |    [ ]  Construction cost sensitivity
|   |    [ ]  Timeline sensitivity
|   |    [ ]  Revenue/rent sensitivity
|   |    [ ]  Interest rate sensitivity
|   |    [ ]  Exit cap rate sensitivity
|   |
|   |--- 12 KILL CRITERIA (any one = KILL)
|   |   See Appendix G, §3C for full list
|   |   Key triggers:
|   |    [ ]  #3: TDC exceeds realistic exit value
|   |    [ ]  #5: Regulatory denial risk > 30%
|   |    [ ]  #8: Timeline exceeds financing term
|   |    [ ]  #11: Market shift eliminates demand thesis
|   |
|   \--- ALL CLEAR?  -> GO / COMMIT CAPITAL
|
\--- DECISION
    |
    |--- GO — All 3 levels passed
    |   -> Authorize design (after Appendix E pre-design analysis)
    |   -> File SB 330 preliminary application (if housing)
    |   -> Secure franchise LOI (if hotel)
    |
    |--- KILL — Failed at any level
    |   -> Document reason
    |   -> Release site
    |   -> Move to next opportunity
    |
    \--- CONDITIONAL — Marginal at Level 2 or 3
        -> Renegotiate purchase price
        -> Modify program (density, product type)
        -> Re-run Levels 1–3 with new assumptions
```

> **Full Methodology**: See Appendix G for the complete 3-level financial decision framework with worked examples and all 12 kill criteria.

**Last Updated**: February 2026


# Pre-Design Site Constraint Analysis Template

\ornsep

## Overview

This template is designed to be completed **before your architect draws a single line**. It systematically surfaces every constraint that can derail a project — title issues, easement conflicts, zoning violations, physical site limitations, and financial misalignment — so that your design brief reflects reality, not assumptions.

The template is based on lessons learned from a Peninsula residential project that experienced a **3-year permit delay, $800K+ in holding costs, and 5 systemic failures** — all of which could have been prevented by completing this analysis before design.

\ornsep

## How to Use This Template

Complete each section in order. Sections 1–4 should be completed within the **first 4 hours** of site evaluation. If any section reveals a fatal flaw, stop and evaluate whether the project is viable before investing in design.

Each section includes checkboxes, fill-in fields, and a status determination.

| Status | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| **CLEAR** | No constraints found. Proceed to next section. |
| **MITIGATED** | Constraint exists but has a clear, documented path to resolution. Note the mitigation strategy. |
| **FATAL FLAW** | Constraint that cannot be resolved or makes the project economically unviable. STOP. |

**STOP: Do not proceed to design until every section has a status of CLEAR or MITIGATED. Any FATAL FLAW stops the project.**

\ornsep

## Project Information

```
PROJECT INFORMATION
===============================================================
Property Address: _______________________________________________
APN (Assessor Parcel Number): ___________________________________
Lot Size (SF): __________________________________________________
Lot Shape/Configuration: ________________________________________
Current Zoning Designation: _____________________________________
General Plan Land Use: __________________________________________
Overlay Districts: ______________________________________________
Target Use (SFR / MF / Hotel / Mixed): __________________________
Target Gross SF (above grade): __________________________________
Target Basement SF: _____________________________________________
Target ADU SF: __________________________________________________
Total Project SF: _______________________________________________
Number of Stories: ______________________________________________
Analyst Name: ___________________________________________________
Date of Analysis: _______________________________________________
Sources Reviewed: _______________________________________________
```

\ornsep

## Section 1: Title & Easement Analysis

> **LESSON LEARNED**: On a Peninsula residential project, 10-foot PUEs on both sides of a pie-shaped lot were not identified until 18 months and 4 revisions into plan check. This single oversight invalidated the entire design and added 7+ months of delay.

### Preliminary Title Report Review

Order a preliminary title report (PTR) from a title company before any design work begins. Review every exception listed in Schedule B.

-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] Preliminary title report ordered and received
-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] All Schedule B exceptions listed and mapped below
-  [ ]  Title vested in expected owner / entity
-  [ ]  No liens, judgments, or encumbrances that affect development
-  [ ]  Property tax status current

```
Title Company / Order Number: ___________________________________
Date of PTR: ____________________________________________________
Number of Schedule B Exceptions: ________________________________
```

| # | Exception Description | Type | Impact on Buildable Area | Status |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | | | | |
| 2 | | | | |
| 3 | | | | |
| 4 | | | | |
| 5 | | | | |
| 6 | | | | |

### Easement Mapping

**CRITICAL: Every easement must be physically drawn on the site plan before design begins. This is the #1 lesson from the case study referenced above.**

For each easement, identify its type, width, location, and what can/cannot be built within it.

-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] All PUEs (Public Utility Easements) identified and mapped
-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] PUE widths confirmed with utility providers (PG&E, water, sewer)
-  [ ]  All access easements identified and mapped
-  [ ]  All drainage easements identified and mapped
-  [ ]  All private easements / shared driveway agreements identified
-  [ ]  Easement locations confirmed against ALTA survey (not just title report)
-  [ ]  Buildable area recalculated AFTER all easements deducted

| Easement Type | Width | Location | Grantor | Build Restrictions | Confirmed By |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| | | | | | |
| | | | | | |
| | | | | | |
| | | | | | |

### Buildable Envelope Calculation

After mapping all easements AND setbacks, calculate the actual buildable area. This number — not the lot size — drives the design.

| Constraint | Deduction (SF) |
| --- | --- |
| Total Lot Area | |
| Front Setback | |
| Rear Setback | |
| Left Side Setback | |
| Right Side Setback | |
| PUE - Left Side | |
| PUE - Right Side | |
| Access Easement(s) | |
| Drainage Easement(s) | |
| Other Easement(s) | |
| **BUILDABLE AREA (NET)** | |

```
Buildable Area as % of Lot: _______ %
Maximum FAR Allowed: ______________
Maximum Above-Grade SF (Buildable Area x FAR): ______________
Does target project SF fit within buildable envelope?
  [ ] Yes   [ ] No   [ ] Requires Variance
```

> If buildable area is less than 50% of lot size on a residential lot, the project is high-risk for design iteration. Consider whether the economics still work.

**Section 1 Status**: _______________________________________________

**Notes / Mitigation Required**: ___________________________________

\ornsep

## Section 2: Zoning & Development Standards

> **LESSON LEARNED**: On the same Peninsula project, a daylight plane violation on the eave overhang was caught by a new planner months after the previous planner had indicated approval. Daylight planes, height limits, and setbacks must be verified independently — never rely on a single reviewer's verbal confirmation.

### Zoning District Analysis

-  [ ]  [HIGH] Zoning designation confirmed from official zoning map (not Zillow/Redfin)
-  [ ]  General Plan consistency verified
-  [ ]  Permitted uses confirmed (residential / hotel / mixed-use)
-  [ ]  All overlay districts identified (historic, flood, hillside, etc.)

```
Zoning District: ________________________________________________
General Plan Designation: _______________________________________
Overlay Districts: ______________________________________________
Approval Pathway (Ministerial / CUP / Design Review / PD): _____
```

### Development Standards

| Standard | Code Requirement | Project Proposed |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Max FAR (above grade) | | |
| Max Lot Coverage | | |
| Max Height (feet) | | |
| Max Height (stories) | | |
| Front Setback | | |
| Rear Setback | | |
| Left Side Setback | | |
| Right Side Setback | | |
| Daylight Plane - Left | | |
| Daylight Plane - Right | | |
| Daylight Plane - Rear | | |
| Min Lot Area per Unit | | |
| Parking Required | | |
| Basement Exemption from FAR? | | |
| ADU Exemption from FAR? (if < 800 SF) | | |

### Daylight Plane Analysis

**CRITICAL: Daylight planes are one of the most common causes of design rework on Peninsula residential projects. Verify daylight plane compliance for EVERY wall face and every roof overhang before design proceeds.**

-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] Daylight plane calculated for all side and rear setbacks
-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] Eave overhangs checked against daylight plane on every elevation
-  [ ]  Second floor stepped back sufficiently for daylight plane compliance
-  [ ]  Roof ridge height verified within daylight plane at all points

```
Daylight Plane Formula (typically: starts at X ft at setback,
  rises at Y:Z slope): __________________________________________
Most Constrained Elevation: _____________________________________
Maximum Building Height at Most Constrained Point: ______________
```

### Parking & Access

-  [ ]  Required parking spaces calculated
-  [ ]  Driveway width meets code (typically 10-12 ft minimum)
-  [ ]  Driveway does not conflict with easements or PUE
-  [ ]  Guest parking requirement verified
-  [ ]  ADU parking requirement (or exemption) verified

```
Total Parking Required: _________________________________________
Parking Provided: _______________________________________________
```

**Section 2 Status**: _______________________________________________

**Notes / Mitigation Required**: ___________________________________

\ornsep

## Section 3: Pre-Application Conference & Multi-Department Review

> **LESSON LEARNED**: On the Peninsula project, Planning reviewed the design for 18 months without Public Works ever weighing in on the PUE. A single pre-application meeting with all departments present would have surfaced this issue before the first submittal.

### Pre-Application Conference

-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] Pre-application conference requested with city
-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] ALL relevant departments invited: Planning, Public Works, Building, Fire
-  [ ]  Written summary of pre-app sent to all attendees for confirmation
-  [ ]  All constraints identified in pre-app incorporated into design brief

```
Pre-App Meeting Date: ___________________________________________
City Staff Present (names and departments): _____________________
Key Issues Identified: __________________________________________
Follow-Up Actions Required: _____________________________________
```

### Department-Specific Constraints

| Department | Constraints Identified | Resolution Required |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Planning | | |
| Public Works | | |
| Building | | |
| Fire | | |
| Utilities (PG&E) | | |
| Water District | | |
| Sanitary District | | |
| School District | | |
| Stormwater / C.3 | | |

### Staff Continuity Protection

**On the Peninsula project, the assigned planner left the city. The replacement introduced new comments that the previous planner had not raised. Always document every staff interaction in writing.**

-  [ ]  [CRITICAL] After every city meeting, send follow-up email summarizing agreements
-  [ ]  All staff confirmations documented in writing (not just verbal)
-  [ ]  Assigned planner name and contact recorded
-  [ ]  Backup contact identified in case of staff turnover
-  [ ]  All plan check comments responded to in formal response letter with cross-references

```
Assigned Planner: _______________________________________________
Assigned Plan Checker: __________________________________________
Public Works Reviewer: __________________________________________
```

### Entitlement Vesting Strategy

For projects with extended timelines, consider filing an SB 330 preliminary application (if housing) or requesting a development agreement to vest development standards and prevent mid-review rule changes.

-  [ ]  SB 330 applicability evaluated
-  [ ]  SB 330 preliminary application filed (if applicable)
-  [ ]  Development agreement requested (if applicable for large projects)
-  [ ]  Vesting tentative map filed (if subdivision required)

```
Vesting Strategy Selected: _____________________________________
Filing Date: ____________________________________________________
```

**Section 3 Status**: _______________________________________________

**Notes / Mitigation Required**: ___________________________________

\ornsep

## Section 4: Physical Site Constraints

> **LESSON LEARNED**: The Peninsula project site was a pie-shaped lot adjacent to a 2-story apartment (privacy concern) and a public park (access easement). Lot shape, adjacencies, and topography directly affect design feasibility and must be documented before design.

### Lot Configuration & Adjacencies

-  [ ]  Lot shape documented (rectangular, flag, pie-shaped, irregular)
-  [ ]  ALTA/ACSM survey ordered or existing survey reviewed
-  [ ]  All adjacent property uses documented
-  [ ]  Privacy concerns from adjacent properties identified
-  [ ]  Noise sources identified (roads, commercial, park, school)

```
Lot Shape: ______________________________________________________
Frontage Width (feet): __________________________________________
Depth (feet): ___________________________________________________
Narrowest Point (feet): _________________________________________
```

| Direction | Adjacent Use | Height/Stories | Privacy or Impact Concern |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| North | | | |
| South | | | |
| East | | | |
| West | | | |

### Topography & Drainage

-  [ ]  Site topography reviewed (flat, sloped, hillside)
-  [ ]  Drainage patterns identified
-  [ ]  Flood zone status checked (FEMA map)
-  [ ]  Creek/waterway setbacks identified (if applicable)

