| **Turnaround time** | Civil engineering plan check corrections can bottleneck the entire building permit timeline. Fast turnaround on correction responses saves weeks. |

**Critical Deliverables**:

| Deliverable | When Needed | Typical Cost | Chapter Reference |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Preliminary grading feasibility | Phase 1 (due diligence) | $3K–$8K | Ch 14, §14.1 |
| C.3 stormwater preliminary sizing | Phase 2 (pre-design) | $3K–$5K | Ch 14, §14.2 |
| Full grading plan | Phase 3 (with architect) | $15K–$30K | Ch 14, §14.1 |
| Full stormwater management plan | Phase 4 (building permit) | $8K–$20K | Ch 14, §14.2 |
| SWPPP | Phase 5 (before grading) | $5K–$10K | Ch 14, §14.3 |
| Encroachment permit plans | Phase 4 (parallel with BP) | $5K–$10K | — |

\ornsep

### Licensed Land Surveyor — Boundaries, Elevations, and Legal Descriptions

The surveyor defines the **physical and legal reality** of your site. Everything the architect draws, everything the civil engineer grades, and everything the title company insures is based on the surveyor's work.

**Survey Types and When to Order**:

| Survey Type | Purpose | When to Order | Cost | Chapter Reference |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Boundary Survey** | Confirms property lines, monuments, encroachments | Phase 1, Day 3–5 | $3K–$8K | Ch 13, §13.2 |
| **ALTA/NSPS Survey** | Comprehensive survey required by lenders; includes easements, improvements, access | Phase 1, Day 3–5 (with Table A items specified) | $5K–$15K | Ch 13, §13.2 |
| **Topographic Survey** | Existing grades, trees, structures, utilities | Phase 1 (usually combined with boundary) | $3K–$8K | Ch 14, §14.1 |
| **Construction Staking** | Locates building corners, grades, utilities for construction | Phase 5 (after permit, before construction) | $3K–$8K | — |
| **Record of Survey / Corner Record** | Filed with county after subdivision | Phase 6 (if subdivision) | $5K–$10K | Ch 7 |

**ALTA/NSPS Table A Items — What to Request**:

When ordering an ALTA survey, you must specify which optional "Table A" items to include. The standard items cost extra but are essential for development:

| Table A Item | What It Adds | Cost Adder | Importance |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Item 1** | Monument placed or verified | $500–$1K | Essential — confirms physical corners |
| **Item 5** | Exterior dimensions of buildings | $500–$1K | Essential if existing structures |
| **Item 6(a)** | Setback and building lines | $1K–$2K | Critical — shows buildable envelope |
| **Item 8** | Substantial features observed (walls, paths, drives) | $500–$1K | Essential — shows encroachments |
| **Item 11** | Utilities (observed + recorded) | $1K–$2K | Critical — maps utility easements |
| **Item 16** | Evidence of earth moving or building construction | $500 | Important if recent work on site |
| **Item 18** | Offsite easement evidence | $1K–$2K | Important — catches offsite encroachments |
| **Item 19** | Professional liability insurance | Varies | Required by most lenders |

> **Budget Rule**: For any site where you plan to build, order the ALTA survey with Items 1, 5, 6(a), 8, 11, 18, and 19. Total cost: $8K–$20K. This is non-negotiable — the survey is the foundation of every drawing that follows.

\ornsep

### Geotechnical Engineer — What's Underground

The geotech report determines your **foundation type and cost** — which can swing from $20/SF (spread footings on good soil) to $80+/SF (deep piles in liquefiable soil). A $15K geotech report can reveal a $500K foundation cost difference.

**Selection Criteria**:

| Criterion | Why It Matters |
| --- | --- |
| **Local soil knowledge** | Bay Area soils vary wildly — bay mud in East Palo Alto, expansive clay in San Jose, bedrock in the hills. Your geotech should have a library of borings from your target area. |
| **Lab capacity** | Firms with in-house labs return results 2–3 weeks faster than those who outsource. |
| **Structural coordination** | The geotech must communicate directly with your structural engineer. Foundation recommendations that don't translate into buildable structural details cause delays. |

**Deliverable Sequence**:

| Deliverable | When | Cost | What You Learn |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Desktop study** (preliminary) | Phase 0–1 | $1K–$3K | Soil type, liquefaction zone, known hazards |
| **Full geotechnical investigation** | Phase 1 (Day 5–7) | $8K–$20K | Soil borings, lab tests, foundation recommendations |
| **Supplemental investigation** | Phase 3–4 (if design changes) | $3K–$8K | Additional borings if building footprint shifts |
| **Construction observation** | Phase 5 | $5K–$15K (hourly) | Foundation verification, compaction testing |

\ornsep

### Environmental Consultant — Phase I ESA and CEQA

Two distinct scopes that often (but not always) come from the same firm:

**Phase I / Phase II Environmental Site Assessment** (see Ch 14, §14.4):

| When to Order | Cost | What It Tells You |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Phase 1 DD, Day 5–7 (any site with commercial/industrial history or adjacent contamination) | Phase I: $3K–$6K; Phase II: $10K–$30K | Whether the site has recognized environmental conditions (RECs) that require remediation |

**CEQA Documents** (see Ch 5, Ch 6):

| Document | When Needed | Cost | Timeline |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Categorical Exemption memo | Phase 2 (before application) | $2K–$5K | 1–2 weeks |
| Initial Study / MND | Phase 3 (with application) | $15K–$50K | 3–6 months |
| Focused EIR | Phase 3 (after IS identifies significant impacts) | $100K–$300K | 12–18 months |
| Full EIR | Phase 3 (programmatic or complex project) | $200K–$500K+ | 18–36 months |

> **Selection Tip**: CEQA firms are specialized. A firm that writes excellent Phase I ESAs may not write effective CEQA documents, and vice versa. For projects requiring both, verify the firm's CEQA track record separately. Ask: "How many MNDs have you prepared in the last 2 years that were NOT challenged?"

\ornsep

### Architect — The Design Lead

The architect is your **most expensive consultant** (by total fee) and the professional who most directly determines whether the project is buildable, beautiful, and profitable.

**Selection Criteria for Developer Projects**:

| Criterion | Why It Matters |
| --- | --- |
| **Code fluency** | The architect who knows the zoning code, daylight planes, and setbacks as well as a planner will submit plans that pass review faster. |
| **Jurisdiction experience** | Design review criteria vary dramatically — what passes in Redwood City gets redesigned three times in Palo Alto (see Ch 2, §2.5). |
| **Plan check efficiency** | Ask: "What is your average number of plan check correction cycles?" Best architects: 1–2 cycles. Average: 3+. |
| **Construction cost awareness** | Architects who design without cost input produce beautiful, unbuildable projects. Require your architect to coordinate with the GC at every design phase. |
| **Responsiveness to corrections** | Plan check corrections are time-critical (see Ch 16, §16.6 on AB 1114 deadlines). Your architect must turn corrections in days, not weeks. |

**Fee Structures**:

| Project Type | Fee Basis | Typical Range |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SFR Custom | % of construction cost | 10–15% |
| SFR (production / repeated plan) | Flat fee per plan | $15K–$40K |
| Multifamily (5–20 units) | % of construction cost | 8–12% |
| Multifamily (20–100 units) | % of construction cost | 6–10% |
| Hotel (select-service) | % of construction cost | 5–8% (brand prototype reduces scope) |
| Hotel (full-service) | % of construction cost | 7–12% |

\ornsep

## Part 4: Common Mistakes — The "Too Late" Failures

### The 8 Most Expensive Timing Mistakes

Each mistake below is drawn from real Bay Area projects. The cost is not the professional's fee — it is the **project delay and rework** caused by engaging too late.

| # | Mistake | When It Usually Happens | What It Costs | Prevention |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | **No PTR before design** | Architect starts design without reading title exceptions | $50K–$200K in redesign when easements surface | Order PTR Day 1 of DD; read every Schedule B exception |
| 2 | **No geotech before design** | Foundation type assumed, not verified; design doesn't accommodate actual soil conditions | $30K–$150K in foundation redesign | Order geotech borings in first week of DD |
| 3 | **No land use attorney at pre-app** | Developer attends pre-app alone; misses legal leverage points; accepts staff interpretation without challenge | $100K+ in unnecessary entitlement conditions | Land use attorney attends every pre-app conference |
| 4 | **No civil engineer at pre-app** | Public Works issues (PUE, stormwater, grading) surface 12+ months into plan check instead of Day 1 | $200K–$800K (Peninsula project — see App E) | Civil engineer attends pre-app |
| 5 | **No GC pricing during design** | Architect completes CDs; GC prices 30% over budget; 3+ months of value engineering | $50K–$100K redesign cost + 3 month delay | GC provides budget check at SD, DD, and CD milestones |
| 6 | **No CEQA consultant before filing** | Application filed without CEQA strategy; city determines EIR required; 18-month surprise | $100K–$500K+ in EIR cost + 18 month delay | Environmental consultant determines CEQA pathway before application |
| 7 | **No traffic engineer for hotel/large MF** | Traffic impact not studied; opposition files "fair argument" based on traffic; EIR triggered | $100K–$300K EIR cost + 12–18 month delay | Traffic engineer scoping at pre-app for any project >20 units or hotel |
| 8 | **Surveyor not ordered early enough** | ALTA survey takes 4–6 weeks; architect starts design without survey; first floor plan doesn't match lot | 4–8 week delay + $15K–$30K redesign | Order ALTA on Day 3–5 of DD with rush turnaround if needed |

\ornsep

## Part 5: Integrated Professional Fee Budget

### Total Professional Fee Estimates by Project Type

| Professional | SFR / ADU | Small MF (5–20) | Large MF (20–100) | Hotel (Select-Service) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Land Use Attorney | $5K–$15K | $15K–$40K | $30K–$100K | $40K–$120K |
| Transaction Attorney | $3K–$8K | $5K–$15K | $10K–$25K | $15K–$30K |
| Architect | $25K–$75K | $60K–$150K | $150K–$400K | $200K–$500K |
| Civil Engineer | $10K–$30K | $25K–$60K | $40K–$100K | $50K–$120K |
| Structural Engineer | $8K–$20K | $15K–$35K | $30K–$60K | $35K–$70K |
| Surveyor (ALTA + Topo) | $5K–$15K | $8K–$20K | $10K–$25K | $10K–$25K |
| Geotechnical Engineer | $5K–$15K | $8K–$20K | $15K–$30K | $15K–$30K |
| Environmental (Phase I + CEQA) | $3K–$8K | $5K–$25K | $15K–$150K | $20K–$200K |
| Traffic Engineer | $0 | $0–$15K | $15K–$40K | $15K–$50K |
| Arborist | $0–$3K | $0–$5K | $2K–$5K | $2K–$5K |
| GC (Pre-Construction) | $0–$5K | $2K–$10K | $5K–$20K | $10K–$25K |
| Title / Escrow | $2K–$5K | $3K–$8K | $5K–$12K | $5K–$15K |
| Insurance Broker | Minimal | $2K–$5K | $5K–$10K | $5K–$15K |
| CPA / Tax Advisor | $3K–$8K | $5K–$12K | $8K–$20K | $10K–$25K |
| Appraiser | $3K–$5K | $3K–$8K | $5K–$10K | $5K–$10K |
| **TOTAL PROFESSIONAL FEES** | **$72K–$212K** | **$156K–$428K** | **$345K–$1.01M** | **$437K–$1.26M** |
| **As % of Hard Costs** | **12–18%** | **10–15%** | **8–12%** | **7–12%** |

> **Budget Rule of Thumb**: Professional fees (soft costs excluding government fees) typically run **10–15% of hard construction costs** for well-managed projects. If your soft cost ratio exceeds 18%, investigate which professional is over-scoped or which process is inefficient. If it's below 8%, you're probably under-investing in due diligence — which creates downstream cost exposure.

\ornsep

## Practitioner Checklist

### Before LOI (Phase 0)
-  [ ]  Run 3-minute GIS screen yourself — no professionals needed
-  [ ]  Run 5-minute Napkin Test (Appendix G, Level 1) — no professionals needed
-  [ ]  Quick call to land use attorney only if jurisdiction is unfamiliar

### Due Diligence Period (Phase 1)
-  [ ]  Day 1: Order preliminary title report (PTR)
-  [ ]  Day 3–5: Order ALTA/NSPS survey with appropriate Table A items
-  [ ]  Day 5–7: Engage geotechnical engineer for soil borings
-  [ ]  Day 5–7: Engage environmental consultant for Phase I ESA (if warranted)
-  [ ]  Day 7–10: Engage land use attorney for zoning analysis and entitlement strategy
-  [ ]  Day 10–15: Engage transaction attorney for PSA review and entity formation
-  [ ]  Day 10–15: Engage arborist if heritage trees are present
-  [ ]  Day 15–20: Engage civil engineer for preliminary utility/grading feasibility
-  [ ]  Day 20–30: Engage GC for preliminary cost estimate
-  [ ]  Day 20–30: Engage CPA for entity and tax structure advice
-  [ ]  Day 20–30: Engage lender for pre-qualification and term sheet

### Pre-Design (Phase 2)
-  [ ]  Pre-application conference scheduled with ALL departments
-  [ ]  Land use attorney confirmed to attend pre-app
-  [ ]  Civil engineer confirmed to attend pre-app
-  [ ]  Architect engaged for preliminary massing study (AFTER constraints are known)
-  [ ]  Written pre-app summary sent to all attendees for confirmation

### Design & Entitlement (Phase 3)
-  [ ]  Architect has PTR, ALTA survey, geotech report, and zoning analysis BEFORE drawing Line 1
-  [ ]  GC provides budget check at schematic design
-  [ ]  GC provides updated estimate at design development
-  [ ]  Environmental consultant engaged BEFORE application filed
-  [ ]  Traffic engineer scoped (if CUP, hotel, or 20+ units)
-  [ ]  Land use attorney preparing for hearing at least 30 days in advance

### Building Permit (Phase 4)
-  [ ]  Architect coordinates all consultant plans into unified permit set
-  [ ]  Civil engineer responds directly to Public Works plan check comments
-  [ ]  Structural engineer available for plan check conferences
-  [ ]  GC provides final GMP/lump sum before permit issuance

### Construction (Phase 5)
-  [ ]  Geotechnical engineer present at foundation excavation
-  [ ]  Structural engineer performs field observation at framing
-  [ ]  Special inspector engaged for all required inspections
-  [ ]  Land use attorney reviews conditions of approval 30 days before C of O request

\ornsep

## Cross-Reference: Companion Appendices

| Sequence | Appendix | Purpose | Connection |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **1st** | **Appendix G** — Pro Forma Financial Decision Framework | Determines whether to spend money on professionals at all | Phase 0: If Napkin Test fails, stop — don't hire anyone |
| **2nd** | **Appendix H** — Professional Team Assembly (this document) | Tells you WHO to engage and WHEN | Phase 1–6: This appendix |
| **3rd** | **Appendix E** — Pre-Design Site Constraint Analysis | The template your professionals fill out | Phase 1–2: Survey, geotech, zoning analysis populate Appendix E |
| **4th** | **Appendix F** — Frontier Builder Case Studies & AI-Agile Strategies | AI tools that augment (not replace) professional services | Phase 0–3: Symbium reduces attorney zoning research; Testfit reduces architect iterations |

> **Workflow**: Run Appendix G's Napkin Test. If it passes, engage Phase 1 professionals per this appendix. Their deliverables populate Appendix E's Pre-Design Site Constraint Analysis. Feed the findings back into Appendix G's Level 2 Pro Forma. Use Appendix F's AI tools to accelerate every professional's work — not to skip their engagement.

\ornsep

## Key Takeaway

> The development team is not a cost center — it is a **risk reduction engine**. Every professional listed in this appendix exists because skipping their work creates downstream costs that are 10x–100x their fee. The $500 preliminary title report prevents the $200K redesign. The $3K land use attorney phone call prevents the $100K entitlement condition. The $15K geotechnical report prevents the $500K foundation surprise. **The cheapest professional engagement is the one that happens on time. The most expensive is the one that happens too late.**


# Bay Area Jurisdiction Profiles

**Developer's Quick Reference for Santa Clara County & San Mateo County**

28 Jurisdictions — Entitlement Pathways, Timelines, Design Review & Strategic Assessment

\ornsep

## How to Use This Appendix

This appendix provides a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction reference for every city in Santa Clara County (South Bay) and San Mateo County (Peninsula) that a developer is likely to target. Each profile answers four questions:

1. **Can I build here?** — Zoning framework, General Plan constraints, Housing Element status
2. **How fast?** — Typical entitlement timeline, ministerial pathway availability (SB 35, SB 9, ADU)
3. **How hard?** — Design review process, political environment, CEQA exposure
4. **Is it worth it?** — Land cost context, fee levels, strategic developer assessment

### Reading the Profiles

Each city profile uses a standardized format:

- **Developer Difficulty Rating**: 1 (easiest) to 5 (most difficult) — a composite score reflecting design review burden, political opposition risk, entitlement timeline, and regulatory complexity
- **SB 35 Eligible**: Whether the city is subject to SB 35/SB 423 streamlining (most Bay Area cities are, as few have met their RHNA targets across all income categories)
- **Housing Element Status**: 6th Cycle (2023–2031) HCD certification status
- **Cross-References**: Links to case studies and detailed analysis elsewhere in this book

### Important Caveats

- Regulatory environments change. Housing Element status, SB 35 eligibility, and fee schedules are current as of **February 2026**. Always verify with the jurisdiction before committing capital.
- All Bay Area cities are subject to **SB 330** (Housing Crisis Act) protections through 2036, including the preliminary application fee-lock and the prohibition on downzoning.
- **SB 423** (2023) extended and expanded SB 35 streamlining through 2036.

\ornsep

## Regional Overview: Two Counties, Two Development Cultures

### Santa Clara County — The Innovation Corridor

Santa Clara County (population ~1.95M) is the economic engine of Silicon Valley. Development culture varies dramatically from north to south:

- **North County** (Palo Alto, Mountain View, Sunnyvale, Santa Clara, Cupertino): High land values ($3M–$15M/acre), intense design review, sophisticated NIMBY opposition, but strong state-law override opportunities (SB 35, density bonus)
- **Central County** (San Jose, Campbell, Los Gatos, Saratoga, Monte Sereno): San Jose offers scale and relative speed; smaller cities are increasingly restrictive
- **South County** (Morgan Hill, Gilroy): Lower land costs, faster approvals, but limited market depth and infrastructure constraints

**6th Cycle RHNA Allocation**: 129,577 units across all jurisdictions (2023–2031)

### San Mateo County — The Peninsula Premium

San Mateo County (population ~760K) commands some of the highest land values in the nation. The development culture is characterized by:

- **Mid-Peninsula** (Menlo Park, Redwood City, San Carlos, Belmont): Active development pipeline, particularly along El Camino Real corridor. Menlo Park and Redwood City are the most developer-friendly
- **North County** (Millbrae, San Mateo, Foster City): Transit-oriented development opportunities near BART and Caltrain
- **Coastside** (Half Moon Bay): Coastal Act overlay creates a fundamentally different regulatory environment
- **Wealthy Enclaves** (Atherton, Hillsborough, Woodside, Portola Valley): SFR-dominated, politically hostile to density, but SB 9 creates limited by-right opportunities

**6th Cycle RHNA Allocation**: 46,418 units across all jurisdictions (2023–2031)

\ornsep

## Santa Clara County Jurisdiction Profiles

### Palo Alto

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~68,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 5/5 (Highest) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes — significantly behind RHNA targets |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted; HCD certified 2023 (late) |
| **Design Review** | Architectural Review Board (ARB) — Very High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–36 months (discretionary); 5–6 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | RM-30/40 (multifamily), CN/CS (commercial), PC (planned community — 43 unique zones) |
| **RHNA Target** | 6,086 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $5M–$15M/acre (among highest in nation) |

**Strategic Assessment**: Palo Alto is the most challenging development jurisdiction on the Peninsula, with a 50-ft General Plan height cap (Chapter 1, Case 2), the most complex zoning code in the county (43 PC zones — Chapter 2, Case 1), and an engaged opposition base that has defeated multiple hotel projects (Chapter 8, Case 3). However, SB 35 has forced ministerial approvals the city would never have granted voluntarily (Chapter 3, Case 1: 3709 El Camino Real). Density bonus waivers override the 50-ft height limit (Chapter 4, Case 1). The city has issued 400+ ADU permits (Chapter 4, Case 3), making ADUs a viable infill strategy.

**Developer Playbook**: Use SB 35 for multifamily with affordable component. Use density bonus for height waivers. Avoid discretionary hotel projects. ADU and SB 9 are viable for SFR lot acquisition strategy.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 1 Case 2, Ch 2 Case 1, Ch 3 Cases 1 & 3, Ch 4 Cases 1 & 3, Ch 5 Case 1, Ch 6 Case 2, Ch 7 Case 1, Ch 13 Case 2, Ch 14 Case 3, Ch 15 Case 1, Ch 16 Case 1

\ornsep

### Mountain View

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~82,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified May 2023 (2nd in Santa Clara County) |
| **Design Review** | Development Review Committee — Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 12–18 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R3 (multifamily), P (precise plan areas), MXD (mixed-use) |
| **RHNA Target** | 11,135 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $3M–$10M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Mountain View is one of the most developer-friendly cities in the county. The North Bayshore Precise Plan (Chapter 1, Case 3) unlocks up to 9,850 new residential units in a former office-only area — one of the largest single-plan rezonings in Bay Area history. Google's Middlefield Park master plan adds thousands more units. Staff-level design review for projects consistent with precise plan standards. SB 330 fee savings have reached $935K per project (Chapter 16, Case 3).

**Developer Playbook**: Target North Bayshore and East Whisman precise plan areas for large-scale multifamily. El Camino Real corridor for mixed-use. Modular construction is viable for repetitive unit types (Chapter 10 — Aro Homes prototype built here, Appendix F).

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 1 Case 3, Ch 2, Ch 4, Ch 9, Ch 10, Ch 15, Ch 16 Case 3, App F

\ornsep

### Sunnyvale

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~155,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified 2023 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission / Staff-level — Low-Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 10–18 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-3/R-4 (multifamily), DSP (Downtown Specific Plan), M-S (Moffett Park) |
| **RHNA Target** | 11,996 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $3M–$8M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Sunnyvale is among the fastest-processing cities in the county. The **Moffett Park Specific Plan** (unanimously adopted July 2023) allows up to **20,000 housing units** with 15–20% affordable and 226 acres of open space — one of the largest single-plan housing opportunities in the Bay Area. Downtown Specific Plan enables higher density along transit corridors. The city processes SB 9 + ADU combinations that yield 8 units from a single SFR lot (Chapter 4, Case 4). Hotel development is viable — the Marriott AC Hotel positions well here (Chapter 9, Chapter 11). SB 330 fee savings of $720K documented (Chapter 16, Case 3).

**Developer Playbook**: Moffett Park for large-scale mixed-use. Downtown for mid-rise multifamily. SB 9 + ADU combo for SFR-to-8-unit conversion. Hotel development along El Camino / Mathilda corridor.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 2, Ch 4 Case 4, Ch 9, Ch 11, Ch 16 Case 3, App B

\ornsep

### Cupertino

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~60,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 4/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes — significantly behind RHNA targets |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted; HCD certified 2024 (late, after state pressure) |
| **Design Review** | Design Review Committee + Planning Commission — High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–30 months (discretionary); 5–6 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-3 (multifamily), BQ (Bubb Road Quasi-Public), Heart of the City Specific Plan |
| **RHNA Target** | 4,588 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $4M–$12M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Cupertino has historically been one of the most resistant cities to new housing development in the Bay Area. The city faced HAA enforcement actions from HCD (Chapter 3, Case 4) for denying housing projects that complied with objective standards. The Vallco/The Rise project (former Vallco Shopping Mall) became a statewide symbol of SB 35's power when the developer invoked streamlining for a 2,402-unit mixed-use project that the city had repeatedly blocked. Despite political resistance, state law now forces approvals. The Heart of the City Specific Plan along Stevens Creek Blvd creates the primary development corridor.

**Case Study: Mission Development Group** — Mission Development Group (missiondevel.com) has completed 50+ luxury SFR projects in Cupertino, making them the most active single-family developer in the city. Their portfolio of addresses across Palo Vista Road, Judy Avenue, Heatherwood Drive, and throughout Cupertino's west side demonstrates that even in a high-difficulty jurisdiction, experienced local developers who understand the design review process can maintain consistent production. Their model — acquire existing SFR lots, demolish and rebuild luxury homes ($3M–$6M price points) — navigates Cupertino's design review by maintaining neighborhood-compatible massing while maximizing lot coverage. They also operate in Menlo Park, Los Gatos, Redwood City, and Sunnyvale (385–387 N. Sunnyvale Ave), showing a Peninsula-wide strategy. For developers considering SB 9 lot splits or ADU additions in Cupertino, Mission Development's portfolio demonstrates the achievable per-lot economics.

**Developer Playbook**: SB 35 for larger multifamily projects (the Vallco precedent shows it works). Heart of the City Specific Plan for commercial-to-residential conversion. SFR luxury teardown-rebuild (Mission Development model) remains profitable despite high design review burden. SB 9 lot splits viable on larger lots in R-1 zones.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 1, Ch 2, Ch 3 Case 4, Ch 4, App F (TJH)

\ornsep

### San Jose

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~1,035,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified 2023 |
| **Design Review** | Director-level / Planning Commission — Low-Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 8–18 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-M (multiple residence), CG (commercial general), Downtown zones, Urban Village overlay |
| **RHNA Target** | 62,200 units (2023–2031) — largest in county |
| **Land Cost** | $1.5M–$6M/acre (varies widely by submarket) |

**Strategic Assessment**: San Jose is the largest city in the Bay Area and processes more permits by volume than any other jurisdiction. The city's Urban Village strategy concentrates density at 70+ designated village locations along transit corridors. Entitlement timelines are moderate, and political opposition is less organized than in Peninsula cities. However, geotechnical conditions require attention — the Valley floor features liquefaction zones and expansive clay soils that add $5K–$15K per unit in foundation costs (Chapter 13, Case 3). CEQA "fair argument" challenges are a real risk on larger projects (Chapter 5, Case 3). The Downtown area is undergoing massive transformation with Google's Diridon Station project.

**Developer Playbook**: Urban Village sites for mixed-use density. Downtown for high-rise. North San Jose for large-scale multifamily. Always budget for geotechnical surprises (Chapter 13). SB 330 fee savings documented at $720K (Chapter 16, Case 3). CEQA risk is real — budget for MND at minimum.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 5 Case 3, Ch 10, Ch 13 Cases 1 & 3, Ch 15, Ch 16, App B

\ornsep

### Santa Clara

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~130,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified May 2024 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission / Staff-level — Low-Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 10–18 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-3/R-4 (multifamily), PD (planned development), TEC (thoroughfare commercial) |
| **RHNA Target** | 11,632 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $2M–$8M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Santa Clara is developer-friendly with significant redevelopment potential, particularly along El Camino Real and in the mixed-use zones near Santa Clara University and Levi's Stadium. The **Tasman East Specific Plan** is delivering 4,500+ homes in a former industrial area near Levi's Stadium — one of the largest active residential pipelines in the county. The city adopted SB 684/SB 1123 ministerial review pathways for starter home projects (up to 10 units), enabling streamlined approvals for smaller infill. Processing times are reasonable and design review is not a significant barrier. Valley soil conditions require attention — liquefaction risk and expansive clay are common (Chapter 13, Chapter 15 GIS screening). The city has embraced the GIS-based planning tools described in Chapter 15, making preliminary site screening efficient. Industrial-to-residential conversion opportunities exist along the Great America corridor.

**Developer Playbook**: El Camino Real corridor for mixed-use. Tasman East for large-scale residential (4,500+ units in pipeline). Great America area for redevelopment. SB 684/SB 1123 for starter home ministerial projects. Industrial conversion opportunities near Caltrain stations. Budget for geotechnical work on any Valley floor site.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 5, Ch 13, Ch 15, App B

\ornsep

### Milpitas

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~80,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified May 2023 (1st in Santa Clara County) |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission with Housing Opportunity District (HOD) objective standards — Low-Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 8–16 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35/ministerial in HOD zones) |
| **Key Zoning** | NCMU1-3 (neighborhood commercial mixed-use), TC1-3 (town center), Metro Specific Plan, Transit Area Specific Plan |
| **RHNA Target** | 6,713 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $2M–$6M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Milpitas is the most active development market in Santa Clara County outside San Jose. First to certify its Housing Element, the city adopted Housing Opportunity Districts (HOD) in April 2025 with six distinct zones and objective design standards, enabling ministerial review for conforming projects. The Metro Specific Plan area (437 acres, 7,109 dwelling units, 993,843 SF office, 340 hotel rooms) is one of the largest redevelopment opportunities in the Bay Area. The BART Milpitas station has catalyzed transit-oriented development, including "The Fields" resort-style development with 142,000 SF retail and a Virgin Hotel. SummerHill's 694-apartment project near Great Mall demonstrates institutional developer interest. 100% affordable housing projects are exempt from non-residential requirements in HOD zones.

**Developer Playbook**: Metro Specific Plan area for large-scale mixed-use (7,109 units planned). HOD zones for ministerial-track multifamily. BART station area for transit-oriented density. Industrial conversion along McCarthy/Montague corridor. Best value-to-density ratio in the county. Geotechnical due diligence essential — Bay margin soils.

\ornsep

### Campbell

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~43,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified May 2023 (1st in Santa Clara County, 14th in Bay Area) |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission with adopted objective design standards — Low-Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 8–16 months (discretionary); 3–6 months (SB 35/ministerial) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-M (multiple family), C-2 (general commercial), P-D (planned development), Downtown overlay, Winchester Station TOD |
| **RHNA Target** | 2,977 units (2023–2031); city has identified capacity for 6,000+ |
| **Land Cost** | $2.5M–$7M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Campbell was the first city in Santa Clara County to achieve HCD certification and won the APA California Northern Section Planning Agency Award of Excellence for its integrated Housing Element approach. The city has proactively adopted multi-family objective design standards to facilitate ministerial review, and is pursuing the state Prohousing Designation. The Winchester Station TOD area (VTA light rail) is a prime development site — a 90-unit 100% affordable project at 2400 Winchester Blvd is in construction. City Ventures' 173-home development at 700 W. Hamilton Ave (84 townhomes, 84 condos, 5 tiny homes) demonstrates the city's receptiveness to innovative housing types. The city is also implementing SB 684 for starter home projects (up to 10 units with streamlined ministerial approval).

**Developer Playbook**: Winchester Station TOD for transit-oriented multifamily. Downtown Campbell for boutique mixed-use. Hamilton Ave corridor for larger projects. Objective design standards enable ministerial review for conforming projects. One of the most proactive planning environments in the county.

\ornsep

### Los Gatos

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~33,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 4/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified 2023 (after HCD revision requests) |
| **Design Review** | Architecture and Site Review Committee — High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–30 months (discretionary); 5–6 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | HR (hillside residential), RM (multi-family residential), C-2 (commercial), CBD (central business district) |
| **RHNA Target** | 1,993 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $4M–$12M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Los Gatos is an affluent hillside community with strong neighborhood preservation politics and a highly engaged Architecture and Site Review Committee. Downtown Los Gatos along Santa Cruz Ave and N. Santa Cruz Ave has limited mixed-use potential, but the town's resistance to change makes discretionary projects unpredictable. TJH (Thomas James Homes, Appendix F) has been active here with luxury SFR development, and Mission Development Group has completed projects in Los Gatos. Hillside development triggers additional geotechnical, drainage, and environmental review. The town's RHNA allocation is modest but still represents a significant increase for a small jurisdiction.

**Developer Playbook**: SB 35 for any affordable-inclusive multifamily. Downtown mixed-use if design quality is exceptional. Luxury SFR teardown-rebuild following the TJH/Mission Development model. Avoid hillside sites unless budget allows for extended entitlement.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 1, Ch 4, App F (TJH)

\ornsep

### Saratoga

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~30,500 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 3/5 (recently improved from 4/5) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified July 2024 (after RHNA appeal denied) |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission + Heritage Preservation Commission — High difficulty, BUT new ministerial pathway adopted January 2025 |
| **Typical Timeline** | 12–24 months (discretionary); ministerial for projects meeting objective standards |
| **Key Zoning** | R-1 (single family, dominant), R-M (limited multifamily), C-N/C-V (commercial), Saratoga Village |
| **RHNA Target** | 1,712 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $4M–$10M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Saratoga is in transition. In January–February 2025, the city adopted **new objective design standards and a ministerial review process** for projects meeting established criteria (including anything over 250 SF) — a landmark shift from the previous fully discretionary system. The Planning Commission approved 4-3 and City Council approved 4-1, indicating significant internal division and community controversy. Residents must now "opt in" to receive notification about new housing projects (a contested change from the old notification system). The Heritage Preservation Commission (heritage trees, historic landmarks, heritage lanes) still adds review complexity in certain areas. The Saratoga Village area is developing new objective design standards (initiated early 2025). SB 684/SB 1123 eligibility for starter home projects (up to 10 units) provides an additional pathway.

**Developer Playbook**: The new ministerial pathway is a game-changer — projects meeting objective standards can receive approval without Planning Commission input. SB 9 and ADU development in R-1 zones. Saratoga Village for small-scale mixed-use. Heritage Preservation overlay adds time in historic areas. Monitor community reaction to new policies — political reversal is possible.

\ornsep

### Monte Sereno

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~3,500 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 5/5 (Highest) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes (50% affordability threshold) |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted May 2024; HCD certification pending as of 2026 |
| **Design Review** | 5-member Site and Architectural Commission (SAC, meets 1st Wednesday monthly) — Very High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–36 months (discretionary) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-1 (single family only — entire city); 2 parcels identified for SB 10 rezone (up to 10 units each) |
| **RHNA Target** | 193 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $5M–$15M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Monte Sereno is one of the smallest and wealthiest cities in California, zoned entirely R-1. The five-member Site and Architectural Commission (SAC) reviews and regulates the aesthetic and functional aspects of all structures — an exceptionally stringent process for a city of 3,500 people. The city's Housing Element was one of the last in Santa Clara County to be adopted (May 2024) and still awaits full HCD certification. The SB 10 rezoning of just two parcels has been in process since 2024, with SAC consideration not until December 2025 and City Council not until 2026 — illustrating the pace of change here. The city appealed its RHNA allocation (denied). ADU/JADU development on existing estates is the city's primary strategy for meeting affordable housing objectives.

**Developer Playbook**: Luxury SFR only. SB 9 lot splits for duplexes on qualifying lots. The two SB 10 rezone parcels may present narrow multifamily opportunity. Not recommended for developers seeking scale or predictable returns.

\ornsep

### Los Altos

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~31,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 4/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted; HCD certified 2024 (late) |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 16–28 months (discretionary); 5–6 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R1 (dominant — single family), CRS (retail/service commercial), CD (downtown commercial) |
| **RHNA Target** | 1,958 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $5M–$12M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Los Altos is a predominantly single-family community that has historically permitted very little multifamily development. The downtown area along Main Street and State Street has limited commercial-to-mixed-use conversion potential. Livio Building Systems (Appendix F) is headquartered here. The city's large RHNA allocation relative to its size creates significant state-law pressure. Some development opportunity exists along El Camino Real at the city's eastern edge and on First Street. SB 9 and ADU development are the most practical paths for small-scale developers.

**Developer Playbook**: SB 35 for any parcels with multifamily zoning. Downtown mixed-use if compliant with objective standards. SB 9 + ADU on R-1 lots. El Camino Real frontage parcels for density.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 4, Ch 10, App F (Livio)

\ornsep

### Los Altos Hills

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~8,500 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 5/5 (Highest) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted; HCD certification contested — Twin Oaks site controversy |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — Very High difficulty (1-acre minimum lots) |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–36 months (discretionary) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-A (residential-agricultural, 1-acre minimum lot size) |
| **RHNA Target** | 489 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $5M–$20M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Los Altos Hills is a rural-residential community with 1-acre minimum lot sizes and no commercial zones. The town's entire character is defined by estate-style living on large wooded lots in the foothills. There is no sewer system — all properties use septic. This creates a fundamental infrastructure constraint for any density. The **Twin Oaks site** controversy is illustrative: the town designated this site for multifamily housing to meet RHNA, but community opposition has been fierce and HCD certification of the Housing Element remains contested and uncertain. The RHNA allocation of 489 units is a practical impossibility under current infrastructure conditions without massive sewer extension investment. SB 9 can theoretically create duplexes, but septic system capacity limits even this pathway.

**Developer Playbook**: Luxury estate SFR only. Not a viable jurisdiction for any density-oriented strategy. SB 9 constrained by septic infrastructure. Monitor Twin Oaks site — if approved, it may be the only multifamily opportunity in the jurisdiction.

\ornsep

### Gilroy

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~61,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes (50% affordability threshold for SB 423 due to income-level shortfalls) |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified August 2023 (adopted with 2040 General Plan) |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — Low difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 8–14 months (discretionary); 3–4 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R3 (multifamily), PUD (planned unit development), Glen Loma Ranch Specific Plan, C (commercial), M (industrial) |
| **RHNA Target** | 1,773 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $500K–$2M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Gilroy is the southernmost city in Santa Clara County and offers the lowest land costs in the region. The city adopted its Housing Element simultaneously with its 2040 General Plan, signaling proactive growth management. The **Glen Loma Ranch Specific Plan** (359 acres, 1,467 residential units) is the largest active master-planned community in the county — in early 2025, Planning Commission approved two new tentative maps for 42 duet lots and 84 SFR lots. Agriculture-to-residential conversion (Chapter 2, §2.2) is a significant opportunity. Note: SB 423 streamlining requires 50% affordability in Gilroy due to income-level RHNA shortfalls — market-rate streamlining requires traditional entitlements. The challenge is market depth: rents and sale prices are substantially lower than North County, so projects must achieve scale.

**Developer Playbook**: Glen Loma Ranch for entitled subdivision parcels. Greenfield development on agricultural conversion sites along Monterey Road. Traditional entitlements more practical than SB 423 for market-rate. Good for developers who can achieve scale (50+ units) at lower land basis.

\ornsep

### Morgan Hill

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~46,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified November 2023 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — Low-Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 8–16 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-3 (multifamily), MU (mixed-use), Downtown overlay (reduced 10% BMR), CC (community commercial) |
| **RHNA Target** | 1,037 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $1M–$3M/acre |
| **Inclusionary** | 15% BMR citywide; 10% BMR in Downtown |

**Strategic Assessment**: Morgan Hill sits between Gilroy and San Jose along the US-101 corridor and offers a middle ground between South County affordability and North County market depth. A critical development: **SB 330 suspended the city's voter-approved Measure S growth control ordinance**, removing the annual housing permit cap and population cap that had constrained development for years. The downtown area has a developing mixed-use identity with a reduced 10% BMR requirement (vs. 15% citywide). Good school districts support SFR and family-oriented multifamily demand. The Monterey Road and Cochrane Road corridors provide the primary development opportunities.

**Developer Playbook**: Downtown mixed-use infill (10% BMR advantage). Monterey Road corridor multifamily. Agricultural conversion at city edges. SFR subdivision on entitled lots. Measure S suspension under SB 330 is a game-changer for permitting velocity.

\ornsep

## San Mateo County Jurisdiction Profiles

### Menlo Park

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~35,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 3/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted; compliance status under HCD review (overlay zoning challenge) |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 12–24 months (discretionary); 4–6 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-3/R-4 (multifamily), SP-ECR (El Camino Real specific plan), O (office — Meta campus), MP (Marsh/Bayfront) |
| **RHNA Target** | 2,946 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $4M–$12M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Menlo Park punches far above its size in development significance. The Meta (Facebook) campus expansion required a General Plan amendment (Chapter 1, Case 1) and has catalyzed a mixed-use transformation of the Bayfront area. The El Camino Real Specific Plan creates a clear development corridor with height allowances up to 60 feet. The citizenM Menlo Park hotel (Chapter 12, Case 1) is the definitive modular construction reference in this book — 160+ factory-built modules assembled in 2 months. SB 9 lot splits have been successful (Chapter 3, Case 2: 4 units from 1 SFR lot). The city's Housing Element compliance is uncertain due to HCD challenges to overlay zoning. Menlo Park has $1.4M geotechnical surprise risk in Bay-margin soils (Chapter 13, Case 1) and 12% site area loss to C.3 stormwater (Chapter 14, Case 1).

**Developer Playbook**: El Camino corridor for mixed-use multifamily. Bayfront area for large-scale projects (if consistent with master plan). SB 9 in Belle Haven and east-side neighborhoods. Hotel development viable with master plan EIR tiering (Chapter 6, Case 1). Budget heavily for geotechnical and stormwater.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 1 Case 1, Ch 2 Case 2, Ch 3 Case 2, Ch 4 Case 2, Ch 5 Case 2, Ch 6 Case 1, Ch 7 Case 2, Ch 8 Case 2, Ch 9 Case 1, Ch 10 Case 1, Ch 12 Case 1, Ch 13 Case 1, Ch 14 Case 1, Ch 15 Case 2

\ornsep

### Redwood City

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~84,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified 2023 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission / Staff-level — Low-Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 8–16 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | MU (mixed-use downtown), R-3/R-4 (multifamily), CG (general commercial), Precise Plan areas |
| **RHNA Target** | 4,588 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $3M–$8M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Redwood City is the most developer-friendly city on the Peninsula. The Downtown Precise Plan (Chapter 2, Case 3) enables by-right development with clear standards, and the city has exceeded its previous RHNA cycle targets (2,935 units permitted vs. 2,789 target). SB 35 applications process in approximately 5 months — this is the "Developer A" reference case in the Introduction ($2.8M Question). The city's zoning has been simplified to reduce discretionary review. A time-sensitive opportunity: the city launched a **temporary Affordable Housing Overlay (AHO) incentive program** offering a 25% reduction in affordable housing requirements for projects that secure entitlements by June 2026 — a significant cost savings for developers who can move quickly. Modular hotel development is viable here (Chapter 11, Case 3 identifies the Peninsula modular gap). The city is a strong recommendation for developers seeking Peninsula land values with North County processing speed.

**Developer Playbook**: Downtown Precise Plan for by-right mixed-use. El Camino corridor for multifamily. **Time-sensitive**: AHO incentive program (25% affordable reduction) expires June 2026. SB 35 as primary pathway. Modular hotel opportunity is strong. Mission Development Group also operates here — SFR luxury market is active.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 1, Ch 2 Case 3, Ch 6 Case 3, Ch 11 Case 3, Ch 16 Case 1, App B

\ornsep

### San Carlos

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~30,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 3/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified 2023 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 12–24 months (discretionary); 4–6 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | MU/DMU (mixed-use downtown), R-3 (multifamily), CC (central commercial) |
| **RHNA Target** | 2,735 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $3M–$8M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: San Carlos is best known in this book as the site of the Hotel Indigo case study — a 188-key hotel that required a full EIR and 2-year entitlement timeline (Chapter 8, Case 1), with $10.3K/key in impact fees (Chapter 16, Case 2) and $150K in environmental assessment costs (Chapter 14, Case 2). The city's downtown is walkable and attractive, with mixed-use redevelopment potential along Laurel Street and El Camino Real. A significant development: the city adopted **new Objective Design Standards (ODS) in November 2025**, enabling staff-level administrative approvals for conforming residential projects — a major shift that reduces both timeline and political risk. The design review process is professional but thorough for non-ODS projects. For residential projects, both the new ODS ministerial track and SB 35 streamlining provide faster pathways than the discretionary process that the Hotel Indigo endured.

**Developer Playbook**: New ODS (November 2025) enables staff-level approval for conforming projects — use this as the primary pathway. SB 35 for affordable-inclusive multifamily. Downtown mixed-use on El Camino or Laurel. Hotel development is viable but budget for full EIR ($150K+). Use the Hotel Indigo fee structure (Chapter 16, Case 2) as your baseline for impact fee budgeting.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 1, Ch 4, Ch 8 Case 1, Ch 11, Ch 14 Case 2, Ch 16 Case 2, App B

\ornsep

### Belmont

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~27,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 3/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes — HCD confirmed not meeting low/very-low income RHNA |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified October 2024 (later than most Bay Area cities) |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — Moderate-High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 12–20 months (discretionary); 4–6 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | MU (mixed-use), R-3 (multifamily), C-2/C-3 (commercial), Old County Road corridor |
| **RHNA Target** | 1,785 units (2023–2031) — a 268% increase from previous cycle |
| **Land Cost** | $3M–$7M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Belmont's RHNA increased 268% from the previous cycle, creating significant pressure for new housing production. The city's Housing Element was certified later than most Bay Area cities (October 2024), but development is now moving. Critically, **an SB 35 project has already been approved**: The Ridge on Masonic (63 units, 100% affordable) at 951 Old County Road was approved through ministerial review, proving the SB 35 pathway works in Belmont. A 140-unit, five-story apartment building with 25 affordable units was also approved in August 2025. Topography is a significant constraint — many parcels have steep slopes requiring retaining walls and specialized foundations. Development opportunity concentrates along the El Camino Real and Old County Road frontage.

**Developer Playbook**: SB 35 is proven here (The Ridge on Masonic precedent). El Camino Real and Old County Road frontage for mixed-use multifamily. Ralston Ave corridor for smaller infill. Budget for geotechnical on hillside sites — slope stability and retaining walls add significant cost.

\ornsep

### San Mateo (City)

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~105,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified 2023 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission / Staff-level — Low difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 10–18 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-4/R-5 (multifamily), E (El Camino corridor), CBD (central business district), Rail Corridor Plan |
| **RHNA Target** | 7,015 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $3M–$8M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: The City of San Mateo is the largest jurisdiction in San Mateo County and one of the most straightforward to develop in. Staff-level design review for code-compliant projects keeps timelines short. The Rail Corridor Plan area (around the Caltrain station) and El Camino Real corridor provide significant multifamily development opportunities with height allowances up to 55–75 feet. The city has a large and growing pipeline of entitled projects. The Marriott AC Hotel has positioned here (Chapter 9, Chapter 11). Flat terrain on the Bay side eliminates hillside constraints, though Bay-margin geotechnical conditions apply near the Bay.

**Developer Playbook**: Rail Corridor for transit-oriented high-density. El Camino Real for mixed-use. Downtown CBD for infill. Hotel development viable along El Camino. One of the best risk-adjusted development markets on the Peninsula.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 2, Ch 9, Ch 11, App B

\ornsep

### Foster City

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~34,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 3/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Partial — exceeded previous RHNA targets (840 units permitted vs. 430 target) |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified 2023 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 12–20 months (discretionary); 4–6 months (SB 35 if applicable) |
| **Key Zoning** | P-D (planned development — most of city), R-3 (limited multifamily), C (commercial) |
| **RHNA Target** | 1,895 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $3M–$8M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Foster City is a master-planned community built on reclaimed Bay fill in the 1960s, which creates unique development characteristics. Almost the entire city is zoned P-D (planned development), meaning most projects require a site-specific review process. The city exceeded its 5th Cycle RHNA target (840 units permitted vs. 430 target — a 95% surplus), which is exceptional for the Peninsula. However, the 6th Cycle RHNA jumped 341% to 1,896 units, creating new pressure. The city's innovative **GLADUR pre-reviewed plans program** allows streamlined approvals for projects using pre-approved architectural plans. New Single-Family Objective Design and Development Standards are being developed (April–October 2025). Geotechnical conditions are a serious concern — the entire city sits on Bay fill with liquefaction risk and high water tables. Sea level rise adaptation is emerging as a long-term constraint.

**Developer Playbook**: Metro Center area for large-scale redevelopment. GLADUR pre-reviewed plans for streamlined SFR approvals. Budget significantly for geotechnical work — Bay fill conditions throughout. The city's track record of exceeding RHNA suggests institutional willingness to approve housing. Sea level rise adaptation costs should be included in long-term pro forma.

\ornsep

### Millbrae

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~23,000 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 2/5 |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified 2023 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission — Low-Moderate difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 10–18 months (discretionary); 3–5 months (SB 35) |
| **Key Zoning** | MXU (mixed-use), R-3 (multifamily), C-2 (regional commercial), BART station overlay |
| **RHNA Target** | 2,199 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $3M–$7M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Millbrae's BART/Caltrain intermodal station makes it a prime transit-oriented development location. The Downtown & El Camino Real Specific Plan (adopted October 2022) allows high-density mixed-use development up to 85 feet near the station. The city's housing plan exceeds its RHNA mandate by 1,600 units, and 1,247 entitled units await building permits. Key pipeline projects: 959 El Camino Real (278 units, permits issued 2025), 1301 Broadway (97 units), 150 Sarah Ave (488 units, pending HSR litigation), and 1100 El Camino Real (384 units). The city is developing Citywide Objective Design Standards (expected late 2025) that will enable ministerial review for conforming projects. Modular construction is viable for station-area projects given repetitive unit types and good site access.

**Developer Playbook**: BART station area for high-density TOD. El Camino corridor for mixed-use. Once objective design standards are adopted, ministerial track available. Note HSR litigation risk on properties near the planned rail alignment (150 Sarah Ave precedent). One of the best transit-development value propositions on the Peninsula.

\ornsep

### Hillsborough

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~11,500 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 5/5 (Highest) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted; HCD compliance under review |
| **Design Review** | Architecture and Design Review Board — Very High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–36 months (discretionary) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-E (residential estate — minimum 0.5 to 1+ acre lots) |
| **RHNA Target** | 554 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $5M–$15M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Hillsborough is an incorporated town of estates — no commercial districts, no sidewalks in most areas, no apartments. The entire town is zoned for large-lot single-family residential. The Architecture and Design Review Board scrutinizes every project for neighborhood compatibility. The town's RHNA allocation of 554 units represents a profound challenge to its historical character, and the community has resisted housing mandates. SB 9 theoretically allows duplexes, but lot configurations, steep terrain, and septic/sewer limitations constrain even this pathway. Development activity consists almost entirely of custom estate home construction and renovation.

**Developer Playbook**: Luxury custom SFR construction and major renovation only. SB 9 possible on select lots with sewer access. No realistic multifamily pathway. Not recommended for developers seeking scale or predictable returns.

\ornsep

### Atherton

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~7,200 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 5/5 (Highest) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted; HCD compliance contested |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission + Building Committee — Very High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–36 months (discretionary) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-E (residential estate — 1-acre minimum lots) |
| **RHNA Target** | 348 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $10M–$30M/acre (highest in Bay Area) |

**Strategic Assessment**: Atherton consistently ranks among the most expensive zip codes in America, with median home values exceeding $7M. The town is entirely zoned for single-family estates on 1-acre minimum lots. There are no commercial districts, no multifamily zones, and no through-streets designed for anything other than residential traffic. The town has aggressively resisted state housing mandates, initially proposing to meet its RHNA allocation through ADUs on existing estates (a strategy HCD has challenged). SB 9 can theoretically create duplexes, but the 1-acre lots and affluent homeowner opposition create practical barriers.

**Developer Playbook**: Ultra-luxury SFR construction and renovation only ($10M+ price points). ADU construction on existing estates (garage conversions, pool house conversions). No realistic multifamily pathway. Development here is a luxury custom home business, not a development business.

\ornsep

### Woodside

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~5,500 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 5/5 (Highest) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes (10% affordability threshold — insufficient above-moderate RHNA progress) |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle HCD certified January 2025 — after nearly 2 years of non-compliance |
| **Design Review** | 5-member Architectural & Site Review Board (ASRB, meets 1st/3rd Monday) — Very High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–36 months (discretionary) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-E (residential estate — 1-acre+ minimum lots); recently rezoned sites on Raymundo Dr, High Rd, 773 Canada Rd |
| **RHNA Target** | 328 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $5M–$20M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Woodside made national headlines in 2022 when it attempted to declare the entire town a mountain lion habitat to avoid SB 9 compliance — a strategy that was immediately rejected by HCD. The town was a test case for HAA enforcement (Chapter 3, Case 4). Woodside was out of Housing Element compliance for nearly two years (January 2023 – January 2025), requiring an urgency ordinance to amend zoning at the last minute. The 5-member ASRB (which must include a licensed architect and landscape architect) protects the "rural character and natural beauty" of the town. 27 public meetings over three years preceded the Housing Element finalization. The recently rezoned sites on Raymundo Drive, High Road, and 773 Canada Road represent narrow development opportunities, but expect fierce community opposition.

**Developer Playbook**: Custom estate SFR only. SB 423 at 10% affordability on recently rezoned sites may be viable. The urgency-ordinance approach signals the town acts only under state duress. Not recommended for any density-oriented strategy without state-law backing.

**Book Cross-References**: Ch 3 Case 4

\ornsep

### Portola Valley

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~4,300 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 5/5 (Highest) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes (50% affordability threshold due to decertification) |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle adopted Jan 2024, then **DECERTIFIED by HCD March 2024** pending required rezones |
| **Design Review** | Architectural and Site Control Commission (ASCC) — Very High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 18–36 months (discretionary) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-E (residential estate), Open Space; new Mixed-Use and Multi-Family zones being created |
| **RHNA Target** | 253 units (2023–2031) — a 295% increase from previous cycle |
| **Land Cost** | $5M–$15M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Portola Valley's Housing Element was certified by HCD in January 2024 but then **decertified in March 2024** pending completion of required rezones — a rare and damaging reversal. The Dorothy Ford Park controversy (community opposed using a park site for housing, forcing the Town Council to seek alternative sites) exemplifies the political challenges. The town's Opt-in Housing Diversification Program represents an unusual approach to housing production, with new Mixed-Use and Multi-Family zoning classifications being introduced. The San Andreas Fault runs through the town, creating Alquist-Priolo Earthquake Fault Zones. The combination of decertified Housing Element, seismic hazard, steep terrain, limited infrastructure (no sewer in most areas), ASCC review (meets 2nd and 4th Mondays), and organized community opposition makes this among the most challenging jurisdictions in the Bay Area.

**Developer Playbook**: Custom estate SFR on non-fault parcels. SB 9 extremely limited by infrastructure constraints. The decertification creates regulatory uncertainty — verify Housing Element status before committing. Not recommended for developers seeking scale.

\ornsep

### Half Moon Bay

| Attribute | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| **Population** | ~11,300 |
| **Developer Difficulty** | 5/5 (Highest) |
| **SB 35 Eligible** | Yes (50% affordability threshold due to non-certification) |
| **Housing Element** | 6th Cycle NOT CERTIFIED — on 5th draft submission to HCD as of September 2025 |
| **Design Review** | Planning Commission + Coastal Development Permit (CDP) required for ALL development — Very High difficulty |
| **Typical Timeline** | 12–24+ months (longest of all profiled cities) |
| **Key Zoning** | R-3 (multifamily limited), C-1/C-2 (commercial), Open Space, **entire city in Coastal Zone**, Measure D growth cap |
| **RHNA Target** | 480 units (2023–2031) |
| **Land Cost** | $2M–$6M/acre |

**Strategic Assessment**: Half Moon Bay is the most regulatorily complex city profiled here. Three factors create a uniquely challenging environment: (1) The **entire city lies within the California Coastal Zone**, requiring a Coastal Development Permit for all development and Coastal Commission certification of any zoning changes — a three-layer approval process (Planning Commission, City Council, Coastal Commission); (2) **Measure D** (1999, Coastal Commission-certified 2009) imposes a 1–1.5% annual population growth limit by allocating limited new housing certificates, including for ADUs; (3) The city has been on its **5th Housing Element draft** since September 2025, having failed to achieve HCD certification since the January 2023 deadline — the longest non-compliance period of any city profiled here. SB 423 extends streamlining to Coastal Zone cities effective January 1, 2026, but this pathway is untested and the 50% affordability threshold applies. Over 150 vacant infill lots remain in residentially zoned neighborhoods. The Town Center is the designated focus area for development.

**Developer Playbook**: Post-2026 SB 423 in the Coastal Zone is untested — pioneering developers with Coastal Commission experience may find first-mover advantage. Town Center for concentrated infill. Hotel development leveraging oceanfront/agricultural tourism (Ritz-Carlton precedent). Always budget for 12–24 months minimum entitlement. Measure D growth cap is the binding constraint — understand the annual certificate allocation before committing.

\ornsep

### Top 7 Jurisdictions for Multifamily Development (Risk-Adjusted)

1. **Redwood City** — Best combination of Peninsula land values, fast processing, and by-right pathways
2. **Mountain View** — North Bayshore represents the largest single development opportunity in the Bay Area
3. **Milpitas** — Metro Specific Plan (7,109 units), BART access, HOD zones with objective standards
4. **San Jose** — Scale, Urban Village sites, and moderate land costs; watch for geotechnical surprises
5. **Sunnyvale** — Moffett Park transformation + established housing-friendly culture
6. **Campbell** — Award-winning planning process, Winchester Station TOD, objective design standards
7. **San Mateo (City)** — Rail corridor TOD + staff-level design review

### Top 5 Jurisdictions for Hotel Development

1. **Redwood City** — Peninsula location + fast processing + modular hotel viability
2. **Sunnyvale** — El Camino/Mathilda corridor + tech campus demand
3. **San Mateo (City)** — Central Peninsula + transit access + low design review friction
4. **Millbrae** — BART/Caltrain + SFO proximity
5. **Half Moon Bay** — Oceanfront premium positioning (Coastal Act risk)

### Top 5 Jurisdictions for SFR Luxury Development

1. **Cupertino** — Mission Development Group model: consistent luxury SFR production despite high design review (50+ projects completed)
2. **Palo Alto** — Highest price points ($3M–$8M) support teardown-rebuild economics
3. **Los Altos** — Large lots + affluent buyer pool + proximity to tech employers
4. **Los Gatos** — Downtown charm + hillside estate potential + TJH presence
5. **Menlo Park** — West Menlo/Sharon Heights for luxury SFR; east side for SB 9

\ornsep

| Palo Alto | Architectural Review Board (ARB) | Very High | Massing, privacy, compatibility | SB 35 bypasses ARB entirely |
